This study describes isoxazole-based compounds, particularly compound 2j, that bind to the taxane-binding site on tubulin and induce robust anticancer effects through microtubule stabilization. Compound 2j increases tubulin polymerization rates and stabilizes polymerized tubulin in vitro, leading to G2/M cell cycle arrest through upregulation of cyclin B1 and phosphorylation of CDK1, histone H3, and Aurora proteins. Molecular docking reveals that 2j's isoxazole ring forms conserved hydrogen bonds with Ser236 and Arg320 on tubulin, while its C-17 hydroxyl group interacts with Thr276, positioning it within the taxane site similar to paclitaxel. Prolonged mitotic arrest induced by 2j triggers apoptosis via dose-dependent activation of caspases 3, 7, and 9, along with PARP-1 cleavage and histone H2AX phosphorylation. Notably, 2j maintains cytotoxic activity in docetaxel-resistant prostate cancer cells, suggesting potential therapeutic value for overcoming taxane resistance in cancer treatment.

title: Role of progesterone action in inguinal hernia formation via skeletal muscle fibrosis and atrophy
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that progesterone receptor (PGR) signaling plays a critical role in skeletal muscle fibrosis and inguinal hernia formation through estrogen-dependent mechanisms. Estradiol activates estrogen receptor α (ERα) in muscle fibroblasts, which upregulates PGR expression; progesterone then acts through PGR to amplify fibrogenic responses, including increased fibroblast proliferation and extracellular matrix deposition. A distinct population of PGR-expressing fibroblasts orchestrates this pathology by producing TGF-β2, which activates neighboring fibroblasts through Smad2/3 signaling and recruits additional pro-fibrotic pathways including FGF and PDGF signaling. Treatment with PGR-selective antagonists (mifepristone, ulipristal acetate) significantly reduces fibrosis, prevents myofiber atrophy, and blocks hernia development in humanized aromatase mice, offering a novel therapeutic approach for managing this condition. These findings reveal an unexpected pathological role for progesterone signaling in males and identify PGR antagonism as a promising therapeutic strategy.

title: The NLRP3 inflammasome in urogenital cancers: structure, dual function, and therapeutic potential
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome is a multi-protein complex composed of NLRP3, ASC, and caspase-1 that serves as a critical hub in innate immunity and inflammatory disease progression. Upon activation by pathogen-associated molecular patterns or danger signals, the NLRP3 inflammasome triggers caspase-1 activation, which cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature, bioactive forms that drive robust inflammatory responses and pyroptotic cell death. The inflammasome assembly is tightly regulated through multiple mechanisms including toll-like receptor signaling via NF-κB-mediated upregulation of inflammasome components, post-translational modifications such as ubiquitination and phosphorylation, and ion flux events including potassium efflux mediated by TWIK2 channels. Persistent NLRP3 inflammasome activation is associated with chronic inflammation and contributes to cancer initiation and progression, as approximately 25% of carcinogenic factors involve infection and inflammatory pathways, with particular relevance to urogenital malignancies where the inflammasome plays a dual role in tumorigenesis. The inflammasome is terminated through caspase-1 autolysis and selective autophagy of activated complexes, representing key mechanisms to prevent excessive inflammation. These findings underscore the NLRP3 inflammasome's therapeutic potential as a target in urogenital cancers and inflammatory diseases.

title: An engineered antimicrobial peptide Z-FV7 demonstrates bactericidal efficacy against multidrug-resistant Escherichia coli in a murine model of endometritis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Z-FV7, an engineered antimicrobial peptide with a β-hairpin structure, effectively treats multidrug-resistant E. coli-induced endometritis through dual antibacterial and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Z-FV7 directly inhibits bacterial growth and disrupts bacterial membranes while simultaneously binding lipopolysaccharide (LPS) to prevent activation of the TLR4/NF-κB inflammatory pathway, thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) and promoting anti-inflammatory factor production. In a murine endometritis model, Z-FV7 treatment significantly reduced bacterial load, alleviated tissue congestion and edema, restored tight junction proteins (Claudin-1 and ZO-1), and decreased neutrophil infiltration and cellular damage. Notably, Z-FV7 exhibited superior antimicrobial potency compared to its parent peptide Z-d14CFR with a lower minimum inhibitory concentration (32 µg/mL) and demonstrated safety in mammalian cells without causing hemolysis or cytotoxicity. These findings suggest Z-FV7 represents a promising therapeutic candidate for treating antibiotic-resistant endometritis while preserving tissue integrity and reducing excessive inflammation.

title: Role of AMP-Activated Protein Kinase (AMPK) in Female Reproduction: A Review
summary:
# Summary

AMPK is a central metabolic regulator widely expressed in reproductive tissues including the ovaries and uterus, where it governs cellular energy homeostasis by activating catabolic pathways while inhibiting anabolic processes. The AMPK signaling pathway is essential for maintaining reproductive homeostasis and directly regulates multiple aspects of female reproduction, including folliculogenesis, oocyte maturation, ovulation, implantation, decidualization, and steroidogenesis. Dysregulation of AMPK signaling is implicated in multiple female reproductive disorders, including polycystic ovary syndrome, endometriosis, premature ovarian failure, infertility, and reproductive aging. AMPK activation exerts notable anti-inflammatory effects and modulates the mTOR signaling pathway, demonstrating dual roles in both metabolic control and immune regulation that are critical for reproductive function. Notably, AMPK activation suppresses gonadotropin-releasing hormone and luteinizing hormone release during energy deficit, while AMPK inhibition paradoxically stimulates follicle development, ovulation, and oocyte fertilization through the AMPK-mTOR/Hippo pathway. The dysregulation of this energy-sensing pathway therefore represents a key mechanism underlying endometriosis and other reproductive disorders, positioning AMPK as a therapeutic target for modulating both metabolic and inflammatory aspects of female reproductive health.

title: Do NGF and LPS Interact Synergistically to Modulate Inflammation in Sheep Endometrial Epithelial Cells?
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how nerve growth factor (NGF) and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) interact to modulate inflammatory responses in sheep endometrial epithelial cells. NGF alone potently upregulates its own receptors (NTRK1 and p75NTR) alongside inflammatory mediators COX2 and the steroidogenic enzyme STAR, while simultaneously activating NF-κB and ERK1/2 signaling pathways. LPS activates a distinct but overlapping response primarily through TLR4, increasing COX2 and IGFBP6 expression. Notably, combined NGF and LPS treatment produces synergistic upregulation of NGF, its receptors, TLR4, and COX2, with markedly elevated prostaglandins (PGE2 and PGF2α) in epithelial cells. NTRK1 inhibition completely reverses NGF-driven gene expression and prostaglandin production, confirming that NTRK1 signaling is essential for these inflammatory effects. These findings establish NGF as a potent neuroimmune mediator in endometrial inflammation and suggest that NGF-TLR4 crosstalk may amplify endometrial immune dysfunction relevant to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Innovative Peptide Therapeutics in the Pipeline: Transforming Cancer Detection and Treatment
summary: This review examines innovative peptide-based therapeutics designed to improve cancer detection and treatment through targeted mechanisms. The article highlights how peptides achieve superior selectivity by binding to overexpressed receptors on cancer cells, thereby reducing the systemic toxicity associated with conventional chemotherapy. Key therapeutic examples include GnRH antagonists like degarelix, which suppress luteinizing and follicle-stimulating hormones to induce androgen deprivation in prostate cancer; somatostatin analogs such as octreotide and lanreotide, which target neuroendocrine tumors through somatostatin receptor 2 (SSTR2) engagement; and PSMA-targeted agents including the radiopharmaceutical Pluvicto and the diagnostic tracer 18F-PSMA-1007 for prostate cancer. Additionally, theranostic systems and tumor-specific antibody-peptide conjugates like BT5528 and SAR408701 represent emerging strategies that integrate imaging and therapeutic functions. The convergence of these targeted peptide approaches with personalized medicine strategies, exemplified by compounds such as CLP002, demonstrates a paradigm shift toward precision cancer therapies that maximize therapeutic efficacy while minimizing collateral damage to healthy tissues.

title: A Beautiful Bind: Phage Display and the Search for Cell-Selective Peptides
summary:
# Summary

Phage display technology has emerged as a powerful platform for discovering cell-selective peptides that bind to cell surface proteins, enabling applications in targeted drug delivery and molecular imaging. The article explores how structural modifications enhance peptide performance, demonstrating that disulfide bonds and bicyclic peptide architectures substantially improve binding affinity, selectivity, and resistance to proteolytic degradation, though intracellular reducing environments can compromise disulfide-stabilized peptides. Chemical strategies employing linkers such as azobenzene and ortho-phthalaldehyde have enabled the generation of light-responsive and conformationally constrained cyclic peptides with enhanced therapeutic potential. Recent advances in next-generation sequencing and machine learning have transformed phage display screening by enabling high-throughput analysis of peptide composition, consensus sequence determination, and computational discrimination between specific binders and selection noise. Negative selection strategies further refine peptide candidates by reducing off-target binding, while peptide length and conformation critically influence phage propagation capacity. These integrated advances in peptide engineering and selection methodologies position phage display as an increasingly sophisticated tool for identifying bioactive peptides suitable for clinical applications analogous to successful therapeutics like ecallantide and pegcetacoplan.

title: Genetic Variants Linked with the Concentration of Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin Correlate with Uterine Fibroid Risk
summary:
# Summary

This study identified sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) concentration-related genetic variants associated with uterine fibroid (UF) risk, revealing a hormone-mediated mechanism linking genetic predisposition to fibroid development. Two key single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) demonstrated significant associations: rs3779195 in BAIAP2L1 showed protective effects against UF (odds ratio 0.38), while rs440837 in ZBTB10 increased UF risk (odds ratio 1.93). These variants regulate SHBG levels, which control the bioavailability of sex hormones including testosterone and estrogens; elevated estrogens stimulate myomatous growth through estrogen receptor interactions and progesterone receptor upregulation. Seven SHBG-related SNPs exhibited significant epistatic interactions influencing UF risk, with three loci (NR2F2, GCKR, and PPP1R21) showing the strongest combinatorial effects. The identified variants operate through multiple pathways including gene transcription regulation, estrogen-dependent gene expression, steroid hormone processes, and TGF-beta signaling, with tissue expression in reproductive organs, liver, and metabolic tissues directly relevant to UF pathophysiology. These findings establish genetic variants affecting SHBG as important determinants of UF susceptibility through hormone bioavailability regulation.

title: Nuclear receptors in health and disease: signaling pathways, biological functions and pharmaceutical interventions
summary: Nuclear receptors are a large family of ligand-activated transcription factors that regulate gene expression and control critical physiological processes including reproduction, development, metabolism, and homeostasis. These receptors bind diverse endogenous ligands such as steroid hormones, thyroid hormone, vitamin D, and retinoic acid, which activate their DNA-binding domains to modulate transcription of target genes. Nuclear receptor signaling influences cell proliferation, differentiation, and survival, making dysregulation of these pathways central to the pathogenesis of cancer, metabolic disorders, cardiovascular disease, and reproductive dysfunction including infertility and obesity. Pharmaceutical interventions targeting nuclear receptors have proven clinically effective, with examples including tamoxifen and raloxifene for estrogen receptor-mediated breast cancer and osteoporosis treatment, enzalutamide for androgen receptor-driven prostate cancer, and thiazolidinediones targeting peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-gamma for type 2 diabetes management. Cross-talk between nuclear receptor pathways exemplifies their integrated regulation, as illustrated by glucocorticoid receptor-mediated upregulation of PPARα expression and progesterone receptor-mediated suppression of estrogen receptor activity. Understanding nuclear receptor signaling mechanisms and their dysregulation provides a foundation for developing improved therapeutic strategies across multiple disease states.

title: Glucose absorption in the duodenum is modulated by an estrogen receptor α-dependent regulation of glucose transporter functional expression
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that estrogen regulates glucose absorption in the duodenum through an estrogen receptor α (ERα)-dependent mechanism. Estrogen promotes glucose uptake by suppressing protein kinase C (PKC) signaling, which in turn allows increased expression of the glucose transporters SGLT1 and GLUT2 on intestinal epithelial cells. In ovariectomized mice with depleted estrogen levels, duodenal glucose transporter expression and glucose absorption capacity were significantly reduced, accompanied by impaired glucose tolerance, weight gain, and increased abdominal adiposity. Mechanistically, estrogen-mediated inhibition of PKC phosphorylation requires functional ERα, as silencing ERα blocked both the PKC inhibition and the upregulation of glucose transporters. These findings reveal a previously uncharacterized role for estrogen in maintaining normal glucose homeostasis via direct regulation of intestinal glucose transporter function, with implications for understanding metabolic dysfunction in estrogen-deficient states.

title: Candesartan Mitigates Perioperative Neurocognitive Disorders by Modulating Hypertension-Linked Neuroinflammatory Factor
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that candesartan, an angiotensin II receptor blocker, mitigates perioperative neurocognitive disorders (PND) by targeting hypertension-linked neuroinflammation. Hypertension causally increases cognitive dysfunction and promotes neuroinflammatory responses through angiotensin II activation of AT1R, which triggers microglial activation and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α). In a PND mouse model, candesartan improved cognitive function by downregulating the neuroinflammatory biomarkers Bdkrb1 and Ccr1 while upregulating the neuroprotective factor Thbs1 in the hippocampus. These molecular changes correlated with reduced microglial activation and suppressed pro-inflammatory cytokine levels. The findings identify Bdkrb1, Ccr1, and Thbs1 as hypertension-associated PND biomarkers and establish candesartan's therapeutic potential for preventing postoperative cognitive decline through renin-angiotensin system inhibition and neuroinflammation suppression.

title: Dual Roles of Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) in Bone Remodeling and Pain Management: Bridging the Gap in Osteoarthritis Research
summary:
# Summary

Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) exhibits dual opposing roles in osteoarthritis: it drives pain sensitization while also regulating bone metabolism and repair. PGE2 is produced by multiple cell types including osteoblasts, chondrocytes, and macrophages downstream of COX2 activation, which is induced by pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-6. In pain pathways, PGE2 activates peripheral and central sensitization through EP4 and TRPV1 receptors, increasing nociceptor trafficking of Nav 1.8 sodium channels and promoting pain peptide release, with blockade by COX2 inhibitors, EP4 antagonists, or TRPV1 antagonists effectively reversing mechanical hypersensitivity. Conversely, PGE2 signaling through EP2 and EP4 receptors promotes bone and cartilage repair by enhancing proteoglycan accumulation, type II collagen regeneration, and osteogenic differentiation while suppressing catabolic matrix metalloproteinases. These findings suggest that selective receptor targeting—particularly EP2 activation for tissue protection or EP4 antagonism for pain relief—may offer more nuanced therapeutic strategies than broad COX2 inhibition in osteoarthritis management.

title: Mechanisms of programmed cell death in livestock follicular development and atresia: a review
journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how programmed cell death pathways—autophagy, apoptosis, and ferroptosis—regulate follicular development and atresia in livestock. Autophagy protects granulosa cells through clearance of damaged organelles and can inhibit apoptosis by removing dysfunctional mitochondria, while ferroptosis operates through iron-dependent lipid peroxidation involving reactive oxygen species generation. FSH and growth factors like IGF-1 activate survival pathways through PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling to promote follicular growth, whereas declining FSH levels and factors like AMH suppress these signals to initiate atresia in subordinate follicles. These three cell death mechanisms interact dynamically during follicular progression—with autophagy predominating early to maintain homeostasis and apoptosis becoming more prominent during selection stages—collectively determining which follicles survive to ovulation and which undergo degeneration. The coordinated regulation of these pathways by hormonal signals, growth factors from the oocyte-granulosa-theca cell complex, and intracellular signaling cascades ensures proper follicle selection and quality control throughout reproductive cycles.

title: Revisiting the Link Between Factor XII and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: A Scoping Review
summary:
# Summary

Factor XII (FXII), a liver-derived serine protease involved in coagulation, fibrinolysis, inflammation, and complement activation, has been consistently associated with recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL) through multiple mechanisms. FXII deficiency leads to placental dysfunction and hypercoagulability by impairing the contact system pathway, which normally regulates maternal-fetal blood flow and facilitates trophoblast invasion during early pregnancy. Low FXII levels and anti-FXII antibodies both suppress fibrinolytic activity and enhance platelet aggregation, collectively creating a hypercoagulable state that compromises placental perfusion. Clinical evidence demonstrates that reduced FXII activity correlates significantly with both primary and secondary RPL, with the strongest associations observed in patients experiencing three or more pregnancy losses, and anti-FXII antibodies appear to directly inhibit FXII's physiological function. While the C46T polymorphism reduces FXII translation efficiency, population-level associations with RPL remain inconsistent across studies. The precise mechanisms linking FXII deficiency to pregnancy loss—whether through microthrombus formation, impaired trophoblast invasion, or placental vascular dysfunction—warrant further investigation, though the combined impact on hemostasis and fibrinolysis represents a credible pathophysiological pathway in RPL etiology.

title: Challenges with measuring tissue factor antigen and activity in human plasma
summary:
# Summary

Tissue factor (TF) is a transmembrane glycoprotein that initiates blood coagulation by binding and activating factor VII (FVII/FVIIa), which subsequently activates factors X and IX through formation of the TF-FVIIa complex. This procoagulant activity is tightly regulated by tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI), which binds the TF-FVIIa-FXa complex and prevents further thrombin generation. TF is highly expressed on cell surfaces including adventitial fibroblasts, pericytes, and keratinocytes, and is also released on extracellular vesicles (EVs) from circulating monocytes and cancer cells, with elevated TF-positive EV levels associated with thrombotic diseases including cancer, sepsis, and viral infections. The measurement of plasma TF antigen presents significant technical challenges, as commonly used ELISA assays (IMUBIND and Quantikine) produce highly variable and discordant results, while activity-based assays demonstrate superior sensitivity and specificity for detecting functional TF. Clinical studies have identified elevated TF antigen in leukemia, coronary artery disease, breast cancer, lung cancer, and COVID-19, though standardized measurement methods are needed to improve the reliability and comparability of TF quantification across different assay platforms.

title: Using Reporter Gene Assays to Screen and Identify Chemical Compounds that Modulate Estrogen Receptor Activity
summary: Estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) is highly expressed in reproductive tissues including the breast, uterus, and ovary, as well as metabolically important tissues such as bone, adipose tissue, and liver, where it regulates reproduction, mammary gland development, and glucose homeostasis. Dysregulation of ERα activity—whether through uncontrolled activation or inhibition—contributes to pathological conditions including endometriosis and breast cancer, highlighting the clinical importance of precise ERα modulation. This protocol describes reporter gene assay systems using engineered cell lines (MCF7-VM7Luc4E2 and HEK293 ERα-bla cells) that stably express either luciferase or β-lactamase reporter genes under ERα transcriptional control, enabling high-throughput screening of chemical compounds. The assays validate known ERα modulators including agonists like β-estradiol and bisphenol A that activate ERα, and antagonists like 4-hydroxytamoxifen that inhibit receptor activity across physiologically relevant concentration ranges. These reporter-based screening methods provide quantitative, sensitive approaches to identify and characterize novel chemical compounds that modulate ERα function, supporting drug development for endometriosis and other estrogen-dependent disorders.

title: Periodontal Implications of Porphyromonas gingivalis-Derived Metabolites in Host Antioxidant and Anti-Inflammatory Mechanisms: A Computational Analysis
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary: Porphyromonas gingivalis, a major periodontal pathogen, produces multiple virulence factors including lipopolysaccharides, proteases, and metabolites that disrupt host antioxidant defenses and trigger chronic inflammation, contributing not only to periodontitis but also to systemic diseases such as atherosclerosis, diabetes, and neurodegeneration. The enzyme paraoxonase 1 (PON1), a critical antioxidant highly expressed in blood plasma, serum, oral epithelium, and immune cells, plays a protective role through detoxification of reactive oxygen species and lipid peroxides while exerting anti-inflammatory and anti-atherosclerotic effects. P. gingivalis-derived metabolites, particularly a compound with molecular formula C38H70O10P+, directly inhibit PON1 function by binding to its active site and inducing conformational changes, thereby impairing the enzyme's capacity to protect against oxidative stress. This metabolite-mediated suppression of PON1-dependent host defense mechanisms represents a key pathogenic strategy through which P. gingivalis contributes to both local periodontal destruction and systemic disease pathogenesis. Network analysis reveals that PON1 functions within interconnected pathways involving lipid metabolism, immune regulation, and oxidative stress mitigation, interacting with related paraoxonases, apolipoprotein A1, and antioxidant enzymes like superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase. Understanding the specific interactions between bacterial metabolites and host antioxidant systems provides therapeutic targets for preventing periodontal disease progression and its systemic complications.

title: Mobiluncus mulieris alters the transcriptomic profile of cervicovaginal epithelial cells, shedding light on molecular drivers of adverse reproductive outcomes
summary: Mobiluncus mulieris, a gram-positive anaerobe associated with bacterial vaginosis and adverse reproductive outcomes including preterm birth and endometriosis, triggers robust inflammatory responses in cervicovaginal epithelial cells primarily through toll-like receptor and nucleotide-binding oligomerization domain-like receptor signaling pathways. The bacterium and its extracellular vesicles (bEVs) activate innate immune responses via TLR2 and TLR5 signaling—notably, M. mulieris is unique in expressing flagellin to activate TLR5—and significantly upregulate NLRP3 inflammasome components including NLRP3, BIRC3, and IRAK2. Bacterial extracellular vesicles elicit the strongest transcriptomic response, upregulating multiple matrix metalloproteinases (MMP1, MMP9, MMP10, MMP13, MMP19), which are linked to preterm birth and epithelial barrier disruption, along with collagen remodeling genes and inflammatory mediators including CXCL8/IL-8, CXCL10, SAA1, and TNF. The exposure also triggers antimicrobial peptide responses and interferon-mediated immunity through upregulation of DDX58/IFIH1 pathway components, collectively promoting extracellular matrix remodeling, compromised epithelial integrity, and sustained inflammatory signaling that likely contributes to reproductive pathology.

title: IGF-1R promotes pain-like behavior in rats with myofascial pain syndrome via the PI3K/AKT pathway
summary: This study demonstrates that insulin-like growth factor-1 receptor (IGF-1R) promotes pain-like behavior in myofascial trigger points through activation of the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway. IGF-1R and its ligand IGF-1 are significantly elevated in muscle tissue of rats with myofascial trigger points, and IGF-1R activation induces hyperalgesia by stimulating the PI3K/AKT cascade, which subsequently upregulates downstream effectors including mTOR and RhoA. The PI3K/AKT pathway activation leads to neuronal hyperexcitability through TRPV2-mediated calcium influx and promotes muscle contracture via RhoA-dependent skeletal muscle contraction signaling. Pharmacological inhibition of IGF-1R using picropodophyllin or PI3K inhibition using LY294002 significantly reduced pain-like behaviors and suppressed expression of pathway components, demonstrating the causal role of this axis in myofascial pain pathogenesis. These findings identify the IGF-1R/PI3K/AKT signaling axis as a critical contributor to myofascial trigger point pain and suggest potential therapeutic targets for treatment of this chronic pain condition.

title: Nerve growth factor: what can surgeons and oncologists learn from a neurological and psychological biomarker?
summary: Nerve growth factor (NGF) is a multifunctional neurotrophin that plays dual roles in both physiological and pathological processes, particularly through its interaction with the TrkA receptor. As the founding member of the neurotrophin family, NGF is essential for peripheral sensory and sympathetic neuronal development, survival, and nerve fiber healing and outgrowth. Beyond its classical neuronal functions, NGF acts as a significant cytokine regulating stromal cell activation and epithelial cell proliferation and differentiation. Abnormally elevated NGF expression is implicated in diverse pathological conditions including neurological and psychological disorders, as well as cancer progression, where it promotes perineural invasion—a marker of poor prognosis in pancreatic, colorectal, and gallbladder cancers. The balance between NGF signaling through TrkA (which promotes proliferation and nerve outgrowth) and the precursor form ProNGF signaling through p75NTR (which promotes apoptosis) is critical to cellular outcomes, with elevated TrkA expression specifically correlating with abdominal pain and perineural invasion. Targeting this pathway through NTRK inhibitors such as entrectinib and larotrectinib offers therapeutic potential for treating cancers with abnormal NGF pathway activation.

title: Targeting oncofetal fibronectin and neuropilin-1 in solid tumors with PL2 peptide
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies and characterizes the PL2 peptide as a dual-targeting agent for solid tumors that simultaneously binds fibronectin extra domain-B (Fn-EDB) and neuropilin-1 (NRP-1), two markers overexpressed in cancer vasculature and tumor tissue. The PL2 peptide's C-terminal arginine residue is critical for both Fn-EDB binding and NRP-1 engagement, with NRP-1 interaction activating the C-end rule pathway to promote cellular entry and tissue penetration. When conjugated to iron oxide nanoworms, PL2-functionalized nanoparticles demonstrated enhanced accumulation and extravasation in multiple solid tumor models including glioblastoma, prostate carcinoma, and ovarian carcinoma, with approximately 7-9 fold greater penetration into tumor parenchyma compared to control nanoparticles. These findings establish PL2 as a promising targeting moiety for delivering diagnostic and therapeutic agents to solid tumors, particularly relevant for ovarian cancer where both Fn-EDB and NRP-1 are significantly elevated.

title: The critical roles of bioactive sphingolipids in inflammation
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the critical regulatory roles of bioactive sphingolipids, particularly sphingosine, ceramides, and sphingosine 1-phosphate (S1P), in controlling cellular inflammation and immune responses. Sphingosine, produced from catabolism of complex sphingolipids, inhibits key signaling enzymes including protein kinase C and phosphatidate phosphohydrolase, while regulating diverse cellular processes such as proliferation, survival, cell death, autophagy, and cytoskeletal dynamics. Ceramides act as broad regulators of cell death, survival, differentiation, autophagy, and inflammation, with their pro-inflammatory effects counteracted by S1P and ceramide 1-phosphate, establishing an important metabolic balance in inflammatory signaling. S1P exerts its effects through both receptor-dependent mechanisms via five distinct S1P receptors and receptor-independent intracellular actions, including activation of ERK1/2 and phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase signaling cascades that promote proliferation, survival, and migration. Additionally, sphingosine demonstrates potent antimicrobial and antiviral properties against multiple pathogens, making it therapeutically relevant for infections including SARS-CoV-2 and bacterial species, with reduced sphingosine levels in cystic fibrosis lungs linked to increased infection susceptibility. The compartmentalized localization of sphingosine kinases—SphK1 in the cytoplasm regulating autophagy and ubiquitination, and SphK2 in the nucleus controlling gene transcription—underscores the sophisticated spatial regulation of sphingolipid signaling in cellular homeostasis and inflammation control.

title: A novel miR-4536-3p inhibition ameliorates Alzheimer’s disease by reducing Aβ accumulation and tau phosphorylation
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies miR-4536-3p as a therapeutic target for Alzheimer's disease, demonstrating that inhibiting this microRNA upregulates DBN1, a synaptic plasticity regulator that is reduced in AD pathology. miR-4536-3p inhibition activates the PI3K/Akt/GSK3β signaling pathway, leading to decreased amyloid-beta accumulation, reduced tau phosphorylation, and suppressed neuronal apoptosis in 5xFAD mice. The mechanism involves miR-4536-3p directly binding to DBN1's 3′-UTR; restoration of DBN1 expression protects against neuropathological lesions and improves spatial learning and memory. These findings establish miR-4536-3p inhibition as a potential therapeutic strategy that addresses multiple hallmarks of Alzheimer's disease—amyloid pathology, tau dysfunction, and neuronal loss—through a single molecular intervention.

title: Essential Pieces of the Puzzle: The Roles of VEGF and Dopamine in Aging
summary: This review examines the interconnected roles of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and dopamine in aging and age-related diseases. VEGF promotes angiogenesis and tissue repair by binding to endothelial receptors VEGFR-1 and VEGFR-2, supporting wound healing, hypoxia adaptation, and organ development, while dopamine regulates vascular tone and peripheral blood flow to maintain vascular homeostasis. However, aging drives progressive decline through multiple mechanisms including genomic instability, telomere shortening, and proteostasis loss, which collectively drive cellular senescence and chronic inflammation that impair tissue regeneration. Notably, VEGF signaling and dopamine synthesis both decline with age, resulting in reduced angiogenesis, diminished capillary density, and impaired tissue perfusion, while declining dopamine contributes to cognitive slowing and increased vulnerability to neurodegenerative diseases. The review highlights a complex regulatory relationship where dopamine can inhibit VEGF-driven angiogenesis through D2-like receptor signaling, yet VEGF simultaneously promotes dopaminergic neuron survival through mitochondrial support and oxidative stress reduction. In age-related diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease, therapeutic restoration of VEGF-induced angiogenesis and dopaminergic signaling shows promise for enhancing cerebral perfusion, improving synaptic function, and ameliorating cognitive decline.

title: Molecular Mechanisms of the Endocannabinoid System with a Focus on Reproductive Physiology and the Cannabinoid Impact on Fertility
summary:
# Summary

The endocannabinoid system (ECS) is a neuromodulatory network essential for maintaining physiological homeostasis and plays crucial roles in reproductive physiology, including regulation of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, gametogenesis, implantation, and lactation. Cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2, activated by endogenous ligands and exogenous cannabinoids, are widely distributed across the central nervous system, immune tissues, and reproductive organs including the ovary, uterus, testis, and placenta. Upon activation, these receptors initiate intracellular signaling cascades through MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways that regulate critical cellular processes such as proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, and migration, with specific roles in folliculogenesis, spermatogenesis, and implantation. The endocannabinoids anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol regulate follicular development, sperm capacitation, embryo implantation, and uterine contractility. Emerging evidence indicates that exogenous cannabinoids possess therapeutic potential, including anti-inflammatory, pro-apoptotic, analgesic, and immunosuppressive properties, though comprehensive research is needed to define their clinical applications and associated risks in reproductive health.

title: IL2RG knockout mitigates polycystic ovary syndrome pathogenesis by transitioning pyroptosis to apoptosis through the GSDME pathway
summary:
# Summary

Elevated IL2RG expression in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) promotes a pathogenic shift from apoptosis to pyroptosis in ovarian granulosa cells, a process driven by caspase-3-mediated cleavage of gasdermin E (GSDME). IL2RG acts as a shared receptor for multiple cytokines (IL-2, IL-4, IL-7, IL-9, IL-15, and IL-21) that are significantly elevated in PCOS patients and contributes to disease pathogenesis by disrupting the estrous cycle and elevating androgens. IL2RG knockout or knockdown inhibits caspase-3-mediated GSDME cleavage and reverses pyroptosis back toward apoptosis, simultaneously restoring normal ovarian index, testosterone, glucose, and LH/FSH ratios in a DHEA-induced PCOS model. The IL2RG/JAK/STAT5 signaling axis regulates GSDME expression and pyroptotic cell death in testosterone-stimulated ovarian cells, providing a mechanistic link between IL2RG activation and gasdermin-dependent pyroptosis. These findings identify IL2RG as a critical therapeutic target for mitigating PCOS pathogenesis through modulation of the pyroptosis-to-apoptosis balance via the GSDME pathway.

title: Immuno-metabolic diseases and therapeutics: molecular mechanisms via inflammasome signaling
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway and its central role in immuno-metabolic diseases including obesity, diabetes, atherosclerosis, and sarcopenia. The NLRP3 inflammasome functions through a two-stage activation process: a priming stage where toll-like receptor signaling via MyD88 and TRIF activates NF-κB to upregulate pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18, followed by an activation stage where pathogen-associated and danger-associated molecular patterns trigger NLRP3 to assemble caspase-1, which then cleaves these pro-cytokines into their mature, secreted forms and activates gasdermin D to facilitate further cytokine release. Aberrant inflammasome activation contributes to insulin resistance, lipid dysregulation, muscle wasting, and vascular injury underlying metabolic diseases. The review highlights emerging therapeutic strategies including adiponectin and the myokine METRNL, which suppress NLRP3 inflammasome activation and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokine production, while exercise and cold exposure upregulate METRNL to improve glucose tolerance and induce thermogenic gene expression. These findings suggest that targeting inflammasome signaling represents a promising therapeutic approach for treating immuno-metabolic diseases.

title: Potentiation of visualized exosomal miR-1306-3p from primary sensory neurons contributes to chronic visceral pain via spinal P2X3 receptors
summary:
# Summary

Neonatal maternal deprivation induces chronic visceral pain through a pathway involving primary sensory neuron-derived exosomes and spinal P2X3 receptors. Rab27a upregulation in dorsal root ganglia mediates increased release of exosomes containing miR-1306-3p into the spinal dorsal horn, where these exosomes accumulate and activate P2X3 receptors on spinal neurons. This P2X3R activation enhances spinal synaptic transmission, ultimately contributing to chronic visceral pain. Blocking this pathway at multiple levels—via Rab27a knockdown, exosome release inhibition with GW4869, miR-1306-3p inhibition, or P2X3R antagonism with Gefapixant—effectively alleviates visceral pain without affecting motor function or baseline pain thresholds in control animals. These findings establish exosomal miR-1306-3p as a novel signaling molecule linking primary sensory neuron activity to spinal pain processing, offering potential therapeutic targets for chronic visceral pain conditions like irritable bowel syndrome.

title: Advances in the study of the relationship between Porphyromonas gingivalis and various diseases
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: Porphyromonas gingivalis is a gram-negative anaerobic bacterium strongly associated with periodontal disease and multiple systemic conditions including oral, esophageal, colorectal, and pancreatic cancers, as well as Alzheimer's disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The pathogen exerts its effects through multiple virulence mechanisms including lipopolysaccharide (LPS), gingipains, outer membrane vesicles, and long fimbriae, which activate innate immune signaling pathways including TLR2/TLR4, NF-κB, and MAPK cascades leading to pro-inflammatory cytokine production (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α). P. gingivalis LPS binds TLR4 to activate MyD88 and NF-κB signaling, while long fimbriae bind TLR2 to similarly activate NF-κB and inflammatory responses. Additionally, the bacterium produces small RNAs packaged in outer membrane vesicles that inhibit CBX5 and reduce H3K9me3 levels on p53, resulting in increased p53 expression and host cell apoptosis, while also inducing epithelial-mesenchymal transition to promote oral cancer progression. The multifactorial pathogenic mechanisms of P. gingivalis underscore its role as a key driver of both oral and systemic inflammatory and neoplastic diseases.

title: Corneal enzymatic digestion resistance in the presence of oestradiol and oestradiol plus selective tissue oestrogenic activity regulators (STEAR)
summary:
# Summary

Oestradiol reduces corneal biomechanical stability and enzymatic digestion resistance through multiple mechanisms involving collagen degradation. Oestradiol upregulates matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) and enhances prostaglandin release, both of which activate collagenolytic enzymes that cleave corneal type I collagen and disrupt corneal integrity. These effects occur because oestrogen receptors are present throughout the cornea and regulate collagen biosynthesis. When the selective tissue oestrogen activity regulator tibolone is combined with oestradiol, corneal resistance to enzymatic digestion is further reduced, with tibolone-pretreated corneas showing significantly lower resistance than oestradiol treatment alone. Given tibolone's clinical use in treating endometriosis and menopausal symptoms, these findings suggest that STEAR therapy may increase risk for keratoconus progression and corneal complications associated with elevated collagenase activity, particularly in conditions like infectious keratitis. This study highlights an important adverse effect consideration for patients receiving hormone replacement therapy or endometriosis treatments that modulate oestrogen signaling.

title: Identification of Druggable Targets for Alzheimer's Disease by Analyzing Circulating Inflammatory Proteins With Mendelian Randomization
summary:
# Summary

Using Mendelian randomization, this study identified circulating inflammatory proteins as druggable targets for Alzheimer's disease by analyzing their causal relationships with disease risk. Five inflammatory proteins—TSLP, S100A12, CD244, CCL4, and MMP1—were identified as both associated with elevated AD risk and potentially druggable. TSLP interacts with vascular adhesion molecules to promote neuroinflammation, while S100A12 drives inflammation and amyloid-beta protein aggregation; conversely, NRTN and CCL4 exhibit protective effects against AD progression. The study highlights multiple therapeutic strategies, including cyclosporin to inhibit the calcineurin/NFAT pathway and reduce amyloid neurotoxicity, and leuprolide to inhibit MMP1, a biomarker elevated in AD patients that correlates with oxidative stress and cognitive decline. These findings provide a systematic approach to targeting neuroinflammation as a key mechanism in AD pathogenesis, offering several candidate proteins for drug development aimed at reducing disease incidence and improving cognitive outcomes.

title: A Systematic Review of Clinical and Experimental Periodontitis Studies Demonstrating the Expression of PPAR-Gamma: A Meta-Analysis and Bioinformatics Approach
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examined PPAR-γ expression in periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease of periodontal tissues. The meta-analysis revealed significantly lower PPAR-γ protein levels in periodontal tissues from periodontitis patients and animals compared to healthy controls, while PPARG mRNA expression was consistently higher in healthy gingival tissues across both human and animal studies. PPAR-γ plays a critical anti-inflammatory role by inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production and attenuating periodontal tissue destruction through its function as a ligand-activated transcription factor that forms a heterodimer with RXR to regulate gene expression. Periodontitis is associated with excessive, poorly controlled inflammatory responses mediated primarily by immune cells—particularly TH17-derived IL-17, which induces RANKL expression leading to osteoclast activation and bone resorption. The dysregulation of PPAR-γ contributes not only to periodontal pathology but also to systemic diseases including type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, and osteoporosis, suggesting PPAR-γ as a potential therapeutic target for managing both periodontal inflammation and its comorbidities.

title: Lipoxins as Modulators of Diseases
summary:
# Summary: Lipoxins as Modulators of Diseases

Lipoxins are specialized pro-resolving mediators derived from arachidonic acid through lipoxygenase-dependent pathways that play critical roles in inflammation resolution across multiple disease contexts including systemic inflammation, infection, asthma, cancer, diabetes, and cardiovascular disorders. These eicosanoid molecules are produced through distinct biosynthetic pathways involving transcellular cooperation between cell types—particularly platelets and neutrophils, or neutrophils and epithelial cells—as well as through aspirin-triggered pathways in which acetylated cyclooxygenase-2 generates more metabolically stable 15-epi-lipoxins. Lipoxins exert their anti-inflammatory and pro-resolving effects primarily by binding to the G-protein coupled ALX/FPR2 receptor, which is widely distributed across epithelial cells, neurons, immune cells, and other tissues, and can also activate alternative receptors including GPR32 and the aryl hydrocarbon receptor. Mechanistically, lipoxins inhibit pro-inflammatory mediator release, facilitate non-phlogistic phagocytosis of apoptotic neutrophils by macrophages, activate pro-survival PI3K/Akt and ERK/Nrf-2 signaling pathways, and competitively antagonize pro-inflammatory leukotriene signaling. These findings position lipoxins and their synthetic analogs as promising therapeutic targets for inflammatory and degenerative diseases where dysregulated inflammation contributes to pathology.

title: The Synergistic Effect of Heat Therapy and Electroacupuncture Treatment in Inflammatory Pain Mouse Models
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that electroacupuncture (EA) and heat therapy (HT) synergistically reduce inflammatory pain in mouse models through complementary mechanisms. Both treatments individually suppressed acetic acid-induced writhing and mechanical allodynia, with combined EA+HT achieving the greatest analgesic effect by reducing writhing responses by approximately 65% compared to untreated pain models. The analgesic effects operate through distinct but overlapping pathways: EA primarily activates adenosine A1 receptors and opioid receptors to suppress nociceptive signaling, while HT activates TRPV1/TRPV2 channels to trigger heat shock protein induction and vasodilation with anti-inflammatory consequences. Both treatments converge on reducing neuroinflammation by suppressing phosphorylated p38 (p-p38) and glial fibrillary acidic protein (GFAP) expression in the spinal dorsal horn, thereby limiting astrocyte activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine release that drive central sensitization. The critical role of adenosine A1 receptor signaling in both modalities was confirmed by pharmacological antagonists (caffeine and DPCPX), which fully reversed analgesia, while naloxone only partially blocked EA's effects, establishing adenosine signaling as the dominant shared mechanism underlying this therapeutic synergy.

title: Effects of Asprosin and Role of TLR4 as a Biomarker in Endometrial Cancer
summary: This study investigates asprosin and TLR4 as potential biomarkers in endometrial cancer, revealing complex interactions between metabolic and inflammatory pathways. Asprosin, an adipokine regulating glucose homeostasis and appetite, significantly alters gene expression in endometrial cancer cell lines (Ishikawa and NOU-1) through multiple mechanisms including apoptosis, glycolysis, PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling, and inflammatory responses. Notably, TLR4 and PTPRD—putative receptors for asprosin—are significantly downregulated in endometrial cancer patients compared to healthy controls, with higher TLR4 expression correlating with improved overall survival and serving as a prognostic indicator. Asprosin activates diverse oncogenic pathways including Notch, Wnt/β-catenin, IL6/JAK/STAT3, and TGF-β signaling, while promoting hypoxia-responsive CA9 expression through pro-inflammatory cytokine induction. TLR4 engagement by asprosin triggers a pro-inflammatory cascade via NF-κB activation, which upregulates COX-2 and modulates immune escape mechanisms. These findings suggest that asprosin-TLR4 axis dysregulation contributes to endometrial cancer pathogenesis, positioning TLR4 as a clinically relevant biomarker for patient stratification and prognosis.

title: Emerging roles of KIR2DL4 in cancer immunotherapy
summary:
# Summary

KIR2DL4 is an inhibitory receptor uniquely expressed on all natural killer cells that binds to HLA-G, a non-classical MHC class I molecule frequently overexpressed on tumor cells and fetal tissues. This KIR2DL4-HLA-G interaction transmits inhibitory signals through SHP-1 and SHP-2 recruitment, suppressing NK cell activation and antitumor cytotoxicity while simultaneously facilitating immune escape in cancers including melanoma, lung cancer, and ovarian cancer. The receptor displays functional complexity with allelic variants—the 10A allele encoding a full-length membrane-expressed receptor with dual activating and inhibitory capacity, while the 9A allele lacks inhibitory function due to a premature stop codon. Blocking the KIR2DL4-HLA-G axis enhances antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity and trastuzumab efficacy in breast cancer, suggesting that therapeutic targeting of this pathway through KIR blockade, SHP inhibition, or CAR-NK cell engineering represents a promising strategy to overcome immune evasion mechanisms in HLA-G-expressing tumors. KIR2DL4 expression levels also correlate with immune infiltration and serve as a potential prognostic marker for cancer outcomes.

title: Progress in animal models for studying hyperuricemia
summary:
# Summary

Hyperuricemia is a chronic metabolic disease resulting from dysregulation of purine metabolism and uric acid excretion, representing a significant health burden comparable to hypertension and hyperglycemia. Elevated serum uric acid is associated with gout, chronic kidney disease, and cardiovascular disease, with pathogenesis involving impaired function of renal and intestinal uric acid transporters (URAT1, GLUT9, ABCG2) and increased xanthine oxidase activity. Critically, soluble uric acid crystals activate a TLR4-MyD88-NF-κB-NLRP3 inflammasome cascade in podocytes, leading to caspase-1 activation, IL-1β and IL-18 maturation, and pyroptotic cell death that disrupts glomerular filtration and causes proteinuria. Estrogen exerts protective effects by downregulating xanthine oxidase and uric acid reabsorptive transporters while upregulating the excretory transporter ABCG2; conversely, ovariectomy reverses these benefits. Emerging therapeutic approaches target the gut-NLRP3 inflammasome axis and SIRT1-mediated ABCG2 regulation, with butyrate and probiotics showing promise for reducing serum uric acid levels through transporter modulation.

title: Utilising Human Myometrial and Uterine Fibroid Stem Cell‐Derived Three Dimentional Organoids as a Robust Model System for Understanding the Pathophysiology of Uterine Fibroids
summary: This study developed three-dimensional organoid models from human myometrial and uterine fibroid stem cells to investigate the pathophysiology of uterine fibroids, which are characterized by excessive extracellular matrix deposition involving collagen and fibronectin accumulation. The organoids successfully recapitulated key fibroid features, including elevated expression of fibrotic markers (COL1A1, COL3A1, periostin, and TGFβ-3), increased mechanical stiffness measured by Young's modulus, and enhanced estrogen receptor signaling that drives fibroid proliferation through estrogen and β-catenin pathways. Treatment with vitamin D3 and its receptor agonist doxercalciferol significantly reduced fibrotic markers and induced apoptosis by downregulating pro-survival factors (BCL-2) and inflammatory mediators (NF-κβ, IRF-3, CXCR4) through mechanotransduction and inflammatory pathway modulation. The research also identified environmental and genetic factors contributing to fibroids, including phthalate exposure and disparities in Black women who showed greater extracellular matrix accumulation and DNA methyltransferase activity. These findings provide a robust experimental platform for understanding fibroid pathogenesis and validating therapeutic strategies targeting fibrosis and inflammation.

title: Progress of estrogen receptor and spliceosome in endometrial carcinoma
summary:
# Summary

This article examines estrogen receptor signaling and alternative splicing in endometrial carcinoma (EC) development. Estrogen drives EC through multiple receptor pathways: ER-α mediates proliferative signals via binding to estrogen-responsive elements and activating PI3K/AKT and MAPK pathways, while ER-β antagonizes ER-α and acts as a growth inhibitor with tumor-suppressive properties. GPER, a membrane estrogen receptor, mobilizes calcium and activates PI3K and MAPK cascades to promote cell proliferation and gene regulation. Aberrant splicing of estrogen receptors—particularly elevated ER-α36 and ER-β5 variants—contributes to tamoxifen resistance and altered signaling patterns in EC tissues. The balance between progesterone inhibition and estrogen-driven growth is critical; inadequate progesterone production allows excessive estrogen to promote endometrial hyperplasia and carcinogenesis, with MAPK/ERK pathway activation closely associated with EC proliferation and chemoresistance. These findings highlight how dysregulation of estrogen receptor signaling and alternative splicing mechanisms collectively drive endometrial malignancy.

title: Nuclear receptors in metabolic, inflammatory, and oncologic diseases: mechanisms, therapeutic advances, and future directions
summary:
# Summary

Nuclear receptors function as ligand-activated transcription factors that serve as intracellular sensors, converting metabolic and hormonal signals into gene expression changes that regulate metabolic homeostasis, inflammation, immune function, and energy expenditure. Key receptor subtypes including PPARs, FXR, LXR, and thyroid hormone receptors control distinct metabolic processes: PPARα promotes fatty acid oxidation and lipid clearance in liver and muscle; PPARγ enhances adipogenesis and insulin sensitivity in adipose tissue; and FXR and LXR regulate cholesterol metabolism and bile acid synthesis. Dysregulation of nuclear receptor signaling is linked to metabolic and chronic diseases, and therapeutic agonists targeting these pathways have demonstrated clinical efficacy—PPARγ agonists treat type 2 diabetes by improving insulin sensitivity, while FXR and thyroid hormone receptor agonists show promise for liver diseases including MASH and MASLD. However, side effects such as fluid retention and weight gain associated with PPARγ agonists highlight the need for optimized therapeutic strategies. This comprehensive mechanistic understanding of nuclear receptor biology provides a foundation for developing improved therapeutic interventions across metabolic, cardiovascular, neurological, and immune-related diseases.

title: Advances in the therapeutic applications of dichloroacetate as a metabolic regulator: A review
summary:
# Summary

Dichloroacetate (DCA) functions as a metabolic regulator by inhibiting pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase (PDK), thereby maintaining pyruvate dehydrogenase complex in an active state and promoting aerobic oxidation of pyruvate over lactic acid synthesis. This metabolic shift enhances mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation and addresses the underlying energy metabolism disorders and mitochondrial dysfunction that contribute to various pathological conditions. DCA has demonstrated therapeutic efficacy in treating lactic acidosis and congenital mitochondrial diseases, while also showing protective effects in diabetes-related complications including improved cardiac function, reduced retinal degeneration, and improved ovarian and endometrial damage. In cancer cells characterized by the Warburg effect, DCA inhibits PDK-mediated glycolytic reliance, promoting a shift to oxidative phosphorylation and inducing apoptosis while simultaneously suppressing HIF-1α-driven tumor angiogenesis and proliferation. The compound's metabolic inactivation by glutathione transferase zeta-1 into oxalate represents a potential limitation requiring thiamin supplementation to prevent peripheral neurotoxicity, though genetic variations affecting DCA metabolism influence individual susceptibility to adverse effects.

title: Opposing profiles of Netrin-1 and adiponectin in metabolic inflammation and insulin resistance
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals opposing metabolic roles for Netrin-1 and adiponectin in obesity-associated inflammation and insulin resistance. Netrin-1 is elevated in clinical obesity and acts as a pathogenic mediator by binding to its receptor UNC5B on macrophages, promoting their retention in adipose tissue through cytoskeletal reorganization and activating NF-κB signaling to perpetuate chronic inflammation. In contrast, adiponectin levels are reduced in obesity and exerts protective effects by activating AMPK and inhibiting NF-κB to suppress inflammation and enhance insulin sensitivity. Genetic deletion of Netrin-1 in myeloid cells facilitates macrophage egress, attenuates adipose tissue inflammation, reduces adipocyte hypertrophy, and improves metabolic homeostasis, whereas elevated circulating Netrin-1 correlates with insulin resistance indices across obesity, prediabetes, and type 2 diabetes. The findings establish Netrin-1 and adiponectin as central opposing modulators of obesity-driven metabolic dysfunction, with Netrin-1 deletion representing a potential therapeutic strategy to break the cycle of macrophage-mediated inflammation and insulin resistance in metabolic disease.

title: Molecular Mechanisms of Phthalates in Depression: An Analysis Based on Network Toxicology and Molecular Docking
summary:
# Summary

This network toxicology and molecular docking study reveals that phthalates induce depression through multiple interconnected molecular pathways. Phthalates bind with high affinity to key regulatory proteins—CASP3, TNF, TP53, BCL2, and IL6—whose expression is significantly elevated in depressed individuals, as validated in the GEO dataset GSE23848. The mechanism involves phthalate-mediated activation of apoptotic cascades, where CASP3 triggers neuronal death, while TNF disrupts blood-brain barrier integrity and drives neuroinflammation that infiltrates the central nervous system. Additionally, TP53 upregulation activates tryptophan-metabolizing enzymes that reduce serotonin synthesis, and BCL2 downregulation amplifies oxidative stress, whereas IL6 overexpression activates neurotoxic microglia. Phthalates cross the blood-brain barrier to accumulate in emotion-regulating regions like the amygdala and hippocampus while disrupting intestinal barrier function, allowing lipopolysaccharide to trigger systemic inflammation through AGE-RAGE and PI3K-Akt signaling pathways. These findings demonstrate that phthalates—particularly elevated in women—function as endocrine disruptors converging on neuroinflammatory, apoptotic, and oxidative stress mechanisms to precipitate depression.

title: A Digital Image Analysis of the Morphology and Immunolocalization of Catalase and Caspase-3 in the Skin of Adult Male Rats After Treatment with Letrozole and Vitamin C
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how letrozole, an aromatase inhibitor used to treat endometriosis, affects skin morphology and oxidative stress markers in male rats, and how vitamin C co-treatment modulates these effects. Letrozole blocks cytochrome P450 aromatase, the enzyme encoded by CYP19A1 that converts androgens to estrogens in skin fibroblasts, hair follicles, sebaceous glands, and adipocytes, thereby inducing estrogen deficiency. The resulting estrogen depletion caused morphological changes, oxidative stress, and programmed cell death in skin tissue, including decreased collagen fiber bundle width and reduced catalase expression, an important antioxidant defense mechanism. Vitamin C supplementation counteracted these letrozole-induced effects by upregulating catalase expression, increasing collagen fiber width, and promoting collagen biosynthesis through its roles as an antioxidant and enzymatic cofactor. These findings highlight the critical role estrogen plays in maintaining skin homeostasis through estrogen receptor signaling and the protective potential of antioxidant supplementation during aromatase inhibitor therapy in endometriosis treatment.

title: CD95/Fas stoichiometry in future precision medicine
summary:
# Summary

CD95 is a ubiquitously expressed death receptor that functions as a critical regulator of immune homeostasis and tumor surveillance through both apoptotic and non-apoptotic mechanisms. The receptor pre-associates at the plasma membrane in a ligand-independent fashion via its pre-ligand assembly domain (PLAD), a feature shared with other TNF superfamily receptors including TNFR1, TRAILR1, and CD40. CD95 mutations or dysregulation of its signaling pathways lead to autoimmunity and inflammation, including autoimmune lymphoproliferative syndrome, while metalloprotease cleavage of CD95 ligand generates soluble CD95L, which paradoxically promotes inflammatory signaling through NF-κB and PI3K pathways rather than apoptosis. CD95's involvement in cancer is context-dependent: its expression is necessary for growth of ovarian cancer, hepatocellular carcinoma, and glioma, and correlates with tumor grade in breast cancer, yet elimination of CD95 in cancer cells can release aberrant NF-κB signaling. The discovery that selective inhibitors like DB550 can block CD95-mediated PI3K signaling and alleviate symptoms in lupus-prone models demonstrates the therapeutic potential of targeting specific CD95 signaling nodes in precision medicine approaches to autoimmunity and cancer.

title: Rational design of antibodies with pH-dependent antigen-binding properties using structural insights from broadly neutralizing antibodies against α-neurotoxins
summary:
# Summary

This study rationally designed pH-dependent antibodies against α-neurotoxins by leveraging structural insights from broadly neutralizing antibodies. The A01 monoclonal antibody, which differs from related antibodies only in its light chain, exhibits pH-sensitive binding to α-cobratoxin and α-elapitoxin, dissociating 16.9–19.7 times faster at acidic pH 5.5 compared to neutral pH 7.4. Structural analysis revealed that the heavy chain CDR-H3 loop dominates the antibody-toxin interface through receptor mimicry of the muscle-type nicotinic acetylcholine receptor, while specific light chain residues such as Asp95 in CDR-L3 stabilize critical pH-dependent interactions with toxin arginine residues. The engineered pH-dependent binding properties, driven by ionizable residues rather than traditional histidine substitutions, enable improved pharmacokinetics through differential FcRn binding and have therapeutic applications in treating inflammatory diseases including endometriosis, complement dysregulation disorders, and cancer via acid-switched antibody platforms. Both A01 and D11 IgG1 antibodies provided complete protection against α-cobratoxin toxicity in mouse models, demonstrating the functional relevance of these rational design principles.

title: Mechanisms, therapeutic uses, and developmental perspectives of redox-active thiomolybdates
summary:
# Redox-Active Thiomolybdates: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Applications

Redox-active thiomolybdates, particularly tetrathiomolybdate (TTM), are copper-chelating metal-based therapeutics with established clinical use in treating Wilson's disease and copper toxicity. Functionally, thiomolybdates operate through multiple mechanisms including inhibition of mitochondrial respiration and reduction of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production, while also sequestering free copper and preventing its accumulation in tissues. Beyond their established role in genetic copper disorders, TTM demonstrates potent anticancer properties by inhibiting critical copper-dependent processes: it suppresses angiogenic growth factors (VEGF and bFGF) through NF-κB regulation, blocks the MEK-1/MAPK/ERK proliferation pathway, inhibits lysyl oxidase (LOX)-mediated extracellular matrix remodeling, and downregulates superoxide dismutase (SOD1) activity leading to elevated oxidative stress in cancer cells. Recent formulation advances, including bis-choline tetrathiomolybdate (BCTTM), improve bioavailability and stability while maintaining copper-depletion efficacy, with potential for combination therapy alongside conventional anticancer agents such as doxorubicin, cisplatin, and BRAF inhibitors like vemurafenib.

title: Humanized anti-P2X4 scFv reduces ATP-induced P2X4 currents and modulates excitability in human DRG neurons
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a humanized anti-P2X4 receptor single-chain variable fragment (hP2X4R hscFv) effectively inhibits P2X4R signaling in human dorsal root ganglia (DRG) neurons, a key site implicated in pain processing. The antibody reduces ATP-evoked ion currents and causes receptor relocalization to the perinuclear region without affecting overall P2X4R protein abundance. In electrophysiological recordings, hP2X4R hscFv selectively increased action potential threshold and suppressed depolarizing spontaneous fluctuations and spontaneous activity specifically in multi-firing DRG neurons, while single-firing neurons were unaffected. These findings are clinically significant because elevated depolarizing spontaneous fluctuations and spontaneous neuronal activity are associated with chronic pain states in human DRG neurons. The study supports P2X4R as a therapeutic target for chronic pain management and suggests that hP2X4R hscFv could provide an alternative to problematic pain treatments like opioids that carry risks of addiction, tolerance, and hyperalgesia.

title: Evaluation of a new commercial assay for the measurement of tissue factor activity of extracellular vesicles isolated from human plasma
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary: This study evaluated a new commercial assay (CY-QUANT MV-TF) for measuring tissue factor (TF) activity on extracellular vesicles (EVs) isolated from human plasma. Tissue factor, released by activated monocytes and cancer cells, binds to coagulation factor VII to form the TF-FVIIa complex, which physiologically initiates blood coagulation and is essential for hemostasis. Elevated plasma EV TF activity is associated with thrombotic diseases including cancer, sepsis, and viral infections, making accurate measurement clinically important. The study found that the CY assay detected lower EV TF activity than the established CH assay because the CY isolation protocol retained significantly more tissue factor pathway inhibitor (TFPI), an endogenous inhibitor of the TF-FVIIa complex. When TFPI was neutralized with inhibitory antibodies, both assays showed equivalent EV TF activity levels, demonstrating that the difference was due to residual TFPI rather than assay performance. These findings highlight the critical importance of wash stringency during EV isolation and the need to account for TFPI contamination when comparing commercial assays for measuring pathologically relevant EV TF activity.

title: Targeting prostaglandin E2 receptor 2 in Schwann cells inhibits inflammatory pain but not inflammation
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a critical dissociation between pain signaling and inflammation in prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) physiology. The researchers demonstrate that EP2 receptors in Schwann cells specifically mediate PGE2-induced mechanical allodynia through a compartmentalized cAMP/PKA signaling pathway anchored by AKAP79/150, whereas EP4 receptors in dorsal root ganglion neurons mediate transient non-evoked nociception. Using selective antagonists and cell-type-specific genetic silencing, they show that blocking EP2 in Schwann cells robustly attenuates inflammatory pain in carrageenan and complete Freund's adjuvant models without affecting edema or other inflammatory markers. Importantly, EP2 antagonism in Schwann cells reduces pain as effectively as systemic NSAIDs (like indomethacin) but without suppressing the inflammatory response itself, suggesting that pain resolution proceeds independently of inflammation resolution. These findings identify Schwann cell EP2 as a druggable target for pain management that could provide analgesia without the anti-inflammatory side effects of conventional NSAIDs, offering a novel therapeutic strategy for inflammatory pain conditions including endometriosis.

title: NLRP3 Inflammasome in Stress-Related Neuropsychiatric Disorders: Mechanisms of Neuron–Microglia–Astrocyte Crosstalk, HPA Axis Dysregulation, and Therapeutic Perspective
summary:
# Summary: NLRP3 Inflammasome in Stress-Related Neuropsychiatric Disorders

Chronic stress disrupts neuroimmune homeostasis and initiates central nervous system inflammation through dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and sympathetic nervous system activation, which collectively increase production of pro-inflammatory cytokines. The NLRP3 inflammasome, expressed in microglia, neurons, and astrocytes, serves as a critical molecular hub in this process, becoming activated by damage-associated molecular patterns (ATP and HMGB1) and triggering caspase-1-dependent maturation of interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and IL-18. These mature cytokines promote neuroinflammatory cascades that impair synaptic plasticity, enhance glutamatergic transmission disturbances, induce neuronal apoptosis, and promote neuronal hyperreactivity in the amygdala through inhibition of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor function and calcium/calmodulin-dependent protein kinase II phosphorylation. Chronic HPA axis activation paradoxically leads to glucocorticoid resistance under allostatic load, preventing normal immune suppression and perpetuating sustained elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines that stabilize an inflammatory microglial phenotype. These findings establish NLRP3-driven neuroinflammation as a key mechanistic link between chronic stress and neuropsychiatric pathologies including posttraumatic stress disorder and depression, suggesting that NLRP3 inflammasome modulation represents a potential therapeutic target for stress-related disorders.

title: Matrix Metalloproteinase Inhibitors and Their Potential Clinical Application in Periodontitis
summary:
# Summary

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are zinc- and calcium-dependent endopeptidases that play dual roles as essential mediators of physiological processes including tissue remodeling, wound repair, and angiogenesis, but become pathologically destructive in periodontitis when excessively activated. In periodontal disease, dysbiotic biofilms trigger exacerbated host responses and proinflammatory cytokine release (IL-1β and TNF-α), which induce MMP synthesis and activation by reactive oxygen species, leading to excessive collagen, fibronectin, and elastin degradation by MMP-8 and MMP-9. MMP-8, primarily synthesized by neutrophils, is particularly associated with irreversible periodontal tissue destruction and alveolar bone loss, with elevated active MMP-8 levels in gingival crevicular fluid serving as a biomarker for disease severity and progression. Specific periodontal pathogens including Fusobacterium nucleatum, Tannerella forsythia, and Treponema denticola further drive MMP upregulation and tissue destruction. Matrix metalloproteinase inhibitors represent a promising therapeutic approach by reducing inflammation, preserving connective tissue integrity, and preventing the progression of periodontitis and its associated complications including alveolar bone loss and attachment loss.

title: The effect of Hydrocortisone on implantation through upregulation of
summary: This study demonstrates a concentration-dependent dual role of hydrocortisone in regulating endometrial tight junction proteins critical for blastocyst implantation. Emotional stress elevates cortisol levels, which dysregulates the implantation process by affecting tight junction protein expression in endometrial epithelial cells; however, therapeutic hydrocortisone at optimal concentrations (100 nM) upregulates claudins (CLDN3, CLDN4), zonula occludens-1 (ZO-1), desmoglein-1 (DSG1), and E-cadherin (CDH1), strengthening the endometrial epithelial barrier. The mechanism involves glucocorticoid receptor signaling, where ligand-receptor complexes act as transcriptional regulators of tight junction gene expression, though higher concentrations (200 nM) paradoxically downregulate these proteins, suggesting glucocorticoid receptor saturation and potential glucocorticoid resistance. Beyond barrier function, hydrocortisone provides dual immunological benefits by suppressing natural killer cells that would otherwise reject the implanting embryo, which is particularly relevant in recurrent miscarriage cases. These findings indicate that glucocorticoid therapy presents a double-edged sword: appropriate doses may facilitate implantation through tightened epithelial junctions and immunosuppression, while excessive doses impair barrier function and reduce implantation success.

title: Interaction Between Thyroid Hormones and Bone Morphogenetic Proteins in the Regulation of Steroidogenesis by Granulosa Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the crosstalk between thyroid hormones and bone morphogenetic proteins in regulating steroidogenesis in granulosa cells. Triiodothyronine (T3) potently upregulates multiple steroidogenic enzymes including StAR, P450scc, 3βHSD2, and aromatase, thereby enhancing FSH-induced estradiol production, while thyroxine (T4) shows more selective effects on aromatase and 3βHSD2. T3 suppresses BMP-6 signaling by inhibiting Smad1/5/9 phosphorylation while upregulating the inhibitory Smad6 and downregulating BMP type-II receptor expression, effectively antagonizing BMP-6's inhibitory effects on progesterone synthesis. The reciprocal regulation is demonstrated by BMP-6's upregulation of thyroid hormone receptor β, revealing bidirectional communication between these two signaling pathways. These findings indicate that thyroid hormone dysregulation, which impairs follicular steroidogenesis and is associated with menstrual disturbances and infertility, may partly result from dysbalanced interactions with the BMP signaling axis in ovarian cells.

title: Cannabinoids in Chronic Pain: Clinical Outcomes, Adverse Effects and Legal Challenges
summary: Cannabinoids exert analgesic effects through dual receptor mechanisms distributed across the nervous and immune systems. CB1 receptors, predominantly located in the central nervous system including the dorsal horn, periaqueductal gray, thalamus, and amygdala, inhibit the release of excitatory neurotransmitters including glutamate and substance P, thereby modulating pain transmission and engaging descending pain modulatory pathways. CB2 receptors, primarily found on immune cells and macrophages, reduce proinflammatory cytokine production including TNF-α and IL-1β while dampening microglial activation and neurogenic inflammation. The endogenous cannabinoids anandamide and 2-arachidonoylglycerol bind to these receptors and regulate synaptic plasticity through presynaptic CB1 signaling, with anandamide additionally acting via TRPV1 to shape dorsal horn pain transmission. Clinical evidence demonstrates that cannabinoids, particularly inhaled cannabis and nabiximols, effectively reduce neuropathic pain and pain in multiple sclerosis, with cannabis use associated with approximately 64% reductions in opioid consumption, though efficacy varies across pain conditions and high-dose CBD carries hepatotoxic risks. Ongoing trials are evaluating cannabinoid formulations including dronabinol for endometriosis-related chronic pelvic pain and various synthetic cannabinoid preparations for neuropathic and musculoskeletal pain conditions.

title: Expression of Follicle-Stimulating Hormone Receptor in Von Hippel-Lindau Associated Tumors and Cysts: An Immunohistochemical Study
journal: Journal of Kidney Cancer and VHL
summary:
# Summary

This immunohistochemical study demonstrates that follicle-stimulating hormone receptor 1 (FSHR1) is uniformly expressed in the blood vessel endothelium and tumor cells of Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL)-associated tumors, including clear cell renal carcinoma, central nervous system hemangioblastoma, and pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. VHL gene mutations lead to constitutive overproduction of hypoxia-inducible factors (HIF-1α and HIF-2α), which drive excessive tumor vascularization through upregulation of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). The ectopic expression of FSHR1 in tumor vasculature marks functionally active angiogenic endothelium that correlates with clinical response to anti-angiogenic therapies like sunitinib, suggesting FSHR1 serves as a biomarker for disease detection and therapeutic response. These findings identify FSHR1 as a novel theranostic target in VHL-associated tumors, with potential applications for both imaging and targeted anti-angiogenic therapy in angiogenesis-driven malignancies.

title: Topical frankincense treatment on relieving high-risk diabetic foot in rats by reducing inflammation and improving microcirculation
summary:
# Summary

Topical frankincense oil extract (FOE) effectively treats high-risk diabetic foot in rats through dual mechanisms of vascular repair and inflammatory suppression. Diabetes-induced hyperglycemia causes vascular endothelium dysfunction, which upregulates inflammatory mediators β-catenin, COX-2, and MMP-9, while simultaneously downregulating the endothelial marker CD31 and the neuropathic sensor TRPV3. FOE reverses these pathological changes by upregulating CD31 and TRPV3 while downregulating all three inflammatory factors, thereby restoring blood circulation, improving plantar temperature and motor nerve conduction velocity, and repairing vascular intimal damage. Additionally, FOE inhibits pathological arteriovenous shunt formation that normally diverts blood from diabetic microcirculation.
