The study identifies β-catenin as a central regulatory hub controlling COX-2 and MMP-9 expression, with β-catenin inhibition alone (via XAV-939) producing measurable therapeutic benefits that support frankincense's multi-target anti-inflammatory mechanism.

title: Posttranscriptional control of hepatic CEACAM1 3′UTR by human antigen R (HuR) mitigates sterile liver inflammation
summary:
# Summary

The hepatic RNA-binding protein HuR (encoded by ELAVL1) protects against sterile liver inflammation by stabilizing CEACAM1 mRNA through binding to cis-elements in its 3′UTR. HuR-deficient mice exhibit reduced CEACAM1-S expression, elevated markers of cellular stress and necrosis, and enhanced inflammatory responses including increased IL-6 production following lipopolysaccharide challenge. CEACAM1, particularly its short isoform (CEACAM1-S), functions as an anti-inflammatory transmembrane glycoprotein that safeguards hepatocytes from cell death during hypoxia-reoxygenation and cold stress by suppressing ROS accumulation and p38 MAPK activation. Therapeutic approaches targeting the CEACAM1 3′UTR with morpholino oligomers or using short-activating RNAs to enhance HuR expression preferentially induce CEACAM1-S splicing and significantly improve hepatocyte viability and reduce liver injury in experimental models. Clinical correlation in human transplant recipients demonstrates that elevated hepatic ELAVL1/CEACAM1 levels are associated with reduced proinflammatory phenotypes, improved tissue homeostasis, and lower incidence of early allograft dysfunction. These findings establish the HuR-CEACAM1 axis as a critical post-transcriptional regulatory mechanism controlling hepatic inflammation and cell survival.

title: Z-DNA-binding protein 1-mediated programmed cell death: Mechanisms and therapeutic implications
summary:
# Summary

Z-DNA-binding protein 1 (ZBP1) is a cytosolic nucleic acid sensor that functions as a critical regulator of multiple forms of programmed cell death including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and PANoptosis, with particular relevance to inflammatory and immune responses. Upon activation by pathogens such as influenza A virus or by inflammatory stimuli like interferon-gamma and lipopolysaccharide, ZBP1 engages the RIPK1–RIPK3–CASP8 axis to trigger cell death pathways, while also regulating NLRP3 inflammasome activation during pyroptosis. ZBP1's Z-DNA binding domains and RHIM motifs enable it to sense abnormal nucleic acid conformations and coordinate innate immune responses, playing roles in antiviral defense, autoimmunity, and cancer immunotherapy. The protein exhibits dysregulation in various pathological conditions including cancers, autoimmune diseases, neurological disorders, and inflammatory diseases, suggesting therapeutic potential for modulating ZBP1 activity. ADAR1 serves as an endogenous inhibitor of ZBP1 activation, preventing aberrant cell death responses under normal conditions. Collectively, these findings establish ZBP1 as a central hub integrating multiple cell death pathways with innate immune signaling, with implications for developing targeted therapeutics in diseases characterized by dysregulated cell death and inflammation.

title: Programmed cell death in triple-negative breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is an aggressive malignancy lacking estrogen, progesterone, and HER2 receptors that exhibits rapid progression, early metastasis, and frequent multidrug resistance. Multiple programmed cell death pathways—including apoptosis, pyroptosis, necroptosis, autophagy, and ferroptosis—are dysregulated in TNBC and contribute to both tumor pathogenesis and potential therapeutic opportunities. TNBC cells evade apoptosis by upregulating anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 family proteins while downregulating pro-apoptotic factors, and this apoptotic resistance is further reinforced by MUC1 overexpression, which activates NF-κB and EGFR/PI3K/Akt signaling to promote survival gene transcription. Necroptosis has emerged as a compensatory cell death mechanism in apoptosis-resistant TNBC cells and shows promise for overcoming therapeutic resistance, while ferroptosis driven by lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress offers an alternative approach to eliminate tumor cells. Therapeutically inducing programmed cell death can suppress tumor growth, inhibit metastasis, and reshape the tumor microenvironment to activate immune responses, suggesting that targeting multiple cell death pathways may overcome the therapeutic challenges posed by TNBC's inherent resistance mechanisms.

title: The role of pyroptosis in ovarian cancer
summary:
# Summary

Pyroptosis is a gasdermin-dependent form of programmed cell death closely linked to ovarian cancer occurrence and progression, representing a potential therapeutic target in this malignancy. The process is initiated when inflammatory caspases, particularly caspase-1 activated by inflammasomes including NLRP1 and NLRP3, cleave gasdermin family proteins (especially GSDMD) into their N-terminal fragments that form membrane pores. These GSDM pores disrupt cellular ion balance, cause cell swelling and rupture, and release large quantities of inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β and IL-18 that amplify immune responses. Multiple regulatory mechanisms control pyroptosis in ovarian cancer, including GSDM family proteins, long non-coding RNAs, and natural compounds such as osthole and nobiletin, while chemotherapeutic agents like paclitaxel and immunotherapies including PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors and CAR-T cells can enhance anti-tumor immunity through pyroptosis induction. Notably, pyroptosis functions as a double-edged sword in cancer, with context-dependent roles that may either inhibit or promote tumor progression, making understanding its regulation essential for developing effective ovarian cancer treatments.

title: Excessive progesterone impairs mouse decidualization via the Kyn-AhR pathway
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

While progesterone is essential for pregnancy establishment, excessive progesterone impairs decidualization in mice through activation of the kynurenine-aryl hydrocarbon receptor (Kyn-AhR) pathway. Excessive progesterone upregulates the tryptophan-metabolizing enzymes IDO1 and TDO via progesterone receptor signaling, leading to increased kynurenine production in the uterus. Kynurenine then activates AhR and its downstream targets CYP1A1 and CYP1B1, which metabolize estrogen to generate 2-hydroxyestradiol and 4-hydroxyestradiol; these metabolites directly inhibit decidualization markers Prl8a2, Prl3c1, and BMP2. Blocking this pathway at multiple points—using IDO1 antagonist epacadostat, progesterone receptor antagonist RU486, or AhR antagonist CH-223191—effectively restored decidualization capacity. These findings reveal a pathological feedback loop whereby excessive progesterone paradoxically impairs the uterine environment necessary for embryo implantation by redirecting tryptophan metabolism toward immune-suppressive kynurenine signaling and estrogen catabolism.

title: Regulation of aquaporin-2 using traditional Chinese medicine in water balance disorders: a literature review
summary:
# Summary

Aquaporin-2 (AQP2) is a critical membrane protein localized to renal collecting duct cells that regulates water reabsorption and maintains fluid balance through the arginine vasopressin (AVP) signaling pathway, which activates protein kinase A to phosphorylate and traffic AQP2 to the apical membrane. Dysregulation of AQP2 is associated with pathological conditions including hypertension, heart failure, and kidney disease. This literature review examines traditional Chinese medicine formulations and their bioactive compounds as modulators of AQP2 expression and water metabolism. Some agents such as Zhenwu decoction, Poria cocos, Rhizoma alismatis, and Ephedra downregulate AQP2 expression to promote diuresis and treat edema, while others like Er Shen Wan increase AQP2 expression to enhance water reabsorption for polyuria treatment. Several compounds including Lagopsis supina components (stachysoside A and acteoside) and Phytolacca acinosa regulate AQP2 through additional mechanisms involving the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system and atrial natriuretic peptide signaling. These findings suggest that traditional Chinese medicine offers multiple pharmacological approaches to normalize water balance disorders by targeting aquaporin regulation and related hormonal pathways.

title: Prostaglandin F2α exacerbates ovalbumin-induced chronic pneumonia
journal: The Journal of Veterinary Medical Science
summary: Prostaglandin F2α, administered via the FP receptor agonist fluprostenol, exacerbates ovalbumin-induced airway inflammation by increasing inflammatory cell infiltration and upregulating expression of inflammatory mediators in the lungs. Paradoxically, fluprostenol stimulation of FP receptors simultaneously activates serotonergic pathways in the hippocampus, increasing both serotonin 1A receptor and tryptophan hydroxylase expression, which reduces depression-like behaviors in the chronic asthma model. This creates a dual and opposing effect wherein FP receptor activation promotes lung inflammation while attenuating asthma-associated depression through enhanced hippocampal serotonin signaling. The findings highlight a mechanistic disconnect between pulmonary and neuropsychiatric responses to prostaglandin F2α, suggesting that the same signaling pathway can simultaneously worsen allergic airway disease while improving mood disorders in the context of chronic asthma.

title: Unravelling the Intricate Link: Mast Cells and Estrogen-Induced Pain Sensitization in Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and dyspareunia, involves a complex neuroimmune mechanism centered on estrogen-driven mast cell activation. Elevated estrogen in endometriotic lesions directly activates mast cells, which accumulate within lesions and release key mediators including histamine and fibroblast growth factor 2 (FGF2) that enhance peripheral pain signaling. Estrogen amplifies this response through multiple pathways: it upregulates aromatase expression via inflammatory cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-α, creates a feed-forward loop promoting local estrogen synthesis and mast cell proliferation, and activates the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway leading to IL-1β production. Additionally, estrogen modulates nociceptor activity by increasing substance P release and regulating ion channels like TRPV1, while promoting neuroangiogenesis through neurotrophins (NGF, BDNF, VEGF) that facilitate nerve fiber density increases and perineural invasion within lesions. These peripheral sensitization mechanisms, combined with central sensitization involving dorsal horn hyperexcitability and altered brain structure, collectively drive the severe pain phenotype, suggesting that therapeutic strategies targeting estrogen pathways—particularly aromatase inhibitors and estrogen receptor modulators—represent promising approaches for endometriosis management.

title: The impact of post-translational modifications and subcellular localization on NLRP3 inflammasome activation: A systematic review
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome is a critical multiprotein complex composed of NLRP3, ASC, and Caspase-1 that functions in innate immunity but can cause inflammatory and autoimmune disorders when dysregulated. Assembly and activation of this complex are tightly regulated by post-translational modifications, including phosphorylation, ubiquitination, and SUMOylation, which modulate NLRP3 subcellular localization across organelles such as the endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, and Golgi apparatus. Key kinases like BTK and PGK1 phosphorylate NLRP3 to regulate oligomerization, while E3 ubiquitin ligases such as MARCH5, TRIM24, and TRIM50 control NLRP3 stability and activation through differential ubiquitination patterns. Once activated, Caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1β, pro-IL-18, and GSDMD to drive inflammatory responses, and this pathway is primed by pattern recognition receptors including TLR4 and TNF signaling through NF-κB-mediated transcription. Notably, TRIM24 is downregulated in ectopic endometrium, suggesting dysregulated NLRP3 inflammasome signaling may contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis, while inhibitors like psoralen and deubiquitinases can suppress inflammasome activation and inflammation.

title: A Systematic Review and Integrated Bioinformatic Analysis on the Gene Expression of Cumulus-Oocyte Complex Following In Vitro Maturation
journal: The Malaysian Journal of Medical Sciences : MJMS
summary:
# Summary

Cumulus cells play essential roles in oocyte maturation through multiple coordinated mechanisms, including production of steroid hormones, extracellular matrix components, and antioxidants, while communicating with the oocyte via gap junctions and cAMP/MAPK signaling pathways. In vitro maturation (IVM) is a promising technology for treating infertility and polycystic ovarian syndrome, though bioinformatic analysis reveals significant differences in gene expression between IVM and in vivo-matured oocytes. The luteinizing hormone receptor (LHCGR) signaling cascade plays a critical role, where LH activation of LHCGR triggers upregulation of the EGF family members AREG and EREG, which bind EGFR and ultimately activate maturation-promoting factor through disruption of gap junction communication. However, AREG and EREG are substantially downregulated during IVM compared to natural ovulation, and this reduced expression correlates with lower pregnancy and live birth rates, suggesting that recapitulating the full gonadotropin surge response may be necessary to optimize in vitro oocyte maturation outcomes. The expansion of cumulus cells, facilitated by HAS2-produced hyaluronic acid, is similarly diminished in IVM-matured complexes compared to IVF-matured ones, highlighting key molecular differences that impact clinical success.

title: Harnessing smart nanomaterials to reprogram neutrophil plasticity in immune modulation
summary:
# Summary

Smart nanomaterials represent a promising therapeutic approach that harnesses neutrophil plasticity to modulate immune responses, particularly in inflammatory conditions. Neutrophils are critical immune cells that mediate pathogen recognition and elimination through phagocytosis-triggered production of antimicrobial peptides, proteases, and reactive oxygen species, while excessive neutrophil activation can lead to harmful outcomes including neutrophil extracellular trap formation and sustained pro-inflammatory chemokine secretion. Engineered nanoparticles—including PLGA, liposomes, and magnetic mesoporous silica platforms—can be surface-modified with targeting ligands such as cRGD, sialic acid, or PEG to selectively deliver therapeutic agents to neutrophils via integrin, selectin, or Fc receptor interactions, enabling preferential uptake by inflammatory neutrophils while reducing systemic exposure. This neutrophil-hijacking strategy has demonstrated efficacy in preclinical models of rheumatoid arthritis and atherosclerosis by using nanoparticles to transport drugs like celastrol and sivelestat sodium directly to sites of inflammation. The degradation byproducts of polymeric nanoparticles integrate safely into normal metabolic pathways, while magnetic variants enable real-time tracking of drug delivery, establishing nanomedicine as a sophisticated approach to reprogram neutrophil behavior and achieve targeted immune modulation in inflammatory disease.

title: NLRP3 Inflammasome in Gynecologic Inflammatory Diseases: Mechanisms, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutic Strategies
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome serves as a central hub for initiating inflammatory responses in gynecologic diseases including endometritis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and endometriosis. Activation of NLRP3 occurs through multiple pathways—including toll-like receptor signaling via NF-κB, potassium efflux, reactive oxygen species, and pathogen-associated triggers from organisms such as Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, and Gardnerella vaginalis—leading to recruitment of the ASC adaptor and subsequent caspase-1 activation. Once activated, caspase-1 promotes maturation and release of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, and cleaves gasdermin D (GSDMD) to initiate pyroptotic cell death, amplifying inflammatory injury in affected tissues. The non-classical inflammasome pathway, activated by lipopolysaccharide, similarly triggers GSDMD cleavage via caspase-11 and caspase-5, facilitating secondary NLRP3 activation and further cytokine release. M1-type macrophage polarization is significantly upregulated alongside NLRP3 in pelvic inflammatory disease, enhancing the inflammatory cascade. Understanding these interconnected NLRP3 signaling mechanisms provides therapeutic targets for managing inflammasome-driven pathology in endometriosis and related gynecologic inflammatory conditions.

title: Bisphenol A Interferes with Mast Cell-Mediated Promotion of Cellular Processes Critical for Spiral Artery Remodeling
summary:
# Summary

Mast cells play a critical role in uterine spiral artery remodeling by promoting trophoblast invasion and inducing a synthetic phenotype switch in uterine vascular smooth muscle cells, processes essential for healthy pregnancy. Bisphenol A (BPA), an endocrine disruptor that binds estrogen receptors and crosses the maternal-fetal barrier, inhibits mast cell function in a dose-dependent manner, thereby impairing the cellular processes required for proper spiral artery remodeling. BPA exposure suppresses mast cell-mediated promotion of trophoblast invasion and fibronectin expression while upregulating matrix metalloproteinase inhibitors and oxidative stress markers, ultimately compromising vascular remodeling. At physiologically relevant concentrations, BPA induces dose-dependent changes in gene expression, including inflammatory mediators at lower doses and cytotoxic responses at higher doses, with particular suppression of progesterone synthesis enzyme Hsd3b1. The impairment of spiral artery remodeling by BPA-induced mast cell dysfunction is mechanistically linked to adverse pregnancy outcomes including preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and implantation failure, suggesting BPA exposure as a significant environmental risk factor for endometriosis and gestational complications.

title: Sympathetic Biomarker Dynamics Post-Myocardial Infarction: TH, PGP9.5, and SYN Expression Discordance in Murine Hearts
summary:
# Summary

This murine study reveals discordant expression patterns of three sympathetic biomarkers following myocardial infarction. Tyrosine hydroxylase (TH), the rate-limiting enzyme in catecholamine biosynthesis, is upregulated by the angiotensin II-AT1R axis and shows elevated expression in infarcted tissue, where it catalyzes local norepinephrine synthesis that contributes to arrhythmogenesis. While TH, PGP9.5 (ubiquitin carboxy-terminal hydrolase L1), and synaptophysin (SYN) mRNA levels similarly peak in post-MI tissue, their protein localization patterns diverge: TH and PGP9.5 accumulate in sympathetic nerves of the infarct epicardium and border zone, whereas SYN localizes primarily to fibroblast-like cells within the damaged myocardium. Post-MI sympathetic remodeling involves nerve growth factor-triggered axonal sprouting and dysregulated reinnervation, processes inhibited by chondroitin sulfate proteoglycans in the scar tissue. The resulting sympathetic hyperactivity and elevated norepinephrine-β-receptor signaling drive malignant arrhythmias that precipitate heart failure and sudden cardiac death, identifying TH as the most specific sympathetic marker for post-infarction cardiac remodeling and arrhythmia risk.

title: Dual Targeting of Orphan Nuclear Receptors NR4A1 and NR4A2 for Nonhormonal Endometriosis Therapy
summary: The orphan nuclear receptors NR4A1 and NR4A2 are highly expressed in endometriotic lesions and regulate key pathways driving endometriosis pathogenesis, including cell growth, survival, migration, and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT). DIM-3,5 derivatives—synthetic dual NR4A1/NR4A2 ligands—effectively suppress endometriotic cell proliferation and migration by downregulating multiple pro-endometriotic signaling molecules including EGFR, mTOR, fibrotic markers (CTGF, collagen I, fibronectin), and EMT regulators (TWIST1, Slug, Snail, ZEB1, N-cadherin). Treatment with DIM-3,5 compounds triggers apoptosis in endometriotic cells through caspase-3 activation and PARP cleavage while simultaneously promoting claudin-1 expression to maintain epithelial integrity. In vivo studies demonstrated that the DIM-3,5-Cl2 compound significantly reduced endometriotic lesion growth, Ki-67 proliferation markers, and increased apoptotic cell death in a mouse model without apparent toxicity. These findings establish dual NR4A1/NR4A2 targeting as a promising nonhormonal therapeutic strategy for endometriosis that addresses multiple disease mechanisms simultaneously.

title: Assessing The Effectiveness of Growth Hormone Releasing
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the effects of GHRP-6, a synthetic growth hormone releasing peptide, on oocyte maturation during in vitro maturation (IVM) through ghrelin receptor signaling. GHRP-6 activates GHSR-1a receptors on germinal vesicle stage oocytes, triggering the phospholipase C signaling cascade that leads to calcium release and accelerated meiotic progression, with an optimal concentration of 75 ng/mL promoting nuclear maturation and the extrusion of the first polar body. However, while GHRP-6 at 75 ng/mL effectively accelerates nuclear maturation and prevents meiotic arrest in a concentration-dependent manner, it does not enhance cytoplasmic maturation or alter expression of the genes CENP-E and LINGO2, which are associated with complete meiotic competence and embryonic development preparation. Notably, higher GHRP-6 concentrations (200-300 ng/mL) inhibit meiotic progression and induce oocyte degeneration, demonstrating a biphasic dose-response relationship. The findings suggest that low-dose ghrelin agonist treatment could improve IVM outcomes by expediting nuclear maturation in culture media, with potential applications in assisted reproductive technology for patients with polycystic ovary syndrome and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome.

title: The endocannabinoid system as a therapeutic target in intestinal fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

Intestinal fibrosis in inflammatory bowel disease involves activation of the TGF-β/Smad signaling pathway, which drives myofibroblast differentiation and excessive collagen deposition leading to stricture formation. The endocannabinoid system presents a dual therapeutic target: CB1 receptor activation paradoxically promotes fibrosis by upregulating TGF-β/Smad signaling and α-SMA expression, while CB2 receptor signaling exerts protective effects by inhibiting inflammation, fibroblast activation, and extracellular matrix deposition. Pharmacological approaches to enhance endocannabinoid tone through FAAH inhibitors (like URB597) or direct CB2 activation reduce colitis severity, inflammatory cell infiltration, and pro-fibrotic markers including TGF-β1 and Smad2. Conversely, CB1 antagonists (such as SR141716A) reduce fibrosis by blocking TGF-β/Smad signaling and inhibiting angiotensin II-mediated pathways critical for myofibroblast activation. Cannabidiol and selective CB2 agonists represent promising therapeutic strategies to interrupt the fibrotic cascade while avoiding CB1-dependent pro-fibrotic effects, offering a refined approach to treating intestinal fibrosis in IBD.

title: Diabetic Kidney Disease‐Associated Pathological Angiogenesis: The Role of Aquaporin‐1
summary:
# Summary

Hyperglycemia in diabetic kidney disease upregulates aquaporin-1 (AQP1) expression in renal endothelial cells, which serves as a central driver of pathological angiogenesis. The resulting abnormal neovascularization leads to structurally and functionally immature vessels that promote glomerular capillary leakage, proteinuria, basement membrane thinning, and progressive glomerular injury. AQP1 knockout studies demonstrate that loss of AQP1 disrupts both physiological fluid regulation and angiogenic capacity, establishing AQP1 as a critical regulator of vascular remodeling in diabetic kidney disease. Multiple natural compounds including hirudin, quercetin, and traditional Chinese medicine formulations (XinLi formula, Compound Danshen Dripping Pills) have been identified as potential therapeutic agents that inhibit or downregulate AQP1 expression and attenuate pathological angiogenesis. These findings position AQP1 as both an important biomarker and therapeutic target for mitigating the microvascular complications characteristic of diabetic kidney disease and end-stage renal disease progression.

title: Potential Changes in the Eutopic Endometrium in Endometriosis: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic inflammation and multifaceted endometrial dysfunction that severely compromises fertility through several interconnected mechanisms. The disease is characterized by enhanced pro-inflammatory immune profiles involving macrophages and Th17 cells that reduce endometrial receptivity, alongside a pathological estrogen-dominant state driven by increased aromatase and 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 activity in ectopic tissue. This hyperestrogeniemia, amplified through a feedback loop involving nitric oxide, cyclooxygenase-2, and prostaglandin-E2, combined with progesterone resistance from diminished progesterone receptor-B expression, impairs critical decidualization pathways including downregulation of ARID1A, Hand2, MIG-6, and the Indian hedgehog signaling cascade. Additionally, endometrial dysfunction is perpetuated by dysregulation of FOXO1 signaling through multiple mechanisms involving Calpain-7-mediated AKT phosphorylation and NEK2 overexpression, alongside reduced NOTCH signaling. These molecular alterations collectively shift the eutopic endometrium toward a pro-inflammatory, pro-proliferative phenotype resistant to apoptosis and characterized by compromised oocyte quality and dysregulated implantation windows, explaining the high infertility burden associated with endometriosis.

title: Endocrine therapy resistance of breast cancer: Important role of G protein-coupled estrogen receptor (GPER) and new therapeutic strategies
summary:
# Summary

This review examines endocrine therapy resistance in hormone receptor-positive (HR+) breast cancer, with emphasis on the G protein-coupled estrogen receptor (GPER) as a critical mediator of treatment failure. GPER, activated by estrogens including 17β-estradiol and estrone, initiates a cascade of signaling events through Src family kinases and matrix metalloproteinases, ultimately activating EGFR and downstream MAPK/ERK and PI3K/AKT pathways that promote cancer cell proliferation and survival. Long-term tamoxifen exposure paradoxically upregulates GPER expression, creating a feedback loop that drives tamoxifen resistance and increases expression of the drug efflux transporter ABCG2, leading to multidrug resistance. To overcome GPER-mediated resistance, multiple therapeutic strategies are discussed including selective estrogen receptor degraders (SERDs such as fulvestrant and elacestrant), estrogen receptor proteolysis-targeting chimeras (PROTACs), CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib), and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway inhibitors, which collectively improve progression-free and invasive disease-free survival. The identification of GPER as a resistance mechanism highlights the need for combination approaches targeting both classical nuclear estrogen receptors and non-genomic GPER signaling pathways in HR+ breast cancer management.

title: A comparison of commercial assays quantifying mature brain-derived neurotrophic factor (mBDNF) and its precursor (pro-BDNF) in human serum
summary: Brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) exists in two primary isoforms with distinct biological roles: pro-BDNF, synthesized in the endoplasmic reticulum and cleaved in the Golgi apparatus, and mature BDNF (mBDNF), generated through further proteolytic processing. Pro-BDNF binds to the p75NTR receptor to promote apoptotic signaling and neuronal pruning, while mBDNF selectively binds TrkB receptors to support neuronal survival, growth, and synaptic plasticity. An altered BDNF/pro-BDNF ratio is associated with neurological and neuropsychiatric diseases, highlighting the clinical importance of accurately quantifying these isoforms in human serum. This study evaluated commercial ELISA assays from R&D Systems, Aviscera-Bioscience, and FineTest for measuring total BDNF and its isoforms, finding that R&D Systems kits demonstrated superior specificity for both total and pro-BDNF measurement. However, no commercially available mBDNF assay exhibited complete specificity, though the R&D Systems kit (#DBD00) showed acceptable performance. BDNF isoform levels vary considerably across populations due to genetic polymorphisms and lifestyle factors including diet, physical activity, sleep patterns, and alcohol consumption, underscoring the need for reliable assay selection in clinical research.

title: Human Small Airway Epithelia Reveal Dichloroacetate as a Broad-Spectrum Antiviral Against Respiratory Viruses
summary:
# Summary

Respiratory viruses including HCoV-229E, SARS-CoV-2, and respiratory syncytial virus hijack host cellular metabolism by activating the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and its downstream effector HIF-1α, which collectively drive the Warburg effect and increase aerobic glycolysis to fuel viral replication. Dichloroacetate (DCA), a clinically approved inhibitor of aerobic glycolysis, demonstrated broad-spectrum antiviral activity by inhibiting pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase (PDK) to activate pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH), thereby shifting infected cells away from glycolytic metabolism toward mitochondrial respiration and reducing viral progeny by two to three orders of magnitude. In human small airway epithelial cells, DCA proved more effective than other metabolic inhibitors (lapatinib, wortmannin, everolimus), achieving near-complete suppression of HCoV-229E infection while simultaneously enhancing compensatory respiration. The metabolic targeting approach has shown efficacy across multiple viral families beyond respiratory viruses, including hepatitis, Zika, and West Nile virus infections, suggesting that metabolic reprogramming represents a conserved viral strategy and a promising therapeutic target. These findings establish that blocking aerobic glycolysis through DCA-mediated PDH activation represents a translatable antiviral strategy that could be rapidly advanced to clinical trials for respiratory virus infections.

title: The ExPAND Study: A Prospective Association Study into Endometriosis-Associated Pain, Neurosteroid Synthesis, and TRPM3
summary: The ExPAND study investigates a novel neurobiological mechanism linking endometriosis-associated pain to aberrant steroidogenesis and TRPM3 ion channel signaling. Endometrial lesions in endometriosis exhibit elevated levels of neurosteroid precursors, including pregnenolone and DHEA, which are locally converted via upregulated steroidogenic enzymes (CYP17A1, CYP19A1, HSD17β1, and 3β-HSD) into active neurosteroids that accumulate in peritoneal fluid at concentrations sufficient to activate TRPM3. The neurosteroid metabolites pregnenolone sulphate and DHEA-sulphate directly activate TRPM3, a calcium-permeable ion channel expressed in approximately 60% of dorsal root ganglia neurons, thereby stimulating sensory neurons and promoting inflammatory pain and hyperalgesia. Additionally, elevated estrogen levels in endometriosis tissue drive neurogenesis of pain-sensing nociceptive nerve fibers, and upregulation of TRP channel family members (TRPV1 and TRPA1) in peritoneal and endometrial tissue correlates with pelvic pain severity. These findings propose that abnormal neurosteroid synthesis and TRPM3 activation constitute a mechanistic link between steroid-dependent lesion proliferation and chronic pain in endometriosis, suggesting potential therapeutic targets for pain management independent of hormone suppression.

title: Pro-Angiogenic Bioactive Molecules in Vascular Morphogenesis: Integrating Endothelial Cell Dynamics
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the molecular mechanisms of vascular morphogenesis, with particular focus on VEGF signaling and its multiple pathways in endothelial cells. VEGF and its receptor family members (VEGFR1, VEGFR2, VEGFR3) orchestrate angiogenesis and vasculogenesis through distinct ligand-receptor interactions, with VEGFA binding both VEGFR1 and the primary pro-angiogenic receptor VEGFR2 to regulate endothelial cell mitogenesis, migration, and survival. VEGFR activation triggers two major signaling cascades: the PI3K-Akt pathway promoting cell growth and survival, and the MAPK-ERK pathway driving proliferation and migration, while also upregulating eNOS and MMPs to support vascular growth. VEGFR1 functions as a negative regulator of embryonic angiogenesis and VEGFA signaling through both its decoy receptor function and soluble variants (sVEGFR1), which sequester VEGF and reduce VEGFR2 signaling, creating a critical balance in angiogenic responses. VEGFR1 also promotes endothelial branching morphogenesis via Rab4A-dependent mechanisms and participates in inflammatory angiogenesis through monocyte and macrophage recruitment. The article notably identifies VEGFR1 signaling as essential for endometriosis-associated tissue growth, linking this pro-angiogenic axis to disease pathology.

title: Exploring the Relevance of S100A8 and S100A9 Proteins in Preeclampsia: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

This narrative review examines the emerging role of S100A8 and S100A9 proteins as inflammatory biomarkers in preeclampsia, a pregnancy disorder characterized by elevated blood pressure and proteinuria that carries serious fetal complications including intrauterine growth restriction, premature birth, and stillbirth. S100A8 and S100A9 are myeloid-related alarmin proteins produced by neutrophils, monocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells, and endothelial cells that activate Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) and the receptor for advanced glycation end products (RAGE) to promote inflammation and systemic immune responses. The pathophysiology of preeclampsia involves impaired remodeling of uterine spiral arteries leading to placental ischemia and hypoxia, which triggers release of reactive oxygen species and antiangiogenic factors such as soluble FMS-like tyrosine kinase 1 and soluble endoglin. Elevated S100A8/A9 levels correlate with the inflammatory cascade in preeclampsia and have been implicated in atherogenesis and atherosclerosis, linking the condition to long-term cardiovascular disease risk in affected women. The review proposes that S100A8/A9 represents a potentially useful plasmatic biomarker and therapeutic target for preeclampsia management, given their involvement in fibrosis, calcification, phagocyte migration, and angiogenic dysregulation central to disease pathogenesis.

title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging Using a Chimeric Anti-Glypican-3 Antibody Conjugated with Gadolinium Selectively Detects Glypican-3-Positive Hepatocellular Carcinoma In Vitro and In Vivo
summary: This study develops gadolinium-conjugated antibody probes targeting glypican-3 (GPC3) for magnetic resonance imaging of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). Glypican-3 is a cell surface proteoglycan highly overexpressed in approximately 77% of HCC cases but absent from normal liver tissue, making it an ideal diagnostic target. The researchers conjugated chimeric anti-GPC3 antibodies (ET58) and mouse anti-GPC3 antibodies (1G12) to gadolinium chelators (DOTA-Gd) to create imaging probes that selectively bind GPC3-expressing HCC cells in vitro and in vivo. The resulting ET58-DOTA-Gd probe demonstrated high specificity and imaging efficacy with favorable immunogenicity, showing selective identification of GPC3-positive tumors while avoiding GPC3-negative cells. These findings support the clinical translation of GPC3-directed imaging, which complements emerging GPC3-targeted therapies including CAR T cell therapy, CAR-NK cell therapy, and bispecific T cell engagers currently in clinical trials for HCC treatment.

title: Endocannabinoid System Regulation in Pyometra-Affected and Healthy Canine Uteri
summary:
# Summary

This study examined endocannabinoid system (eCS) regulation in canine pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection, revealing dysregulation of key signaling pathways linked to inflammation and immune dysfunction. The endocannabinoid system, comprising cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2 and ligands anandamide and 2-arachidonylglycerol, regulates both immunity and inflammation and is involved in reproductive physiology, including ovulation and implantation. In pyometra cases, anandamide levels were significantly reduced while CB1 receptor expression was markedly elevated in luminal epithelial cells, endometrial glands, immune cells, and myometrium. Prolonged progesterone exposure, characteristic of diestrus and pyometra, suppresses neutrophil migration and cytokine production while enhancing glandular secretions and promoting cystic endometrial hyperplasia, creating conditions favorable for bacterial infection. Bacterial lipopolysaccharide and *Escherichia coli* activate Toll-like receptor-4 signaling, triggering chemokine production, upregulating cyclooxygenase-2, and stimulating prostaglandin synthesis—inflammatory cascades that endocannabinoids can suppress via CB1/2-dependent mechanisms. These findings suggest that dysregulation of the endocannabinoid system in pyometra parallels similar dysfunction observed in endometriosis, highlighting the eCS as a potential therapeutic target for managing inflammatory reproductive diseases.

title: Unlocking therapeutic potential: Exploring nuclear receptors in brain cancer treatment
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the therapeutic potential of nuclear receptors (NRs) as targets for brain cancer treatment, particularly glioblastoma. Multiple NR subtypes—including androgen receptors (ARs), estrogen receptors (ERs), glucocorticoid receptors, progesterone receptors, and vitamin D receptors—are significantly elevated in brain cancer tissues and regulate critical cellular processes such as proliferation, survival, and invasion. Pharmacological targeting of ARs through compounds like ALZ003, cedrol, enzalutamide, and seviteronel effectively suppresses tumor cell growth by downregulating AR expression, inducing apoptosis through caspase activation, and promoting cell cycle arrest via p53/p21 upregulation. Similarly, ER-targeted therapies including tamoxifen, LY500307, and ER agonists demonstrate anti-tumor activity, with ERβ1 expression specifically associated with improved survival outcomes. Combination strategies—pairing AR or ER antagonists with conventional chemotherapy or radiation—show enhanced efficacy in preclinical models, suggesting that NR-targeted approaches represent promising adjunctive therapeutic options for improving brain cancer treatment outcomes.

title: Molecular mechanisms and regulation of inflammasome activation and signaling: sensing of pathogens and damage molecular patterns
summary:
# Summary

Inflammasomes are multi-protein immune signaling complexes composed of sensor proteins, the adaptor protein ASC, and caspase-1 that serve as critical regulators of innate immunity and inflammation. Upon activation, caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their bioactive forms while also cleaving gasdermin D, which forms pores in the plasma membrane to facilitate cytokine secretion and trigger pyroptosis through cooperation with ninjurin-1. Multiple sensor protein families, including the NLR family members NLRP1 and NLRP3, as well as AIM2, Pyrin, and caspase-4/-5/-11, can nucleate inflammasome assembly in response to diverse pathogen- and damage-associated signals ranging from bacterial toxins to viral nucleic acids. Dysregulation of inflammasome activation is linked to inflammatory diseases, and germline mutations in NLRP1 and variations in DPP9 contribute to autoinflammatory disorders, highlighting the importance of balanced inflammasome control. The NLRP1 and NLRP3 inflammasomes are being targeted therapeutically with small-molecule inhibitors like ADS032, offering potential strategies to restrain excessive inflammasome-driven inflammation in disease contexts.

title: TET3 is a common epigenetic immunomodulator of pathogenic macrophages
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies TET3-overexpressing macrophages (Toe-Macs) as pathogenic immune cells common to endometriosis, metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis, and non-small cell lung cancer. TET3 functions as an epigenetic regulator that activates inflammatory gene expression, particularly promoting NLRP3 inflammasome signaling through STAT3-mediated binding to promoters of NLRP3, IL-1β, TGF-β1, and CCL2. Disease microenvironments induce Toe-Macs through TGF-β1 and CCL2 signaling, which upregulate TET3 in a Smad3-dependent and synergistic manner, creating a positive feedback loop that sustains inflammatory macrophage activation. Genetic deletion of myeloid Tet3 or pharmacological TET3 degradation markedly reduced disease progression across all three conditions, while NLRP3 inhibition specifically reduced endometriotic lesion size. These findings establish TET3-dependent epigenetic control of macrophage inflammation as a unifying mechanism across diverse pathologies, offering TET3 degradation and NLRP3 inhibition as promising therapeutic approaches.

title: Evaluation of (Z)-endoxifen as a potential therapy for glioblastoma multiforme through computational and experimental analyses
summary: This computational and experimental study investigates endoxifen, the active metabolite of tamoxifen, as a potential therapeutic agent for glioblastoma multiforme (GBM). Endoxifen exerts multi-faceted anti-tumor effects through both estrogen receptor-dependent mechanisms (binding and antagonizing estrogen receptors alpha and beta) and estrogen-independent pathways (inhibiting protein kinase C beta). In GBM cells, endoxifen potently suppresses proliferation and induces apoptosis, particularly when combined with the standard chemotherapy agent temozolomide. Gene expression analysis revealed that GBM tumors aberrantly activate oncogenic pathways including cell-cycle regulators (E2F targets, G2-M checkpoint, Myc targets), growth signaling (mTORC1), and immune-related pathways (TNF-alpha signaling, interferon-gamma response), many of which are effectively inhibited by endoxifen treatment. Three genes highly expressed in GBM (HSPB1, RPA3, and NFKBIZ)—each associated with poor patient survival—were significantly downregulated by endoxifen. These findings suggest that endoxifen warrants clinical investigation as a novel GBM therapeutic, leveraging its established safety profile in other approved indications.

title: Anti-Angiogenic RNAi-Based Treatment of Endometriosis in a Rat Model Using CXCR4-Targeted Peptide Nanoparticles
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological disease involving hormonal dysregulation, immune system dysfunction, and pathological angiogenesis that compromises fertility. VEGFA plays a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and vascularization of ectopic lesions through estrogen and progesterone signaling. The SDF-1/CXCR4 axis contributes to disease development by recruiting endothelial progenitor cells to endometriotic foci. Researchers developed CXCR4-targeted peptide nanoparticles carrying anti-VEGFA siRNA, which achieved a 2.6-fold reduction in endometrial implant size and markedly suppressed VEGFA gene expression and microvessel development in a rat endometriosis model. The ternary polyplex formulation combining siRNA, a cationic R6p carrier, and an anionic CDP-E6pH coating modified with an SDF-1 ligand maintained stability in the extracellular environment while avoiding undesired protein interactions. These results demonstrate that targeted RNAi-based anti-angiogenic therapy represents a promising novel approach for treating endometriosis by specifically inhibiting VEGFA-mediated neovascularization in ectopic lesions.

title: Assessment of Annona muricata Phytochemicals as 17β‐HSD1 Inhibitors through Molecular Docking, Dynamics Simulation, DFT, and ADMET Analyses
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated twelve phytochemicals from Annona muricata as potential inhibitors of 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 (17β-HSD1), an enzyme that catalyzes the conversion of estrone to 17β-estradiol and is overexpressed in hormone-dependent breast cancers. Through molecular docking, dynamics simulation, density functional theory (DFT), and ADMET analyses, reticuline and calamenene emerged as the top candidates, with binding energies of −8.4 and −7.7 kcal/mol respectively and stable anchoring within the 17β-HSD1 catalytic pocket. Reticuline demonstrated superior physicochemical properties, including the lowest HOMO-LUMO gap (5.06 eV), highest softness (0.40 eV⁻¹), and lowest electrophilicity index (12.61 eV), while satisfying all Lipinski's rule of five criteria except for limited oral bioavailability due to P-glycoprotein-mediated active transport. Although calamenene also met most drug-like criteria and functioned as a potential P-glycoprotein inhibitor to enhance bioavailability of co-administered drugs, it violated the logP threshold. These findings suggest that Annona muricata phytochemicals, particularly reticuline, warrant further investigation as potential therapeutic agents for suppressing estrogen biosynthesis in hormone-dependent breast cancer.

title: Role of Peptides in Skeletal Muscle Wasting: A Scoping Review
journal: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle
summary: This scoping review identifies 87 peptides associated with skeletal muscle wasting and maps their roles in regulating muscle homeostasis across four major signaling pathways: PI3K/Akt/mTOR, ActR/SMAD, IKK/NF-κB, and AMPK/PGC1α. The most extensively studied peptides include ghrelin, brain natriuretic peptide, C-peptide, and insulin, with the identified peptides classified into distinct biological groups including neuropeptides, gastrointestinal peptides, myostatin/follistatin derivatives, and mitochondrially-derived peptides. These peptides regulate muscle mass, strength, and physical performance through transcription factors that promote either atrophy (FOXO, NF-κB, SMAD2/3, glucocorticoid receptor, and GSK-3β) or hypertrophy (androgen receptor, PGC-1α, and S6K) by modulating expression of critical genes like MuRF-1 and atrogin-1. Notably, 54% of identified peptides associate with beneficial muscle outcomes while 17.2% produce detrimental effects, underscoring their therapeutic potential. Muscle wasting carries substantial clinical consequences, including increased all-cause mortality (RR 1.36), functional disability (OR 3.03 in sarcopenia), and higher hospitalization rates (IRR 1.52), with marked reductions in quality of life in cachexia and sarcopenia across cancer, heart failure, and kidney disease contexts.

title: Mechanistic decoding of triclosan-induced endometriosis via network toxicology, Mendelian randomization, and molecular docking
summary:
# Summary

This study employs network toxicology, Mendelian randomization, and molecular docking to elucidate how triclosan, a widely used endocrine-disrupting chemical found in personal care products, may promote endometriosis development. Triclosan exhibits stable binding to four key hub proteins—IL1B, EGFR, SRC, and KDR—and activates IL1B and growth factor signaling pathways implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. IL1B emerges as a critical mediator, with Mendelian randomization analysis establishing a causal relationship between elevated IL1B and endometriosis risk; IL1B promotes stromal proliferation and COX-2-mediated prostaglandin synthesis while activating downstream MAPK and VEGF signaling cascades. The identified targets also drive endometrial cell proliferation (EGFR), angiogenesis-essential ectopic lesion growth (KDR and SRC), and cellular migration in early-stage lesions (SRC). Triclosan's additional mechanisms include endocrine disruption through estrogen receptor binding and inhibition of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, with bioaccumulation documented in urine, plasma, and reproductive tissues. Collectively, these findings reveal a mechanistic model wherein triclosan exposure promotes endometriosis through coordinated activation of inflammatory, growth factor, and angiogenic pathways, establishing environmental chemical exposure as a potential endometriosis risk factor.

title: Increased HSD11β1 Expression in Human Leiomyomatous Uteri: Implication for Enhanced Glucocorticoid Signaling
journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a pathological feedback loop in uterine leiomyomas driven by elevated HSD11B1 expression and enhanced glucocorticoid receptor (GR) signaling. FKBP51, which is elevated in leiomyomatous tissue, binds to and amplifies GR-mediated transcription, leading to increased HSD11B1 expression—an enzyme that locally produces active cortisol. This cortisol further activates GR, perpetuating a self-sustaining cycle that promotes the myofibroblast phenotype in leiomyoma cells through altered expression of extracellular matrix genes (decreased LAMA2 and FN1) and smooth muscle genes (increased CNN1). Silencing FKBP5 or its protein product FKBP51 substantially blunts glucocorticoid-induced HSD11B1 upregulation and reduces the transcriptional effects of both dexamethasone and the GR on downstream target genes. These findings establish the FKBP51-GR-HSD11B1 axis as a central mechanism in leiomyoma pathogenesis, suggesting potential therapeutic targets in this common gynecological disease.

title: CXCL10 Involvement in Vestibular Migraine via the PI3K/AKT Signaling Pathway
summary:
# Summary

CXCL10, a pain-related chemokine, is significantly elevated in vestibular migraine (VM) patients and correlates strongly with dizziness and headache severity as measured by clinical scores. The chemokine exerts its pathogenic effects by binding to CXCR3 receptor on microglial cells, astrocytes, and neurons, thereby activating the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway and promoting release of pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-α. In rodent VM models, CXCL10 and CXCR3 expression are elevated in the trigeminal nucleus caudalis and vestibular nuclei, key brain regions involved in migraine processing, accompanied by increased phosphorylation of PI3K and AKT proteins and elevated downstream inflammatory mediators in these same regions. Through enhanced neuronal inflammation and excitability, CXCL10 maintains neuropathic pain and vestibular symptoms, establishing the PI3K/AKT pathway as a central mechanism in VM pathogenesis. These findings suggest that CXCL10 and its associated signaling pathway represent potential therapeutic targets for vestibular migraine treatment.

title: Deciphering oligomeric proanthocyanidins’ dual osteoprotective mechanisms at single-cell resolution: NR4A1-mediated PTGS2 suppression and β-catenin-Runx2 activation
summary: Oligomeric proanthocyanidin (OPC), a natural polyphenolic flavonoid with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, demonstrates therapeutic efficacy against osteoporosis through dual mechanistic pathways identified at single-cell resolution using scRNA-seq. OPC inhibits the transcription factor NR4A1, which normally activates expression of PTGS2, thereby suppressing inflammatory prostaglandin signaling in mesenchymal stem cells. Simultaneously, OPC activates a β-catenin-dependent signaling axis involving NR4A1 and Runx2 that promotes osteogenic differentiation while inhibiting osteoclastogenesis, effectively balancing bone formation and resorption. Single-cell analysis revealed that C2 NR4A1+ MSCs, which display distinct metabolic features and communicate with osteoblasts via FGF7-FGFR1 signaling, are key mediators in osteoporosis pathogenesis. In animal models of estrogen-deficient osteoporosis induced by ovariectomy, OPC treatment significantly improved bone microstructure parameters and prevented bone mass loss. These findings establish OPC as a promising therapeutic candidate that targets specific cell populations to restore bone homeostasis through coordinated suppression of inflammatory pathways and activation of osteogenic differentiation programs.

title: Pro-endometriosis macrophage release of IL-33 is key for endometriosis pain and lesion formation
summary: This study reveals that IL-33 produced by pro-endometriosis macrophages is central to both endometriosis-associated pain and lesion development. Nociceptor-derived calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) activates macrophages through CGRP/RAMP1 signaling, driving these cells to produce IL-33, which then binds to ST2 receptors on endometrial epithelial cells to promote their proliferation and drive lesion growth. IL-33 also directly activates nociceptors, establishing a bidirectional neuro-immune feedback loop that amplifies pain signals. The clinical significance is underscored by findings that IL-33 expression in macrophages associates with endometriosis genetic risk variants and correlates with lesion fibrosis and glandular number, while therapeutic targeting of CGRP, RAMP1, or IL-33 itself effectively reduces pain behaviors, lesion burden, and IL-33 levels in mouse models. These results identify the nociceptor-macrophage-IL-33 axis as a therapeutic target and suggest that existing FDA-approved antimigraine drugs blocking CGRP or RAMP1 may provide dual benefits by reducing both pain and lesion progression in endometriosis patients.

title: A Drug Repurposing Strategy for a New Cause of Endometrial Infertility: Unveiling Promising New Treatments
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies molecular dysfunctions in endometrial stromal cells that impair decidualization—the essential process for embryo protection and nutrient supply—as a non-timing cause of infertility manifesting as implantation failure and miscarriage. Two distinct endometrial failure profiles emerged: a clinical miscarriage-associated (CMA) profile characterized by upregulated differentiation pathways and downregulated cellular respiration, and a biochemical miscarriage-associated (BMA) profile marked by immune dysregulation and heightened inflammatory responses. Through drug repurposing screening, five compounds were identified as therapeutic candidates: genistein and pioglitazone promote decidualization and increase IGFBP-1 secretion by inducing the morphological changes characteristic of healthy decidualized cells, while alprostadil, flunisolide, and tenoxicam suppress pathological immune responses by reducing inflammatory mediators including COX2 and TNF. These findings suggest that targeting defective decidualization through mechanism-specific drug interventions represents a promising therapeutic approach for treating endometrial-factor infertility.

title: Impact of vitamin D deficiency on defective endometrial decidualization and the repressive role of vitamin D receptor (VDR) in the epigenomic network
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that vitamin D receptor (VDR) plays a paradoxical dual role in endometrial decidualization, the critical process enabling embryo implantation and placental development. Vitamin D deficiency impairs decidualization in mice through reduced expression of key decidualization markers including Wnt4, Bmp2, prolactin, and IGFBP1, which are normally upregulated by the active vitamin D metabolite 1,25(OH)2D3 binding to VDR. Counterintuitively, VDR functions as a repressor of in vitro decidualization—VDR knockdown enhanced decidualization and increased prolactin and IGFBP1 production, while VDR overexpression inhibited these responses. VDR acts as a chromatin regulator, controlling chromatin accessibility and histone modifications (H3K4me3, H3K27ac, H3K27me3) to modulate expression of approximately 1,500 genes, with over 80% of differentially expressed genes localized to open chromatin regions. VDR ablation causes uterine hyperplasia and ovarian dysfunction in mice, while vitamin D deficiency is clinically associated with reduced fertility, implantation failure, and pregnancy complications. These findings reveal VDR's complex epigenomic regulation of decidualization, suggesting that both excessive and insufficient VDR activity may compromise reproductive outcomes.

title: Anti-Müllerian hormone ameliorates uterine DNA damage response and prevents pregnancy complications in doxorubicin-treated mice
summary: Doxorubicin chemotherapy induces extensive DNA damage in the uterine myometrium by activating the p53 pathway, leading to elevated expression of damage-response genes (Cdkn1a, Trp53, Bbc3, Ccng1, and others) and resulting in severe pregnancy complications including labor dystocia and reduced fertility in female cancer survivors. Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), produced by ovarian granulosa cells and acting through its receptor AMHR2 expressed in myometrial and endometrial stromal cells, ameliorates doxorubicin-induced uterine damage by suppressing p53 pathway activation and reducing accumulation of DNA double-strand breaks marked by γ-H2AX. AMH achieves this protective effect partly through activation of TGFβ signaling and upregulation of downstream targets including Id3, Smad6, and Cxcl14, while simultaneously suppressing pro-apoptotic genes (Bax, Ei24, Gadd45a, Tnfrs10b) in myometrial fibroblasts. Co-treatment with AMH significantly reduced doxorubicin-induced labor dystocia and prevented the pathological transcriptional remodeling characteristic of chemotherapy-damaged uterine tissue. These findings suggest AMH as a potential therapeutic strategy to preserve reproductive function and prevent pregnancy complications in female cancer survivors undergoing doxorubicin-based chemotherapy.

title: Molecular mechanisms of excitotoxicity and their relevance to the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases—an update
summary: Glutamate excitotoxicity, the pathological consequence of excessive glutamate accumulation, represents a critical mechanism in neurodegenerative diseases including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's disease, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Excessive glutamate release causes neuronal damage through activation of ionotropic and metabotropic glutamate receptors, leading to calcium dysregulation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative stress. The article emphasizes that both ionotropic glutamate receptors (particularly NMDARs and AMPARs) and metabotropic receptors (mGluRs) play distinct roles in excitotoxicity, with various regulatory proteins such as Homer 1a and β-arrestin 2 modulating these receptor signaling pathways. Emerging therapeutic strategies include soluble epoxide hydrolase inhibitors that enhance neuroprotective lipid mediators, repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation that upregulates glutamate transporters, and selective receptor modulation with agents like AMN082 targeting mGluR7 to reduce excitotoxic cell death. Additionally, interventions such as intermittent food deprivation enhance hippocampal synaptic plasticity through sirtuin 3 upregulation, offering complementary neuroprotective mechanisms. The article collectively presents multiple molecular targets and intervention strategies to counteract glutamate excitotoxicity in neurodegenerative disease pathogenesis.

title: S1P3
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that 2-arachidonoylglycerol (2-AG), an endocannabinoid elevated in endometriosis patients, promotes inflammation through a novel S1P3-dependent mechanism distinct from classical cannabinoid receptor signaling. The 2-AG-induced upregulation of sphingosine kinase 1 (SK1) and S1P3 receptor expression orchestrates a pro-inflammatory cascade characterized by increased production of cyclooxygenase-2, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, and other pro-inflammatory cytokines in endometriotic cells. Pharmacological blockade of S1P3 or its selective silencing effectively impairs this 2-AG-driven inflammatory response, implicating S1P3 as a critical mediator independent of direct SK1 activation or intracellular S1P elevation. These findings suggest that the endocannabinoid system contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis through an unexpected cross-talk between endocannabinoid and sphingosine-1-phosphate signaling axes, with S1P3 emerging as a potential therapeutic target for controlling inflammation in this disease.

title: Neutrophil Extracellular Traps in Sepsis and Sepsis-Related Organ Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

Infection triggers sepsis, a systemic inflammatory response that activates neutrophils and leads to multiple organ dysfunctions including acute lung injury and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs), web-like structures composed of DNA, histones, and granular enzymes, form through two main pathways: NOX-dependent NETosis, which is driven by reactive oxygen species generated via NOX complex activation by upstream signals including Rac2, ERK, and Akt; and NOX-independent NETosis, which is triggered by calcium influx, mitochondrial ROS, lactate production, and uric acid. While NETs initially function in immune defense by trapping pathogens through histones and DNA, they paradoxically amplify inflammation and cause tissue damage through multiple mechanisms: they promote platelet aggregation and thrombosis, compromise alveolar epithelial cell junctions leading to pulmonary edema and acute lung injury, and trigger cytotoxicity through their protease components. This article demonstrates that NET formation is a critical pathophysiological mechanism in sepsis-related organ dysfunction, where the same immune structures that combat infection simultaneously contribute to severe complications including shock and multi-organ failure.

title: Regulation of murine follicle-stimulating hormone β subunit transcription by newly identified enhancers
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the transcriptional mechanisms controlling follicle-stimulating hormone beta (FSHβ) subunit expression, identifying multiple enhancer regions that regulate FSH production in pituitary gonadotrope cells. Activin-class ligands activate FSH synthesis through a canonical signaling pathway in which they bind activin type II receptors, leading to phosphorylation of SMAD2/3 proteins that subsequently form a complex with SMAD4 and the transcription factor FOXL2, which collectively bind to cis-regulatory elements in the Fshb promoter and enhancers. Using chromatin accessibility analysis, the researchers identified four open chromatin regions associated with the Fshb locus, with enhancers 3 and 4 (Enh3 and Enh4) demonstrating the strongest capacity to enhance basal and activin A-stimulated promoter activity through SMAD2/3 and FOXL2 binding. Genetic knockout studies confirmed that SMAD3, SMAD4, and FOXL2 are essential for FSHβ expression in mice and humans, and this regulatory mechanism is conserved across species, with human SNPs within these enhancer regions associated with reduced FSH levels in women. Although enhancer deletions did not significantly alter circulating FSH levels in adult mice, the identified enhancers represent key regulatory elements controlling the transcriptional capacity for FSH synthesis.

title: Structural insights into selective and dual antagonism of EP2 and EP4 prostaglandin receptors
summary:
# Summary

Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) regulates inflammation, pain, and cancer progression through binding to four G protein-coupled receptors, with EP2 and EP4 playing particularly important roles in pathological conditions. EP2 and EP4 activation increases cAMP production via adenylyl cyclase and can engage additional signaling pathways including β-catenin and PI3K, whereas EP1 and EP3 trigger distinct mechanisms involving calcium influx and cAMP inhibition respectively. PGE2-EP2/EP4 signaling promotes an immunosuppressed tumor microenvironment by boosting inflammation and angiogenesis through NF-κB, recruiting regulatory T cells, and inhibiting natural killer cell activity and CD8+ T cell-mediated immunity. The study provides structural insights into three antagonists—the selective EP2 antagonist PF-04418948, the selective EP4 antagonist grapiprant, and the dual EP2/EP4 antagonist TG6-129—revealing their distinct binding modes and key residues involved in receptor recognition. PF-04418948 and grapiprant show therapeutic potential for endometriosis and osteoarthritis-associated pain respectively, while structural characterization of these antagonists enables rational drug design for targeting PGE2 signaling in inflammation-associated diseases. The differential tissue distribution and functional redundancy between EP2 and EP4, combined with the availability of selective and dual antagonists, provide multiple therapeutic strategies for modulating PGE2 signaling.

title: Therapeutic potential of common Phytoestrogens found in traditional Chinese medicine in chronic kidney diseases
summary:
# Summary

Phytoestrogens from traditional Chinese medicine, including compounds like baicalin and puerarin, show therapeutic potential for chronic kidney disease by targeting multiple pathological mechanisms. These plant-derived estrogen mimics reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines, oxidative stress, and fibrosis pathways in renal tissue by binding to classical estrogen receptors (ERα and ERβ) and G protein-coupled estrogen receptors (GPER), as well as activating non-receptor pathways such as PI3K/Akt signaling. In chronic kidney disease, excessive reactive oxygen species production drives inflammatory and pro-fibrotic cascades, with pro-inflammatory mediators like IL-6 and TNF-α promoting both epithelial-mesenchymal transition and irreversible fibrosis through TGF-β-mediated pathways that suppress extracellular matrix degradation. Estrogen signaling protects kidney function by regulating extracellular matrix metabolism, nitric oxide levels, and renin-angiotensin system activity, while phytoestrogens enhance these protective effects by upregulating antioxidant protein genes and suppressing NF-κB and MAPK inflammatory mediators. The tissue-specific expression of estrogen receptors in glomeruli, renal tubules, and throughout the renal system positions phytoestrogens as promising candidates for slowing CKD progression and reducing associated cardiovascular complications.

title: Understanding interspecies drug response variations between human and rodent P2X7 receptors
summary: P2X7 receptors play a critical role in multiple pathological conditions including pain, inflammation, depression, rheumatoid arthritis, and tumor progression, making them important therapeutic targets. The study reveals substantial interspecies variations in drug responsiveness between human and rodent P2X7 receptors, with compounds like A740003 and AZ10606120 showing 37-fold and up to 1587-fold differences in inhibitory potency between human and mouse/rat variants. These species differences are structurally mapped to a deep portal-of-central-pocket (PCP1) motif containing residues V312, Y295, M105, F103, and P96, which determines selective ligand binding and efficacy. Novel inhibitors PSFL1191 and JNJ-54175446 demonstrate improved selectivity profiles, with JNJ-54175446 showing comparable affinity for both human and rat P2X7 receptors and clinical promise for treating major depression. The findings indicate that P2X4 antagonists like BX430 also show promise for endometriosis treatment, while structural mutagenesis studies validate the PCP1 pocket as the primary determinant of species-selective drug binding and efficacy.

title: Neuroimmune modulatory effects of the NLRP3 inflammasome: the role of sex and living environment in brain affecting conditions
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome functions as a master regulator of neuroinflammation, linking systemic perturbations to brain dysfunction through a well-characterized two-stage activation mechanism. NLRP3 activation is triggered by cellular stressors including mitochondrial dysfunction, reactive oxygen species production, potassium efflux, and lysosomal rupture, which initiate a cascade involving NF-κB-dependent priming and subsequent assembly with ASC and pro-caspase-1, ultimately leading to caspase-1-mediated maturation of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, pyroptotic cell death via GSDMD, and neuroinflammatory signaling. Dysregulation of NLRP3 contributes to multiple neurological conditions including depression, Alzheimer's disease, neuropathic pain, and cognitive decline, while its sensitivity to biological sex—mediated by fluctuating ovarian steroids and androgens that shape microglial phenotype—and environmental factors such as physical activity and psychosocial experiences creates sex-based disparities in disease risk and treatment efficacy. Critical developmental windows including puberty and aging act as reset points for inflammatory set-points, establishing foundational NLRP3 sensitivity that influences lifelong brain development, neuroplasticity, and vulnerability to neuropsychiatric disorders. The NLRP3 inflammasome thus represents an emerging biomarker and therapeutic target for understanding how sex and environmental context shape individual susceptibility to brain-affecting conditions with neurological repercussions.

title: Nanotechnology-based targeted regulation of NLRP3 Inflammasome: therapeutic strategies and clinical application prospects in inflammatory diseases
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome is a cytoplasmic multiprotein complex critically involved in initiating inflammatory diseases through a two-signal activation mechanism: an initial priming signal via pattern recognition receptors (PAMPs/DAMPs binding to TLRs) activates NF-κB to upregulate NLRP3 and pro-IL-1β, followed by a second signal triggered by ionic flux changes, mitochondrial dysfunction, and reactive oxygen species generation. Upon activation, NLRP3 recruits ASC and pro-caspase-1 to form the inflammasome, which cleaves pro-IL-1β, pro-IL-18, and GSDMD to release pro-inflammatory cytokines and induce pyroptotic cell death, thereby driving inflammatory disorders, autoimmune diseases, and metabolic disorders. Nanotechnology-based approaches offer promising therapeutic strategies by simultaneously inhibiting NLRP3 inflammasome activation through multiple mechanisms—reactive oxygen species scavenging, calcium ion flux regulation, and mitochondrial function stabilization—while enhancing the stability and tissue penetration of traditional NLRP3 inhibitors. Nanomaterials and nanoparticles have demonstrated efficacy in treating inflammatory diseases including atherosclerosis, diabetes, and neurodegenerative conditions, positioning nanotechnology as an innovative platform for targeted regulation of inflammasome signaling in clinical applications.

title: Extracellular matrices regulate extravasation journey of leukocytes and inflammatory tissue fate
summary:
# Summary

Extracellular matrix components and endothelial junctions critically regulate leukocyte extravasation during inflammation through interconnected mechanical and biochemical mechanisms. Cytokines activate local blood vessels and promote leukocyte infiltration, while laminar shear stress stimulates endothelial secretion of PDGF-B to recruit pericytes that stabilize vessels through Ang1/Tie2 signaling. The basement membrane and its laminin composition determine preferential sites for leukocyte diapedesis: laminin-511-rich regions strengthen junctions through RhoA/ROCK-mediated VE-cadherin stabilization, whereas laminin-poor patches permit easier transendothelial migration. During leukocyte transmigration, PECAM-1 engagement triggers calcium signaling cascades that activate actomyosin machinery and deposit lateral border recycling compartments at the junction, locally reducing VE-cadherin density and loosening adherens junctions. Mechanical forces on VE-cadherin expose Y731 for SHP2-mediated dephosphorylation and promote VE-cadherin endocytosis, facilitating paracellular passage, while the tension-sensitive Piezo-1 channel provides an additional calcium influx mechanism. Notably, laminin-α4-deficient mice show compensatory laminin-α5 upregulation that eliminates low-expression patches in the basement membrane, resulting in reduced leukocyte infiltration and altered inflammatory tissue outcomes, demonstrating that matrix heterogeneity actively controls inflammation through spatial control of vascular permeability.

title: The role of relaxins in blood cell modulation: interactions with relaxin family peptide receptor 1 (RXFP1) and glucocorticoid receptor (GR)
summary: Relaxin-2 functions as a pleiotropic hormone with potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties that operates through dual mechanisms involving RXFP1 and glucocorticoid receptor (GR) signaling. Through RXFP1, a G protein-coupled receptor, relaxin-2 activates multiple intracellular pathways including cAMP/PKA, PI3K/Akt, and ERK1/2 signaling, leading to vasodilation and inhibition of fibrotic responses. Remarkably, relaxin-2 also functions as a GR agonist, binding to the GR complex and promoting its nuclear translocation to suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) and transcription factors NF-κB and AP-1. At the cellular level, relaxin-2 modulates blood cell populations by inhibiting megakaryocytes, thrombocytes, and mast cells while elevating neutrophils and reducing lymphocytes, and regulates dendritic cell maturation. In vivo studies demonstrate that relaxin-2 ameliorates TNF-α-induced endothelial dysfunction and acute pancreatitis through reduction of pro-inflammatory mediators, suggesting therapeutic potential for inflammatory conditions. Additionally, elevated RXFP1 expression correlates with hematologic malignancies including acute and chronic myeloid leukemia, raising important considerations for therapeutic applications.

title: Microscaled Cell Surface Proteomics for Cryo-Preserved Cells and Tissue Samples
summary:
# Summary

This study presents optimized microscale cell surface proteomics workflows applicable to cryopreserved tissue samples, directly relevant to understanding cellular signaling in endometrial conditions. Two complementary methodologies were developed and validated: an N-glycopeptide capture method that uses sodium metaperiodate oxidation followed by biotin labeling and streptavidin enrichment, and a wheat germ agglutinin-horseradish peroxidase (WGA-HRP) method that employs tyramide-based amplification for labeling. Together, these approaches identified over 700 cell surface proteins from minimal starting material (1-2 million cells), with cryopreservation maintaining high reproducibility and protein detection integrity. The study validated the methods by detecting dynamic epidermal growth factor receptor internalization upon EGF stimulation, which could be blocked by chloroquine pretreatment, demonstrating the workflows' capability to track receptor-mediated signaling events. The optimized enzymatic dissociation protocols enable accurate surface protein profiling from healthy endometrial tissue, providing a foundation for investigating cell surface proteomes in endometrial pathologies including endometriosis.

title: GPR75 signaling is dispensable for reproduction but contributes to feeding and body growth in rats on normal chow and is involved in high-fat diet-induced hyperphagia, obesity, and hyperglycemia development
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that GPR75 signaling, highly expressed in hypothalamic and other brain regions, plays a critical role in regulating feeding behavior and body weight but is dispensable for normal reproductive function in rats. GPR75 knockout rats exhibited significantly reduced food intake, body weight, and feeding duration on both normal and high-fat diets, while maintaining normal puberty onset, luteinizing hormone secretion, and fertility comparable to wild-type controls. In high-fat diet-fed knockouts, GPR75 loss-of-function prevented obesity, hyperphagia, and associated metabolic dysfunctions including hyperglycemia and hyperinsulinemia, alongside reduced plasma cholesterol and triglyceride levels. The protective effects appear mediated through upregulation of the hypothalamic chemokine CCL5, which promotes satiety and reduces food intake through its signaling pathways. These findings identify GPR75 as a promising therapeutic target for obesity and metabolic disease without compromising reproductive capacity, with potential clinical relevance given that obesity itself is associated with reduced fertility and increased endometriosis risk in women.

title: Dual role of complement in neuronal repair
summary:
# Summary

The complement system exhibits a dual role in neuronal repair, functioning as both a detrimental driver of neuroinflammation and a beneficial promoter of neural recovery. Complement activation through classical and alternative pathways drives neuroinflammation, apoptosis, and pathological autophagy via receptors C5aR1 and C3aR, which also interact with NLRP3 and NLRP1 inflammasomes to amplify pro-inflammatory signaling and pain responses. Conversely, complement components C5a and C3 promote neuroprotective mechanisms including phagocyte recruitment for debris clearance, nerve growth factor and brain-derived neurotrophic factor secretion, and axonal growth through C5aR-dependent signaling that connects to the mTOR regenerative pathway. The intracellular complement system (complosome) further regulates cellular metabolism, survival, and oxidative stress responses while interacting critically with NLRP3 inflammasome signaling. Dysregulation of complement components—particularly excessive C3-mediated synaptic stripping, microglial activation, and neuroinflammation—underlies pathological synapse loss in neurodegenerative conditions, whereas selective complement inhibition, such as CD59 upregulation and C3aR1 blockade, provides neuroprotection and pain reduction. This dichotomy highlights complement as a pivotal node in nervous system pathophysiology where spatial-temporal regulation determines whether it promotes healing or exacerbates neuronal damage.

title: Discovery and optimization of a covalent AKR1C3 inhibitor
summary:
# Summary

AKR1C3 is a metabolic enzyme that catalyzes the reduction of androgens, estrogens, and prostaglandins, producing potent bioactive compounds including dihydrotestosterone, which binds the androgen receptor and drives proliferation in hormone-dependent cancers including prostate cancer. Resistance to standard androgen receptor signaling inhibitors frequently involves upregulation of AKR1C3 alongside the constitutively active AR-V7 variant, creating a significant clinical challenge.
