This study reports the discovery and optimization of RJG-2051, a covalent AKR1C3 inhibitor based on the sulfonyl-triazole chemistry platform that selectively engages tyrosine 24 within the AKR1C3 active site with potent inhibitory activity (IC50 = 13 nM) and remarkable selectivity over related AKR enzymes (>1790-fold selectivity over AKR1C1/C2). Comprehensive proteome-wide profiling demonstrated that RJG-2051 exhibits negligible off-target binding across over 1800 tyrosine and lysine sites, indicating excellent selectivity. The compound blocks AKR1C3-mediated metabolic conversion of substrates and shows promise for addressing castration-resistant prostate cancer, with potential applications extending to endometriosis where AKR1C3 inhibition is also being evaluated clinically.

title: Hyperinflammation by Human Macrophages Induced by SARS‐CoV‐2 Anti‐Spike IgG Is Dependent on Glucose and Fatty Acid Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

Anti-spike IgG immune complexes from severely ill COVID-19 patients activate Fc gamma receptors on human alveolar macrophages, triggering rapid metabolic reprogramming characterized by increased glycolysis, fatty acid synthesis, and pentose phosphate pathway activation—all essential for the excessive inflammatory response observed in severe disease. This metabolic shift is driven by increased glucose uptake (GLUT1 upregulation) and NADPH production, which fuel de novo fatty acid synthesis and subsequently activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, promoting robust proinflammatory cytokine production including IL-6. Pharmacological blockade of glycolysis, the pentose phosphate pathway, or fatty acid synthesis each substantially reduces anti-spike IgG-induced IL-6 production, demonstrating that these metabolic pathways are critical dependencies for hyperinflammation. Notably, increased glycolytic activity in macrophages correlates with poor prognosis in severe COVID-19 patients, and glucose metabolism inhibition has shown clinical benefit in moderate-to-severe disease trials. The study identifies metabolic reprogramming as a targetable mechanism underlying antibody-dependent macrophage hyperinflammation, offering potential therapeutic avenues for controlling the cytokine storm characteristic of severe COVID-19.

title: GPR30 in spinal cholecystokinin-positive neurons modulates neuropathic pain
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies GPR30, a membrane estrogen receptor, as a critical modulator of neuropathic pain when expressed in spinal cholecystokinin-positive (CCK+) neurons of the dorsal horn. GPR30 expression is significantly upregulated in the spinal dorsal horn following chronic constriction injury and enhances AMPA-mediated excitatory synaptic transmission in CCK+ interneurons that receive direct projections from the primary sensory cortex. Pharmacological antagonism of GPR30 using G-15 effectively attenuated both mechanical allodynia and thermal hyperalgesia in neuropathic pain models, while selective GPR30 agonist G-1 exacerbated pain symptoms and increased neuronal activity markers. Notably, the pain-modulatory effects of spinal GPR30 operate independently of sex, with knockdown of Gper1 (the gene encoding GPR30) in either CCK+ neurons or their cortical presynaptic partners substantially reversing neuropathic pain behaviors. These findings establish GPR30 as a key node in a specific pain-processing circuit linking the sensory cortex to spinal dorsal horn interneurons and suggest GPR30 antagonism as a potential therapeutic approach for neuropathic pain management.

title: Estrogen-Mediated Regulation of Fam3d in Mouse Uterus During the Estrous Cycle
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Fam3d expression in the mouse uterus is dynamically regulated by estrogen through estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) signaling during the estrous cycle. Fam3d mRNA and protein levels are significantly elevated during the proestrus and estrus stages when estrogen levels peak, but decrease during metestrus and diestrus. Estradiol administration to ovariectomized mice induced maximal Fam3d expression at 24 hours, and this induction was blocked by ERα antagonist treatment, confirming ERα-mediated regulation. FAM3D protein is exclusively localized to the cytoplasm of luminal and glandular epithelial cells, particularly in the apical region, but is absent from stromal tissue. Beyond its uterine expression, FAM3D functions as a formyl peptide receptor agonist for FPR1 and FPR2, and plays roles in homeostasis, inflammation protection, glucose metabolism, and blood pressure regulation. These findings suggest that estrogen-regulated Fam3d may contribute to uterine immune homeostasis and reproductive function through formyl peptide receptor signaling pathways.

title: The Levonorgestrel Intrauterine System Attenuates the Expression of Angiopoietin-1, Angiopoietin-2, and Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor in Adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is characterized by aberrant endometrial invasion and abnormal angiogenesis, involving upregulation of key vascular growth factors including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), angiopoietin-1 (ANGPT-1), and angiopoietin-2 (ANGPT-2) in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissues. These angiogenic factors promote pathological neovascularization through complementary mechanisms: VEGF stimulates endothelial cell growth and vascular permeability, while ANGPT-1 stabilizes vessels and ANGPT-2 destabilizes them to facilitate vascular remodeling. Treatment with the levonorgestrel intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) significantly reduced protein and mRNA expression of all three angiogenic markers in both eutopic and ectopic tissues, correlating with clinical improvements in pain, bleeding, and anemia. These findings demonstrate that the LNG-IUS exerts therapeutic effects in adenomyosis through suppression of aberrant angiogenic signaling, suggesting that targeting angiogenesis may be a viable approach for managing this condition and its associated morbidities.

title: Estimation of plasma afamin levels in patients with periodontitis and Type II diabetes mellitus: A clinico-biochemical study
journal: Journal of Indian Society of Periodontology
summary:
# Summary

This clinico-biochemical study demonstrates that plasma afamin (AFM) levels are significantly elevated in patients with periodontitis, particularly when combined with Type II diabetes mellitus, serving as a biomarker for oxidative stress and metabolic dysfunction. AFM, a vitamin E-binding glycoprotein synthesized in the liver, becomes elevated when vitamin E is depleted by excessive reactive oxygen species, which are overproduced in both periodontitis and Type II diabetes through AGE-RAGE interactions and inflammatory signaling cascades involving TNF-α, IL-6, and NF-κB activation. The study revealed that deep periodontal pockets and poor glycemic control correlate strongly with increased AFM concentrations, and that oxidative stress mediates the bidirectional relationship between periodontitis and Type II diabetes. The findings extend beyond periodontitis, identifying AFM as a common biomarker elevated in metabolic disorders including endometriosis, gestational diabetes, and polycystic ovary syndrome, where it associates with insulin resistance and compromised antioxidant capacity. These results suggest AFM can be utilized clinically as a marker to identify individuals at higher risk for metabolic disease progression and to monitor the severity of oxidative stress-mediated inflammatory conditions.

title: Potential endogenous lipid ligands for the nuclear receptor transcription factor Steroidogenic Factor-1
summary:
# Summary

Steroidogenic Factor-1 (SF-1) is a master nuclear receptor transcription factor essential for development and function of steroidogenic tissues, but its aberrant activation is linked to endometriosis and adrenocortical carcinoma. Recent evidence reveals that SF-1 regulation depends on endogenous lipid ligands rather than traditional steroid hormones, with multiple phospholipid species including phosphatidylcholine, phosphatidylethanolamine, and phosphoinositides (particularly PIP3 and PIP2) binding directly to SF-1's unusually large ligand pocket and stabilizing its structure to enhance transcriptional activity. Among sphingolipids, sphingosine and lyso-sphingomyelin associate with SF-1, though sphingosine acts as a repressor by inhibiting co-activator recruitment and reducing steroidogenic gene expression including CYP17A1. The acyl chain composition of phosphoinositides critically determines their functional effects on SF-1; stearoyl-arachidonoyl-phosphoinositides, representing 70% of mammalian phosphoinositides, bind with highest affinity and likely represent the primary physiological ligands regulating SF-1-mediated transcription in nuclear signaling pathways.

title: CGRP restrains CD4+ T cell responses and allergic sensitization
summary:
# Summary

Calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), a neuropeptide released by sensory neurons, restrains adaptive immune responses through its receptor complex RAMP1-CALCRL expressed on CD4+ T cells and multiple immune cell populations. The CGRP-RAMP1 pathway suppresses CD4+ T cell activation, differentiation, and the generation of T follicular helper (Tfh) cells by reshaping chromatin accessibility and downregulating Tfh-associated genes including IL-21 and ICOS. CGRP+ sensory nerve fibers densely innervate mediastinal lymph nodes, but their density decreases following allergen sensitization; CD4+ T cell-specific RAMP1 deficiency promotes Tfh accumulation and B cell activation, exacerbating allergic responses. Additionally, CGRP inhibits innate lymphoid cell expansion and IL-13 production through the same receptor complex. Exogenous CGRP treatment mitigates allergic sensitization and effectively alleviates allergic asthma in a RAMP1-dependent manner, suggesting the CGRP-RAMP1 pathway as a therapeutic target for allergic diseases.

title: Estrogen receptor α expression in tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes from patients with endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study examines estrogen receptor α (ERα) expression in tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) from endometrial cancer patients and reveals a complex immunomodulatory role for estrogen signaling in cancer progression. Estradiol activates ERα signaling in TILs, which regulates immune cell function and shifts the T cell landscape toward increased helper T cells and decreased cytotoxic T lymphocytes in endometrial cancer tissues. Early in tumor development, IL-17A-producing macrophages drive ERα upregulation on TILs, increasing local estrogen sensitivity; however, as cancer advances, ERα expression paradoxically declines and loss of ERα expression correlates with advanced disease stage, lymph node involvement, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Notably, ERα+ B cells are enriched in high-grade and mismatch repair-deficient tumors, suggesting a link between ERα+ B cell accumulation and aggressive disease phenotypes. Additionally, estradiol-ERα interactions at the FOXP3 locus enhance regulatory T cell suppressive function, while ERα expression shows inverse correlation with body mass index across TIL subsets, indicating that obesity may modulate estrogen-mediated immune dysfunction in endometrial cancer.

title: Schwann cell Lrp1 deletion drives trigeminal neuron sensitization and orofacial pain by modulating mitochondrial function and TRPV1/TRPA1 activity
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: Schwann cell LRP1 deletion drives orofacial pain through a cascade of metabolic and molecular dysfunction centered on impaired mitochondrial homeostasis and oxidative stress. Loss of Lrp1 in Schwann cells prevents proper internalization of oxidized LDL (oxLDL), resulting in excessive hydrogen peroxide accumulation and elevated reactive carbonyl groups that activate pain-sensing ion channels TRPV1 and TRPA1 on trigeminal neurons. Consequently, trigeminal ganglia from mice with Schwann cell-specific Lrp1 deletion exhibit upregulation of all major mitochondrial electron transport chain complexes alongside maladaptive increases in maximal respiration, proton leak, and spare respiratory capacity, indicating dysregulated mitochondrial function. The heightened expression and sensitization of TRPV1 and TRPA1, coupled with upregulation of calbindin 2 (a calcium-modulating protein), render trigeminal neurons hyperexcitable and produce pronounced mechanical and thermal hypersensitivity alongside orofacial pain and anxiety-like behavior. Notably, conditioned medium from LRP1-deficient Schwann cells is sufficient to activate wild-type trigeminal neurons and induce hypersensitivity in vivo, demonstrating that secreted factors from dysfunctional Schwann cells directly promote neuronal sensitization. These findings establish LRP1 as a critical regulator of Schwann cell-neuron metabolic coupling and identify oxidative stress-mediated activation of nociceptive ion channels as a key mechanism linking peripheral glia dysfunction to orofacial pain pathogenesis.

title: Targeting ICAM1 through LIF-mediated NF-κB/JAK2-STAT3 crosstalk protects nucleus pulposus from IL-1β-driven pyroptosis to ameliorate intervertebral disc degeneration
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic Surgery and Research
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) protects nucleus pulposus cells from IL-1β-induced pyroptosis and intervertebral disc degeneration by downregulating ICAM1 expression through inhibition of NF-κB and JAK2/STAT3 signaling pathways. LIF expression exhibits a temporal pattern in disc degeneration, peaking in mildly degenerated tissues but significantly declining in severely degenerated discs, whereas ICAM1 and pyroptosis-related proteins including gasdermin family members and caspase-1 progressively increase with degeneration severity. Mechanistically, LIF suppresses the NLRP3 inflammasome activation and pyroptotic cascade by blocking both NF-κB and JAK2/STAT3 pathways, which normally induce ICAM1 expression and downstream inflammatory responses. Conversely, LIF knockdown increases pyroptosis-related protein expression, while LIF overexpression reduces NLRP3, GSDMD, GSDMDN, ASC, and caspase-1 levels in IL-1β-stimulated cells. These findings identify LIF as a potential therapeutic target for preventing pyroptosis-driven intervertebral disc degeneration through a novel NF-κB/JAK2-STAT3 crosstalk mechanism involving ICAM1 regulation.

title: The role of Akkermansia muciniphila in the regulation of inflammatory bowel disease: intestinal immunity and metabolism
summary:
# Summary

Akkermansia muciniphila, a commensal bacterium residing in the intestinal mucus layer, plays a crucial protective role in inflammatory bowel disease through multiple immunological mechanisms centered on inflammasome signaling and pattern recognition receptor activation. The bacterium and its bioactive components, including the outer membrane protein Amuc_1100 and lipooligosaccharide, interact with TLR2 and TLR4 to activate downstream signaling cascades involving NF-κB and MAPK/PI3K pathways, ultimately promoting anti-inflammatory IL-10 production and regulatory T cell differentiation. A. muciniphila-mediated NLRP6 inflammasome activation in intestinal epithelial cells maintains the secretory function of Paneth and goblet cells, thereby strengthening the mucosal barrier and preventing colitis, while its metabolic activity in mucin degradation generates short-chain fatty acids that further support intestinal health. Additionally, specific bacterial secretions such as Amuc_2109 inhibit NLRP3 inflammasome activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine production, while suppressing HDAC5-mediated epigenetic modifications to promote anti-inflammatory macrophage responses. The therapeutic efficacy of A. muciniphila supplementation is dependent on functional TLR4 signaling and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, as demonstrated by the loss of protective effects in knockout mice, establishing inflammasome-dependent mechanisms as central to this bacterium's ability to ameliorate intestinal inflammation and prevent disease progression to colorectal cancer.

title: IL-37/IL-1R8 axis: a novel major mechanism of control at the interface between tumor and immune cells
summary: IL-37, a unique anti-inflammatory member of the IL-1 superfamily, exerts dual mechanisms to suppress inflammation and tumor progression through both intracellular and extracellular pathways. Intracellularly, IL-37 acts as a nuclear transcription factor and forms complexes with phosphorylated Smad-3 to upregulate protein tyrosine phosphatase genes, which inhibit pro-inflammatory signaling cascades including p38 MAPK, ERK, and JNK. Extracellularly, IL-37 binds IL-18 receptor alpha and recruits the inhibitory co-receptor IL-1R8, which suppresses IL-1R/TLR signaling and downstream NF-κB/JNK activation, thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine production. Additionally, IL-37 directly inhibits tumor cell proliferation and migration while promoting anti-angiogenic and immunoregulatory mechanisms within the tumor microenvironment. IL-37 is produced by multiple immune cell types including monocytes, dendritic cells, macrophages, and T cells, with its expression upregulated by pro-inflammatory triggers such as IL-18, IL-1β, TNF-α, and TGF-β1, establishing a negative feedback loop that controls excessive inflammation. These findings position the IL-37/IL-1R8 axis as a pivotal regulatory mechanism controlling immune-tumor cell interactions and suggest therapeutic potential for enhancing this pathway in inflammatory and oncologic conditions.

title: Media Matters: Phenol Red and Fetal Bovine Serum Estrogen in Traditional Cell Culture Media Influence Human Mesenchymal Stromal Cell (hMSC) Processes and Differentiation in a Sex-Biased Manner
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that traditional cell culture media components—phenol red and estrogen in fetal bovine serum—exert sex-biased effects on human mesenchymal stromal cell (hMSC) behavior and differentiation. Phenol red, acting as an estrogen-mimetic compound, substantially drives cell proliferation with notably stronger effects in female hMSCs compared to male cells, while also suppressing osteogenic differentiation and promoting adipogenic differentiation in a sex-dependent manner. Conversely, 17β-estradiol supplementation inhibits female hMSC growth and osteogenesis through downregulation of the osteogenic transcription factor RUNX2, whereas it upregulates estrogen receptor alpha and collagen expression more prominently in male cells. Unfiltered fetal bovine serum containing endogenous estrogen increases senescence particularly in male donors and diminishes metabolic activity and osteogenic potential in both sexes. These findings reveal critical interactions between donor sex, estrogen, and phenol red that significantly impact hMSC proliferation, metabolic activity, and lineage-specific differentiation, highlighting the importance of standardizing culture media composition in cell therapy and regenerative medicine research to reduce experimental variability.

title: Targeting CD44-Hyaluronic Acid Signalling in Obesity Treatment: Insights from Small Molecules and Nanobioconjugates
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the CD44-hyaluronic acid (HA) signaling axis as a therapeutic target in obesity, demonstrating how dysregulation of this pathway contributes to metabolic disease. In obesity, the CD44-HA interaction becomes pathologically activated, promoting adipose tissue hypertrophy, macrophage infiltration, and inflammatory signaling through nuclear factor-kappa B and mitogen-activated protein kinase pathways. This dysregulation triggers a cascade of metabolic complications including chronic low-grade inflammation, insulin resistance, and the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α and IL-6, which collectively drive systemic dysfunction and increase cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk. The article proposes that targeting CD44-HA signaling through small-molecule inhibitors like 4-methylumbelliferone and engineered nanobioconjugates offers a promising therapeutic strategy to interrupt this pathological pathway and mitigate obesity-associated complications. These interventions aim to restore normal adipose tissue function and reduce the pathologic immune cell infiltration and inflammatory responses characteristic of obese adipose tissue.

title: Integration of cAMP and TRPV4 Signaling to Optimize Collagen Remodeling for Management of Fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

This article presents a bimodal therapeutic strategy for managing tissue fibrosis by coordinating two complementary signaling pathways: activation of Gαs GPCRs and inhibition of TRPV4. Gαs GPCR activation generates cAMP, which suppresses fibrotic processes by enhancing intracellular collagen degradation through pathways involving β1 integrins, matrix metalloproteinases, and lysosomal hydrolases. Conversely, dysregulated TRPV4 signaling promotes myofibroblast differentiation and pro-fibrotic gene expression, establishing a pathological feedback loop that exacerbates extracellular collagen accumulation. The article identifies calcium influx through TRPV4 as a critical regulator of cAMP levels, suggesting that TRPV4 inhibition can potentiate cAMP-dependent anti-fibrotic responses. By simultaneously elevating cAMP signaling while suppressing TRPV4-mediated calcium-dependent pathways, this integrated approach optimizes collagen remodeling and prevents the excessive fibrillar collagen accumulation characteristic of fibrotic diseases including idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and cardiac hypertrophy.

title: Advances, Mechanisms, and Clinical Perspectives for the In Vitro Maturation of Human Oocytes
summary:
# Summary: In Vitro Maturation of Human Oocytes

This review examines the molecular mechanisms controlling oocyte maturation, a critical process for assisted reproductive technologies. Oocyte meiotic arrest is maintained by cyclic nucleotides (cAMP and cGMP) regulated through the NPPC/NPR2 signaling axis, which is suppressed following the luteinizing hormone (LH) surge. LH triggers meiotic resumption by downregulating natriuretic peptide precursor C, promoting NPR2 dephosphorylation, and activating phosphodiesterases (PDE1 and PDE5) that degrade cGMP, thereby relieving inhibition of cAMP degradation by PDE3A. Meiotic progression is then driven by maturation-promoting factor (MPF/CDK1-cyclin B) activation through Cdc25b phosphatase, with additional regulation by EGF-like growth factors (amphiregulin, epiregulin) and pathways including MAPK and GSK3 that coordinate meiotic spindle formation and cell polarity. Cytoplasmic maturation requires metabolic support from cumulus cells through gap junctions, involving glucose metabolism, pyruvate oxidation, fatty acid β-oxidation, and antioxidant accumulation like glutathione to ensure oocyte quality. The review highlights that successful in vitro maturation depends on replicating these complex, interconnected signaling and metabolic networks that naturally govern oocyte development.

title: Iron, the Essential Micronutrient: A Comprehensive Review of Regulatory Pathways of Iron Metabolism
summary:
# Summary

Hepcidin, a liver-derived peptide hormone encoded by the HAMP gene, serves as the master regulator of systemic iron homeostasis by binding ferroportin to reduce iron absorption in the intestines and iron release from macrophages. Hepcidin expression is tightly controlled by multiple signaling pathways including IL-6/JAK-STAT, BMP/SMAD, and MAPK, which are activated in response to iron overload, inflammation, and other physiological signals; conversely, iron deficiency and hypoxic conditions suppress hepcidin synthesis. Iron accumulation, facilitated by chronic inflammation, causes oxidative damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cell death, while both iron deficiency and overload increase infertility risk, with iron being essential for embryo implantation. The regulatory network involves complex crosstalk between inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) and transcription factors (STAT3, SMAD1/5/9, NFκB, C/EBPα) that enhance HAMP transcription, balanced by negative regulators like HIF1α; additional modulators including insulin, estrogen, and erythroferrone fine-tune hepcidin expression to maintain homeostasis.

title: Genotype–phenotype interaction in Alzheimer's disease immune activation
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how genetic predisposition for immune activation influences Alzheimer's disease progression through altered cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers of neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration. Higher polygenic risk scores for immune activation (AD PRSINFL) were associated with reduced levels of neuroprotective factors including sTREM2, clusterin, and fractalkine, particularly in individuals with elevated tau pathology. The TREM2 receptor on microglia normally suppresses innate immune activation genes while promoting phagocytosis of amyloid-beta, but genetic factors predisposing to immune activation appear to counteract this protective mechanism. Tau pathology emerged as a key modifier of these relationships, with higher tau concentrations strengthening negative associations between immune genetic risk and protective biomarkers. These findings suggest that genetic predisposition for immune activation may drive unfavorable microglial responses in early AD by reducing neuroprotective signaling, ultimately contributing to cognitive decline and neurodegeneration. The results highlight critical genotype–phenotype interactions wherein genetic risk factors interact with ongoing neuropathology to influence disease trajectory.

title: Gonadal hormones contribute to sex differences in behavior, pathology and epigenetic modifications in the 3×Tg-AD mouse model of Alzheimer’s disease
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that gonadal hormones contribute to sex-specific differences in Alzheimer's disease pathology through epigenetic mechanisms in the 3×Tg-AD mouse model. Female gonadal hormones protect against spatial learning impairments, while their loss impairs cognitive function; conversely, male gonadal hormones promote early behavioral dysfunction, and their removal improves spatial learning and memory. The sex-specific effects operate through differential regulation of the repressive histone variant macroH2A1, which in females reduces binding to the Mapt gene (promoting tau expression) but in males increases binding to the Psen1 promoter (suppressing amyloid-beta production). Notably, gonadectomized females exhibit enhanced Mapt and App expression without changes in Aβ42 or phosphorylated tau levels, while gonadectomized males show reduced App expression and cortical soluble Aβ42. These findings establish macroH2A1-mediated epigenetic modifications as a key mechanism underlying the greater AD-like pathology observed in female 3×Tg-AD mice, suggesting that hormone-dependent histone variant dynamics may explain the female predominance and greater severity of Alzheimer's disease in humans.

title: The role of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor in ovarian development, function, xenobiotic metabolism, and disease
summary: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AHR) is a ligand-activated transcription factor widely expressed across ovarian cell types, including granulosa cells, thecal cells, and oocytes, where it plays critical roles in folliculogenesis and steroidogenesis. The AHR is activated by diverse endogenous ligands (including estrogen metabolites and arachidonic acid derivatives) and exogenous ligands (dietary compounds like indole-3-carbinol and curcumin, as well as xenobiotics such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and dioxins). Upon activation, the AHR translocates to the nucleus and heterodimerizes with the aryl hydrocarbon receptor nuclear translocator (ARNT) to regulate target genes including cytochrome P450 enzymes (CYP1A1, CYP1A2, CYP1B1) that metabolize xenobiotics, eicosanoids, and estrogens. Dysregulation of AHR signaling is implicated in multiple ovarian pathologies: female mice lacking AHR exhibit reduced fertility and diminished offspring viability, while aberrant AHR activity is associated with infertility, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and ovarian cancer, partly through altered estrogen metabolism and elevated carcinogenic metabolite production. The AHR also regulates immune responses through T cell signaling, suggesting its involvement extends beyond reproductive endocrinology to systemic health and disease susceptibility.

title: Nociceptive sensory neuron-derived NGF orchestrates a fibrotic mesenchymal stromal cell neurogenic niche to drive tendon pathological fibrosis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals that nociceptive sensory neurons produce nerve growth factor (NGF) that orchestrates pathological tendon fibrosis by promoting mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) fibrotic commitment through the TrkA-Hif1α-Twist1 signaling axis. Nociceptive sensory nerves physically converge with MSC-enriched regions to form a functional fibrotic niche, where NGF-activated TrkA signaling drives Prrx1+ MSCs to differentiate into α-SMA+ myofibroblasts that mediate excessive collagen deposition. Genetic and pharmacological evidence demonstrates that denervation of nociceptive sensory nerves, DRG-specific NGF knockdown, or inhibition of TrkA with the clinical compound TPX-0005 significantly attenuates fibrosis, reduces myofibroblast accumulation, and improves functional outcomes. These findings establish sensory neuron-derived NGF as a critical pathological mediator in fibrosis and identify TrkA inhibition as a therapeutic strategy to reverse excessive tissue fibrosis while promoting tissue repair.

title: Evidence for Maternal Autoantibodies in the Pathogenesis of Preterm Birth
summary: Preterm birth is associated with a breakdown of maternal-fetal tolerance characterized by elevated proteome-wide autoreactivity, particularly enriched in pathways controlling inflammation, vascular development, and reproductive hormone signaling. A key pathogenic mechanism involves maternal autoantibodies targeting interleukin-1 receptor antagonist (IL1RA), which normally inhibits IL1β—a potent inflammatory cytokine sufficient to induce labor. Anti-IL1RA antibodies neutralize IL1RA activity, permitting unopposed IL1β signaling and triggering fetal resorption, inflammation, and vascular malperfusion in pregnant mice. The anti-IL1RA autoantibody signature emerges early in gestation and is significantly enriched in preterm pregnancies, particularly those delivering before 28 weeks, with increasing prevalence across trimester and higher burden in multiparous women. Additionally, the autoantibody signature targets multiple proteins involved in critical pregnancy processes, including histocompatibility molecules, prostaglandin synthesis enzymes, and placental angiogenic factors, suggesting that dysregulated autoimmunity against pathways essential for normal placentation and gestation underlies spontaneous preterm birth pathogenesis.

title: CEBPD-mediated SGPP2 upregulation via PERK/ER stress in endothelial cells disrupts S1P homeostasis and impairs angiogenesis in chronic endometritis
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals how chronic endometritis impairs endometrial angiogenesis through a dysregulated sphingolipid metabolism pathway centered on S1P degradation. Lipopolysaccharide-induced endoplasmic reticulum stress activates the PERK/eIF2α pathway, which drives CEBPD transcription factor activity and subsequently upregulates SGPP2, an S1P-degrading enzyme, in endothelial cells. This SGPP2-mediated degradation of sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P) impairs the S1PR1-STAT3-VEGFA signaling axis required for vascular remodeling and angiogenesis, thereby disrupting endometrial receptivity and contributing to reproductive failure. In vivo, intrauterine S1P administration reversed these pathogenic effects by restoring angiogenesis and reducing inflammation. These findings establish a mechanistic link between inflammatory stress, sphingolipid dysmetabolism, and angiogenic dysfunction in chronic endometritis, identifying S1P restoration as a potential therapeutic strategy for inflammation-associated implantation failure and pregnancy loss.

title: Sustained STING-IRF7 signaling aggravates LPS-induced endometrial inflammation via excessive neutrophil extracellular traps generation
summary:
# Sustained STING-IRF7 Signaling Aggravates LPS-Induced Endometrial Inflammation via Excessive Neutrophil Extracellular Traps Generation

This study demonstrates that sustained STING-IRF7 axis activation promotes pathological neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation in lipopolysaccharide-induced endometritis, thereby exacerbating endometrial inflammation and tissue damage. The mechanism operates through a coordinated molecular cascade in which STING and IRF7 upregulate the surface marker CD11b on neutrophils while simultaneously driving lipocalin-2 (LCN2) expression, which then signals through its receptor MC4R to increase intracellular adhesion molecule-1 (ICAM-1) expression and promote neutrophil recruitment and NETosis. Dysregulated NET formation unleashes proteases, reactive oxygen species, and citrullinated histones that directly compromise endometrial tissue integrity and perpetuate inflammation, contributing to chronic endometritis and associated reproductive failure including infertility and recurrent implantation failure. Importantly, genetic or pharmacological STING inhibition suppresses myeloperoxidase activity, reduces NET formation, curtails NET-driven tissue damage, and restores endometrial receptivity by reducing inflammatory infiltration and restoring the expression of the receptivity transcription factor HOXA10. These findings identify the STING-IRF7-LCN2-ICAM-1 axis as a therapeutically actionable target for precision anti-inflammatory therapy in chronic endometritis and suggest that targeting STING signaling may restore implantation competence in women with endometrial inflammation-associated infertility.

title: Therapeutic Strategies for MASH: An Update on Drug Candidates Under Investigation in Late-Phase Clinical Trials
journal: International journal of translational medicine (Basel, Switzerland)
summary:
# Summary

This review article examines therapeutic strategies for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH), a progressive liver disease characterized by hepatic steatosis, lobular inflammation, and hepatocyte ballooning that can rapidly advance to liver fibrosis and cirrhosis. Multiple drug candidates in late-phase clinical trials target distinct molecular pathways to treat MASH: resmetirom acts as a thyroid hormone receptor-β agonist, while GLP-1 receptor agonists like semaglutide and survodutide enhance insulin secretion and suppress hepatic glucose production and lipogenesis. Fibroblast growth factor 21-based therapeutics including efruxifermin and pegozafermin regulate glucose and lipid metabolism while inhibiting hepatic stellate cell activation and reducing fibrosis. Pan-PPAR agonists such as lanifibranor activate all three PPAR isoforms to suppress hepatic steatosis and macrophage infiltration while promoting lipid catabolism and adipocyte differentiation. These diverse therapeutic approaches collectively address the metabolic dysfunction and inflammatory pathology underlying MASH progression, offering hope for patients suffering from fatigue, weight loss, and eventual liver failure.

title: Synergistic therapeutic impact of dichloroacetate nanoparticles and doxorubicin in modulating pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase in breast carcinoma model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that dichloroacetate nanoparticles (DCA-PNPs) combined with doxorubicin synergistically inhibit pyruvate dehydrogenase kinase (PDK) enzymes, which are central regulators of cancer cell metabolism. PDK normally sustains the Warburg effect by inactivating the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex, allowing cancer cells to maintain glycolysis and resist oxidative stress; doxorubicin shows superior binding affinity to PDK isoforms (−7.7 to −8.3 kcal/mol) compared to DCA alone, while both agents converge to downregulate PDK1-4 gene expression. The combination treatment significantly increased apoptosis and G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, reduced tumor burden and ascitic volume, and improved mean survival time and lifespan in a mouse breast carcinoma model while minimizing hepatorenal toxicity. Nanoparticle delivery improved drug efficacy while reducing collateral damage to healthy tissues, addressing multiple adverse effects associated with doxorubicin monotherapy. These findings suggest that targeting PDK-mediated metabolic reprogramming represents a promising therapeutic strategy to enhance breast cancer treatment and overcome chemotherapy resistance.

title: Advances in the molecular mechanisms underlying progestin resistance in endometrial cancer
summary:
# Molecular Mechanisms of Progestin Resistance in Endometrial Cancer

Unopposed estrogen exposure causes endometrial hyperplasia, a precursor condition that develops in approximately 80% of endometrial cancer patients and is frequently associated with obesity and insulin resistance. Progestins treat endometrial cancer through multiple mechanisms: progesterone binds classical and membrane receptors to induce G0/G1 cell cycle arrest and apoptosis, suppresses estrogen receptor and transforming growth factor-beta signaling, reduces angiogenesis via vimentin downregulation and vascular endothelial growth factor inhibition, and enhances anti-tumor immunity by promoting natural killer cells while reducing regulatory T cells. However, a significant subset of patients exhibits progestin resistance driven by mutations in mismatch repair genes (MSH2, MSH6), Wnt pathway alterations (CTNNB1), and epigenetic silencing of the progesterone receptor through DNA methylation and EGFR-mediated phosphorylation, combined with elevated expression of survival factors like Nrf2 and survivin. Emerging therapeutic strategies to overcome resistance include DNA methyltransferase and histone deacetylase inhibitors that restore progesterone receptor expression, while aromatase inhibition and direct targeting of estrogen synthesis represent complementary approaches to reducing endometrial cancer proliferation.

title: Q-marker identification strategies in traditional Chinese medicines: a systematic review of research from 2020 to 2024
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review (2020-2024) establishes comprehensive strategies for identifying quality markers (Q-markers) in traditional herbal prescriptions through an integrated, mechanism-driven validation framework. The approach combines multiple complementary methodologies: computational techniques including network pharmacology, in silico preliminary filtering, and molecular docking for binding affinity prediction; analytical methods such as HPLC, UPLC, GC-MS, and LC-MS for composition analysis; and experimental validation through in vitro and in vivo bioassays including Caco-2 cell transport assays for bioavailability assessment. Q-markers must satisfy five critical criteria—traceability, specificity, effectiveness, measurability, and compatibility with traditional Chinese medicine theory—and the authors recommend a stepwise approach integrating compound bioavailability screening, disease-relevant biomarker selection, profile-effect correlation modeling, and multi-criteria scoring frameworks. The proposed integrated model improves biological relevance and practical applicability by accounting for the multi-target mechanisms inherent to herbal formulations while enabling scalable identification of bioactive compounds that substantiate therapeutic benefits and support quality control across raw materials and end products.

title: Sex-specific dysregulation of the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 Axis following cocaine exposure: Translational evidence for a potential biomarker of abstinence
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals sex-specific dysregulation of the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 signaling axis in response to cocaine exposure, with important implications for understanding cocaine use disorder. Cocaine disrupts neurotransmitter systems and triggers neuroinflammation by activating microglia, a process normally regulated by the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 axis, which acts as a critical brake on microglial activation. Female rats exhibited significantly lower baseline CX3CL1 and CX3CR1 expression compared to males, and displayed more pronounced dose- and time-dependent transcriptional responses to acute cocaine, with heightened upregulation at moderate and higher doses (15-30 mg/kg). Conversely, repeated cocaine exposure produced persistent downregulation of CX3CL1 in both sexes, though males uniquely showed elevated plasma CX3CL1 concentrations that increased with abstinence duration—a finding suggesting CX3CL1 as a potential biomarker of cocaine abstinence in males. These sex-divergent changes in the CX3CL1/CX3CR1 pathway may underlie documented sex differences in addiction progression, where women escalate more rapidly to cocaine use disorder despite being less likely to initiate use, potentially reflecting distinct neuroinflammatory vulnerability profiles.

title: Covalent modification of a glutamic acid inspired by HaloTag technology
summary:
# Summary

This study describes DeltaTag (compound 6a), a covalent PDEδ inhibitor inspired by HaloTag technology, that targets the glutamic acid residue at position 88 of phosphodiesterase delta (PDEδ). The compound uses a bromoalkane warhead that forms an irreversible ester bond with PDEδ, overcoming the limitation of non-covalent inhibitors that are displaced by Arl2/3 GTPases. DeltaTag achieves potent and selective binding through a fragment-based hybrid design incorporating a piperidine anchor that engages key hydrogen bond networks with PDEδ residues. By covalently modifying PDEδ, DeltaTag disrupts the PDEδ-Rheb-mTORC1 signaling axis and suppresses cancer cell proliferation more effectively than reversible inhibitors. This work demonstrates that targeting carboxylic acid residues—which comprise 12% of the proteome compared to 2.3% for reactive cysteines—represents a valuable approach for developing targeted covalent inhibitors with improved selectivity and durability.

title: The impact of anti-phosphatidylserine/prothrombin antibodies on pregnancy outcomes in patients with unexplained recurrent implantation failure: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study demonstrates that anti-phosphatidylserine/prothrombin (aPS/PT) antibodies are significantly associated with unexplained recurrent implantation failure and adverse pregnancy outcomes, with aPS/PT-positive patients showing markedly reduced cumulative live birth rates (20% versus 32.1%) and elevated miscarriage rates (41.4% versus 20.4%) compared to aPS/PT-negative patients. The pathogenic mechanism involves aPS/PT antibodies binding to the phosphatidylserine-prothrombin complex and subsequently stimulating monocytes to enhance tissue factor and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) production, which activates the coagulation cascade and triggers thromboinflammatory responses. These antibodies directly impair trophoblast function by binding via β2-glycoprotein I, inhibiting proliferation and human chorionic gonadotropin secretion, while TNF-α suppresses placental differentiation and uterine spiral artery function, ultimately leading to endometrial microthrombosis and inadequate placental perfusion. The aPS/PT IgM isotype demonstrated superior diagnostic sensitivity for early recurrent pregnancy loss and unexplained miscarriage, emerging as an independent risk factor for miscarriage in infertile patients. These findings suggest that aPS/PT antibody screening and testing should be incorporated into the diagnostic evaluation of patients with unexplained recurrent implantation failure to identify those at heightened risk for adverse pregnancy outcomes and enable targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: PEBL, a component-based Chinese medicine, reduces virus-induced acute lung injury by targeting FXR to decrease ACE2 levels
summary: PEBL, a Chinese herbal medicine composed of phillyrin, emodin, baicalin, and liquiritin, effectively treats virus-induced acute lung injury by targeting the farnesyl X receptor (FXR) to suppress angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) expression. The study demonstrates that PEBL downregulates FXR expression, preventing FXR from binding to ACE2 promoters and thereby reducing ACE2 transcription, which are critical steps in viral pathogenesis. This disruption of the FXR/ACE2 signaling axis attenuates the downstream NF-κB-p65 pathway, leading to decreased cytokine production, reduced macrophage infiltration, and prevention of cytokine storm formation. In vivo models using Poly(I:C), influenza PR8, and SARS-CoV-2 XBB.1.16 challenge demonstrated that PEBL significantly improved lung pathology, reduced viral load, and decreased mortality while mitigating weight loss and pulmonary lesions. The four individual components of PEBL bind directly to FXR, suggesting their combined anti-inflammatory activity contributes to the protective effects against acute respiratory virus-induced lung injury.

title: Differential peritoneal resident macrophage depletion and omental milky spot disruption after high versus low clodronate‐loaded liposome dose treatment
summary:
# Summary

Clodronate-loaded liposomes deplete peritoneal resident macrophages (resMØs) by disrupting mitochondrial ATP/ADP translocase function, offering therapeutic potential for inflammatory conditions including endometriosis, rheumatoid arthritis, and liver fibrosis. The study reveals a critical dose-dependent difference: high-dose clodronate causes complete and sustained depletion of both Tim4+ embryonic-derived and Tim4− bone marrow-derived resMØs while inducing peritoneal inflammation and severely disrupting omental milky spot architecture, including vascular network disorganization. In contrast, low-dose clodronate achieves transient resMØ depletion while preserving milky spot vascular integrity and structure. The omental milky spot, which produces the chemokine CXCL13 (essential for B-cell homing and lymphoid tissue organization) and expresses retinoic acid to activate the transcription factor Gata6, proves critical for resMØ homeostasis and replacement via CSF1-dependent proliferation. High-dose clodronate prevents resMØ recovery by destroying this supportive niche, whereas low-dose treatment allows regeneration through CCR2-mediated Ly6Chi monocyte recruitment and subsequent differentiation into MHCII+ monocyte-derived macrophages.

title: Multidimensional regulation of estrogen signaling in pelvic floor connective tissue homeostasis and remodeling
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen exerts multidimensional regulatory effects on pelvic floor connective tissue homeostasis through its three main receptors—ERα, ERβ, and GPER1—controlling distinct but interconnected pathways. At the molecular level, estrogen promotes collagen synthesis and cross-linking while simultaneously inhibiting matrix metalloproteinase activity through the ERβ/AP-1 pathway, thereby maintaining extracellular matrix integrity essential for pelvic support structures. In pelvic organ prolapse, this protective mechanism becomes dysregulated, characterized by estrogen deficiency, an elevated ERα/ERβ ratio, reduced ERβ binding at the MMP-9 promoter, and surprisingly, diminished HOXA13 expression independent of estrogen status, indicating both hormonal and genetic contributions to disease pathology. Beyond collagen regulation, estrogen suppresses chronic inflammation and modulates fibroblast function through non-genomic signaling via GPER1, which activates phospholipase C and protein kinase C cascades affecting metabolic and inflammatory pathways. The article emphasizes that local estrogen synthesis through aromatase—itself upregulated by inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α in endometriotic lesions—becomes dysregulated in disease states, suggesting that therapeutic interventions must address both estrogen signaling defects and the underlying inflammatory microenvironment to restore tissue homeostasis.

title: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor: a rehabilitated target for therapeutic immune modulation
summary:
# Summary

The aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AHR) is a ligand-activated transcription factor that has emerged as a critical immune regulator, moving beyond its historical association with mediating toxic effects of environmental pollutants like dioxins. Upon ligand binding, AHR translocates to the nucleus where it heterodimerizes with the AHR nuclear translocator (ARNT) to activate xenobiotic response elements and regulate diverse target genes including cytokine genes (IL-10, IL-21) and xenobiotic-metabolizing enzymes (CYP1A1, CYP1A2, CYP1B1). AHR activation can be initiated by multiple ligand sources including endogenous tryptophan metabolites from the kynurenine pathway and bacterial metabolism, as well as exogenous compounds like the recently approved therapeutic tapinarof for psoriasis treatment. Beyond direct gene regulation, AHR modulates immune responses by controlling NF-κB and STAT signaling pathways, inducing immunosuppressive factors like SOCS2, and promoting regulatory T cell expansion or TH17 cell responses depending on the nature of the activating ligand. The therapeutic potential of AHR activation is now being harnessed to treat immune-mediated inflammatory diseases, representing a significant rehabilitation of this receptor from its original characterization as a mediator of xenobiotic toxicity.

title: NRN1 as a therapeutic target for Alzheimer's disease
summary: Neuritin-1 (NRN1), a neurotrophic factor and synaptic protein, has been identified as a key mediator of cognitive resilience in Alzheimer's disease. NRN1 protein abundance correlates positively with global cognitive performance and slower rates of cognitive decline, independent of neuropathological burden, suggesting a protective mechanism against cognitive deterioration. The protein functions through multiple synaptic mechanisms, including promotion of synapse maturation, long-term stability, activity-dependent plasticity, and neuroprotection against amyloid-beta toxicity. NRN1 operates as a hub protein within synaptic co-expression networks that remain elevated specifically in cognitively resilient individuals, and its expression in excitatory neurons decreases with advancing neurodegeneration. These findings position NRN1 as a promising therapeutic target for enhancing cognitive resilience and potentially slowing cognitive decline in Alzheimer's disease patients.

title: Current Trends in GPCR Allostery
summary:
# Summary

G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) are seven-transmembrane cell surface proteins representing approximately 34% of FDA-approved drug targets, with primary roles in mediating neuroendocrine and pharmacological stimuli. Unlike traditional orthosteric drugs that completely block endogenous ligand binding and inhibit all GPCR signaling, allosteric drugs represent an innovative therapeutic approach by binding to distinct allosteric sites outside the orthosteric pocket, thereby modulating GPCR signaling while preserving endogenous ligand activity. Allosteric modulators function as positive, negative, or silent agents that enhance, reduce, or maintain endogenous ligand action respectively, with their activity remaining dependent on the presence of the endogenous agonist. This allosteric strategy offers significant advantages including high receptor specificity due to low conservation of allosteric sites, reduced off-target activity, and prevention of unwanted side effects while maintaining beneficial physiological signaling. Recent applications demonstrate allosteric modulation across multiple GPCRs including M2 muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, GLP-1R, mGlu5, and FFAR1, with compounds like VU6003586 showing potent activity. Additionally, silent allosteric modulators have potential therapeutic utility in blocking pathological autoantibody-mediated GPCR activation implicated in diseases such as Graves' disease, hypertension, and Alzheimer's disease.

title: Mast cell–expressed Mrgprb2/MRGPRX2 mediates gout pain and inflammation via a neuroimmune axis
summary: This study reveals a critical neuroimmune axis in gout pathogenesis centered on the mast cell receptor Mrgprb2/MRGPRX2 and its activation by substance P. Monosodium urate crystals trigger gouty arthritis by promoting substance P elevation in the synovium, which then activates Mrgprb2-expressing synovial mast cells, leading to their degranulation and release of proinflammatory mediators including TNFα, IL-1β, and chemokines. Activated mast cells subsequently sensitize joint nociceptors, driving both pain and neurogenic inflammation characterized by neutrophil infiltration and joint swelling. Genetic deletion or pharmacological blockade of Mrgprb2/MRGPRX2 significantly attenuated mechanical and thermal hyperalgesia, reduced immune cell infiltration, and decreased joint inflammation in gout models, while osthole, a natural Mrgprb2 antagonist, rapidly alleviated pain and swelling. These findings establish mast cell–sensory neuron crosstalk mediated by Mrgprb2/MRGPRX2 as a major driver of gout pain and inflammation, identifying this pathway as a promising therapeutic target for inflammatory joint diseases.

title: Fractalkine (Chemokine CX3CL1) Signaling During Placentation and Placental Function
summary:
# Summary

Fractalkine (CX3CL1) and its receptor CX3CR1 constitute a critical signaling axis for normal placentation and pregnancy. CX3CL1, expressed in cytotrophoblasts and endometrial glands, exists in both membrane-bound and soluble forms that interact with CX3CR1, which is predominantly expressed on placental endothelial cells and immune cells including monocytes, macrophages, and T cells. This signaling pathway regulates multiple essential processes including trophoblast invasion, spiral artery remodeling, angiogenesis through VEGF-A/VEGFR-2 pathway activation, and immune cell adhesion and migration. Dysregulation of CX3CL1/CX3CR1 signaling is associated with multiple pregnancy pathologies including preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, and abnormal implantation, demonstrating that proper cytokine balance is necessary for successful pregnancy outcomes. The axis operates through G protein-coupled signaling cascades that coordinate cellular migration, adhesion, proliferation, and resistance to apoptosis, with metalloproteinases including ADAM10 regulating the generation of soluble CX3CL1 during physiological conditions and under pathological stress.

title: Estrogen-Dependent Regulation of FDPS in the Mouse Uterus and Its Expression in Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen regulates the expression of farnesyl diphosphate synthase (FDPS), a key enzyme in the mevalonate pathway, through estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) signaling in the uterine endometrium. FDPS catalyzes the synthesis of farnesyl pyrophosphate, which serves as a precursor for both cholesterol biosynthesis and protein prenylation, thus linking estrogen signaling to critical metabolic pathways. In mouse models, FDPS expression is dynamically regulated across the estrous cycle, peaking during the proestrus stage when estrogen levels are elevated, and can be directly induced by estrogen treatment while being suppressed by the ERα antagonist ICI 182,780. FDPS is highly expressed in endometrial cancer tissues, particularly in well-differentiated tumors, where it promotes cancer cell proliferation and viability, and FDPS knockdown significantly reduces cell proliferation in the Ishikawa endometrial cancer cell line. Pan-cancer analysis reveals that FDPS alterations are associated with poor overall survival across multiple cancer types, including breast cancer. These findings suggest that FDPS functions as a metabolic regulator in estrogen-dependent gynecological malignancies and may serve as a prognostic biomarker or therapeutic target in endometrial cancer.

title: The AGE–RAGE Pathway in Endometriosis: A Focused Mechanistic Review and Structured Evidence Map
summary:
# Summary

This review elucidates how the AGE–RAGE pathway drives endometriosis pathogenesis through interconnected inflammatory and metabolic mechanisms. Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) accumulate in endometriosis due to shared metabolic drivers—insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and chronic inflammation—and bind RAGE on immune cells and endothelial cells, triggering cascades through ERK1/2, p38, STAT3, and NF-κB that culminate in NLRP3 inflammasome activation and IL-1β/IL-18 secretion. Key RAGE ligands including HMGB1 and S100 proteins demonstrate direct causal involvement in endometriotic pathophysiology, particularly in lesion remodeling and fibrosis. The pathway establishes a pathogenic amplification loop whereby NF-κB activation transcribes RAGE itself while simultaneously priming NLRP3, creating sustained inflammatory signaling. Notably, endometriosis-associated metabolic stress phenotypes link to significantly elevated cardiovascular risk including heart attack, stroke, arrhythmia, and heart failure, suggesting that AGE–RAGE-driven vascular dysfunction and arterial stiffening extend systemic complications beyond pelvic disease. This mechanistic framework identifies multiple therapeutic intervention points within AGE accumulation, RAGE signaling, and inflammasome assembly for managing endometriosis and its cardiovascular comorbidities.

title: Identification of a novel natural compound that acts on the membrane progestin receptor α (paqr7) from the marine algae Padina
summary:
# Summary

Researchers isolated a novel marine-derived compound named padinic acid (1-CB 2-HPNA) from the brown algae *Padina arborescens* and characterized its function as the first known antagonist of the membrane progestin receptor α (mPRα/PAQR7). The compound binds to mPRα with affinity comparable to progesterone but blocks mPR-mediated oocyte maturation and ovulation in both fish and mice, functioning through nongenomic steroid signaling pathways. Padinic acid demonstrated potent dose-dependent inhibitory activity against agonist-induced oocyte maturation and prevented ovulation in mice with efficacy comparable to the antiprogesterone agent mifepristone. The compound exhibits low toxicity and potential contraceptive applications, and given that mPRs are highly expressed in endometriosis and ovarian cancer tissues, padinic acid warrants investigation as a therapeutic candidate for these mPR-associated pathologies. These findings establish a natural product scaffold for developing selective mPRα antagonists with possible reproductive and oncological therapeutic potential.

title: Antibody glycosylation in neuroimmune diseases
summary:
# Summary

Antibody glycosylation, particularly N-glycosylation at the Asn297 site of the immunoglobulin G (IgG) Fc region, critically regulates immunoglobulin effector functions including antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC) and complement-dependent cytotoxicity (CDC). Specific glycosylation modifications—including core fucosylation, galactosylation, sialylation, and bisecting GlcNAc structures—modulate IgG interactions with Fc receptors (FcγRs) and complement components, shifting antibody functionality between pro-inflammatory and anti-inflammatory states. In neuroimmune diseases such as multiple sclerosis, neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder, and Guillain–Barré syndrome, characteristic reductions in IgG galactosylation, sialylation, and core fucosylation have been consistently observed, correlating with a shift toward pro-inflammatory antibody function and increased disease severity. These altered glycosylation patterns serve as potential biomarkers for neuroimmune disease pathogenesis and offer promising targets for therapeutic intervention through strategies such as engineered monoclonal antibodies or modulation of glycosylation machinery. The findings underscore how post-translational glycosylation modifications fundamentally determine antibody functionality in health and disease, with implications for understanding autoimmune and neuroinflammatory pathogenesis.

title: A duo of redox-sensitive pore-loop cysteines controls the activity of the neural ion channel TRPM3
summary:
# Summary

TRPM3 is a calcium-permeable ion channel expressed widely in the nervous system and peripheral tissues, where it mediates thermosensation and nociception by detecting heat and contributing to pain signaling during inflammation and neuronal injury. The study identifies two redox-sensitive cysteine residues in the extracellular pore loop of TRPM3 that undergo reversible disulfide bridge formation, enabling bidirectional regulation of channel activity by the cellular redox state. Oxidizing agents and hydrogen peroxide enhance TRPM3 responsiveness to activators like pregnenolone sulfate, while reducing agents such as dithiothreitol abolish this sensitivity, demonstrating that the redox state profoundly alters channel properties and ligand responses. These redox-control mechanisms operate on the extracellular side of the plasma membrane and may provide an alternative pathway through which inflammatory conditions involving reactive oxygen species could modulate TRPM3 function in pain pathways and neurological diseases. The findings have implications for understanding how gain-of-function TRPM3 variants drive neurodevelopmental and epileptic disorders, and suggest that redox modulation represents a physiologically relevant control mechanism for this ion channel.

title: The dual face of human chorionic gonadotropin in the CNS: neuroprotection, signaling, and possible pathological effects
summary:
# Summary

Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is a placenta-derived hormone critical for pregnancy maintenance that exerts pleiotropic effects on the central nervous system through interaction with luteinizing hormone/choriogonadotropin receptors expressed in neurons, glial cells, and key brain regions including the hippocampus and cerebral cortex. Beyond its well-established role in stimulating progesterone production and supporting placental angiogenesis, hCG promotes immune tolerance by driving a Th2-dominant response, suppressing Th1 inflammation, and enhancing regulatory T cell activity. At physiological levels, hCG exhibits neuroprotective properties by activating pro-survival signaling pathways (PI3K/Akt, cAMP-PKA, MAPK, and JAK-STAT), stimulating brain-derived neurotrophic factor release, and reducing neurodegeneration and β-amyloid accumulation in preclinical models. However, dysregulated hCG levels—arising from pregnancy complications, assisted reproductive technologies, or hCG-secreting tumors—are associated with pathological consequences including blood-brain barrier disruption, systemic and central nervous system inflammation, and potential neurodevelopmental effects such as autism spectrum disorder and schizophrenia. Additionally, the hyperglycosylated hCG isoform promotes tumor aggressiveness by inhibiting apoptosis and facilitating immune escape, highlighting hCG's dual role as both a protective and potentially pathogenic molecule in the central nervous system.

title: Spinal glial cell derived extra-pituitary prolactin contributes to postoperative pain in females
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies spinal glial-derived extra-pituitary prolactin (PRL ext) as a critical contributor to female-selective postoperative pain following surgical incision. Following hindpaw incision surgery, PRL ext is upregulated in multiple tissue compartments including dorsal root ganglion neurons, epidermal keratinocytes, peripheral nerve Schwann cells, and importantly, spinal GLAST-positive astrocytes. The induced PRL ext activates prolactin receptor (PRLR) signaling on sensory neurons, leading to STAT5 phosphorylation that correlates with increased pain hypersensitivity. Notably, blocking PRLR function with the antagonist ΔPRL substantially reduces post-incision pain hypersensitivity in female rodents, whereas pituitary-derived prolactin is not required for this pain response, as demonstrated by bromocriptine and hypophysectomy experiments. Genetic ablation of spinal GLAST-positive astrocytes similarly attenuates incision-induced hypersensitivity, confirming that these cells are a major source of pain-promoting extra-pituitary prolactin. These findings reveal a novel female-specific pain mechanism involving glial-astrocyte-derived prolactin and suggest PRLR antagonism as a potential therapeutic strategy for postoperative pain in women.

title: Taohong Siwu Decoction Regulates MSC‐Mediated H‐Type Angiogenesis to Accelerate Bone Fracture Healing Through VHL/HIF‐1α Ubiquitination
summary:
# Summary

Taohong Siwu Decoction (TSD), a traditional Chinese medicine formula, accelerates bone fracture healing by regulating mesenchymal stem cell (MSC)-mediated H-type angiogenesis through modulation of the VHL/HIF-1α ubiquitination pathway. In fracture models, TSD upregulates key angiogenic markers including endomucin, CD90, HIF-1α, and vascular endothelial growth factor while simultaneously enhancing MSC proliferation and migration and inhibiting VHL/HIF-1α ubiquitination. H-type capillaries, characterized by high expression of CD31 and endomucin, are critical for promoting both angiogenesis and bone formation during fracture healing. The bioactive component paeoniflorin, identified through molecular docking studies, binds directly to phosphorylated VHL and promotes MSC-mediated angiogenesis via the pVHL/HIF-1α pathway. By suppressing VHL-mediated proteasomal degradation of HIF-1α, TSD facilitates HIF-1α accumulation and downstream angiogenic signaling, thereby enhancing vascularization and the homing of MSCs to fracture sites—processes essential for osteoblast recruitment and bone regeneration.

title: Women with polycystic ovary syndrome exhibit impaired endometrial receptivity with excessive ERα and histone lactylation
summary:
# Summary

Women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) exhibit impaired endometrial receptivity characterized by excessive estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) expression and elevated histone lactylation (Kla), particularly H3K18la and H4K12la modifications. This epigenetic dysregulation occurs through a pathway where elevated intracellular lactate promotes histone lactylation, which in turn upregulates ERα and its downstream estrogen-responsive genes (including MSX1, MSX2, and LTF), while simultaneously suppressing critical decidualization markers such as IGFBP1, PRL, COX2, and HOXA10. The study demonstrates that lactate-driven histone lactylation is essential to this pathogenic mechanism, as inhibiting either glycolysis or histone lactylation in a DHEA-induced PCOS mouse model restores implantation sites and uterine receptivity. These findings identify ERα overexpression and histone lactylation as key indicators of endometrial dysfunction in PCOS, suggesting that targeting lactate production or histone lactylation could restore endometrial receptivity and provide a novel therapeutic strategy to improve fertility outcomes in PCOS patients.

title: Additive effects of high intensity interval training and therapeutic adenosine on gene and protein expression in lipid metabolism and weight loss in high fat diet-induced obese rats
summary:
# Summary

High-intensity interval training (HIIT) combined with therapeutic adenosine administration produces synergistic effects on lipid metabolism and obesity in high-fat diet-induced obese rats. HIIT activates lipolytic pathways while suppressing lipogenic pathways through upregulation of key enzymes including AMPK, HSL, CGI-58, and ATGL, while simultaneously downregulating the lipogenic enzyme ACC. Adenosine further enhances these lipolytic effects by binding to A2A receptors and upregulating lipolytic gene expression, with both interventions converging on the AMPK-cAMP-PKA signaling axis that phosphorylates and activates HSL. The combined HFD + HIIT + adenosine treatment group demonstrated the most significant weight loss and exhibited the highest expression of lipolytic proteins AMPK, HSL, and the A2A adenosine receptor compared to other treatment groups. These findings demonstrate that combining exercise with adenosine therapy provides additive benefits for reversing high-fat diet-induced metabolic dysfunction through coordinated activation of fat breakdown pathways and suppression of fat synthesis.

title: Intelligent nanoliposome ameliorate chronic intermittent hypoxia-mediated neuronal injury via a dual regulation microglial inflammation strategy
summary: Chronic intermittent hypoxia drives cognitive dysfunction through microglial activation and neuroinflammation, characterized by M1 phenotype polarization and elevated ROS, IL-1β, and TNF-α in hippocampal regions. The study demonstrates that a dual-targeted nanoliposome formulation (Ang-Lip@BAY/GW1929), which crosses the blood-brain barrier and targets LRP-1 on activated microglia, ameliorates this pathology by co-delivering a PPARγ agonist (GW1929) and an IκBα phosphorylation inhibitor (BAY). These agents coordinately suppress the NF-κB inflammatory pathway—via both direct PPARγ-mediated inhibition and IκBα/p65-mediated blockade—thereby reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine production and shifting microglia toward a protective M2 phenotype. The ROS-responsive design ensures cargo release specifically in the hypoxic microenvironment, allowing targeted suppression of microglia-driven neuroinflammation and neuronal damage. This dual-regulatory strategy effectively reversed cognitive dysfunction and hippocampal neuronal injury in a chronic intermittent hypoxia model, presenting a promising nanomedical approach for obstructive sleep apnea-related cognitive impairments.

title: Rethinking estrogen receptor β in EAOC: a synergistic modulator in a genomically informed landscape
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) emerges as a critical synergistic driver in endometriosis-associated ovarian cancer (EAOC), progressively upregulated during malignant transformation while estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) is downregulated. ERβ orchestrates a pro-survival and pro-metastatic phenotype by upregulating brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF), which engages its cognate receptor TrkB to amplify PI3K/AKT, MAPK/ERK, and JAK/STAT signaling—pathways already constitutively activated by high-frequency Type I driver mutations in PIK3CA, PTEN loss, and ARID1A alterations. Beyond pathway activation, ERβ confers apoptosis resistance through TNF-α-induced caspase blockade, ferroptosis resistance via GPX4 upregulation, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition through IL-1β, Slug, and Snail induction. The BDNF/TrkB axis particularly amplifies malignancy in PIK3CA-mutant or PTEN-deficient tumors, associating with invasive phenotype, lymph node metastasis, deep stromal invasion, and platinum resistance, especially when the full-length TrkB-TK isoform predominates. This integrated genomic landscape reveals ERβ as a central hub coupling endometriotic inflammation and oxidative stress—notably iron-driven stress and IL-6/STAT3 signaling in ovarian endometriomas—to oncogenic transformation.

title: Neutrophil extracellular traps in gynecological disease: pathogenic mechanisms and therapeutic opportunities
summary: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) are multifunctional structures composed of DNA, histones, neutrophil elastase, and myeloperoxidase that serve as central effectors in innate immunity and endocrine signaling within the female reproductive tract. While NETs contribute to host defense by trapping and neutralizing pathogens, their dysregulation promotes multiple gynecological pathologies including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and polycystic ovary syndrome through mechanisms involving epithelial injury, fibrosis, tissue remodeling, oxidative stress, and chronic pain. Sex hormones—particularly estrogen and progesterone—critically modulate NET formation, localization, and clearance through direct effects on neutrophil chemotaxis, degranulation, and reactive oxygen species production via receptors expressed on neutrophil surfaces. In infectious conditions such as pelvic inflammatory disease initiated by Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, pathogen-activated NETosis is amplified by pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-8, IL-1β, and TNF-α, while vaginal microbiota dysbiosis similarly triggers exaggerated NET release and inflammatory responses. In gynecologic cancers, NETs actively promote tumor progression by facilitating cell adhesion, invasion, immune escape, and thromboembolic complications. These findings suggest that targeting NET formation and clearance mechanisms represents a promising therapeutic strategy across diverse gynecological diseases.

title: Targeting glial-orchestrated neuroinflammation in migraine pathophysiology
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how glial cells—particularly astrocytes and microglia—orchestrate neuroinflammatory cascades that drive migraine pathophysiology. Cortical spreading depolarization (CSD), a key migraine trigger, activates astrocytes and microglia to release pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and HMGB1, which amplify neuroinflammation through NF-κB pathway activation and inflammasome assembly. Sensory neuropeptides including CGRP, PACAP, and VIP promote intracranial vasodilation and central/peripheral sensitization, while also triggering mast cells and dendritic cells to drive neurogenic inflammation. Reactive astrocytes compromise blood-brain barrier integrity and impair glutamate reuptake via downregulation of EAAT2/GLT-1, leading to neuronal hyperexcitability and CSD propagation. Additionally, satellite glial cells and Schwann cells mediate peripheral nociceptive signaling through P2×7 receptor activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine release. The article highlights mutations in genes encoding astrocytic ion transporters (ATP1A2, SLC4A4) and NMDAR subunits as genetic factors increasing migraine susceptibility, suggesting that targeted modulation of glial function represents a promising therapeutic strategy.

title: Nanoengineered Niclosamide as a Microenvironment Modulator for Spinal Cord Regeneration: A Hypothetical Roadmap Toward Brain/Head Transplant Feasibility
journal: Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews. Nanomedicine and Nanobiotechnology
summary:
# Summary

Spinal cord injury triggers a pathological cascade involving immune cell infiltration that activates NF-κB and STAT3 signaling pathways, driving sustained neuroinflammation, astrocytic reactivity, and glial scar formation. Simultaneously, aberrant Wnt/β-catenin and TGF-β signaling stimulates fibroblast activation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition, with enhanced lysyl oxidase activity reinforcing collagen crosslinking to mechanically constrain axonal regeneration. Nanoengineered niclosamide addresses this hostile microenvironment through multiple mechanisms: it suppresses NF-κB and STAT3-driven inflammatory signaling and reactive astrogliosis, inhibits Wnt/β-catenin-mediated fibrotic pathways, reduces pro-fibrotic fibroblast responses to TGF-β, and downregulates lysyl oxidase to decrease ECM stiffness. By lowering reactive oxygen species production and operating at the bioenergetic level through mitochondrial uncoupling modulation, nanoengineered niclosamide effectively reprograms the post-injury microenvironment to suppress inhibitory cues and promote regenerative capacity. This multi-targeted approach positions niclosamide as a promising pharmacological tool for spinal cord regeneration that could facilitate more complex surgical interventions such as transplantation procedures.

title: Analysis of clinical trials on drugs targeting dopamine receptors: a scoping review
summary: This scoping review systematizes clinical trials investigating dopamine receptor-targeting drugs across a broad spectrum of neuropsychiatric and systemic disorders. Dopamine exerts its effects by binding to dopamine receptors, which are G protein-coupled receptors that initiate multiple intracellular signaling cascades including the cAMP-PKA axis and the Gαq-phospholipase C pathway, ultimately regulating synaptic plasticity, motor control, cognitive function, and hormone regulation. The review identifies schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease as the most frequently studied indications, reflecting the established pathophysiological roles of dopamine dysregulation in these conditions, while also documenting trials examining dopamine involvement in diverse conditions ranging from substance use disorders and movement disorders to metabolic and endocrine abnormalities. Key therapeutic agents reviewed include L-DOPA for Parkinson's disease, antipsychotics that inhibit D2 receptors, dopamine agonists, and emerging compounds such as pridopidine and armodafinil that modulate dopaminergic signaling through multiple receptor subtypes and alternative mechanisms. The broad expression of dopamine receptor subtypes across central nervous system and peripheral tissues explains the pleiotropic clinical applications of dopamine-targeting therapeutics, though the review underscores the need for continued investigation into dopamine's role in less commonly studied conditions including endometriosis and acute lung injury.

title: Diagnostic Value of Serum VEGF-D in Lymphangioleiomyomatosis: Results of the First Prospective Study in the Russian Federation
summary: Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM) is a rare lung disease driven by somatic or germline mutations in the TSC genes, particularly TSC2, which leads to aberrant mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) pathway activation and subsequent proliferation of abnormal smooth muscle cells that infiltrate the lungs and abdomen. These LAM cells produce elevated levels of vascular endothelial growth factor D (VEGF-D), which activates VEGFR2 and VEGFR3 to promote pathological angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis, causing characteristic pulmonary cystic lesions and abdominal angiomyolipomas. Serum VEGF-D has emerged as a critical diagnostic biomarker, with levels above 800 pg/mL identified in 75% of LAM patients and a threshold of 738 pg/mL demonstrating 81% sensitivity and 79% specificity for definite LAM diagnosis; elevated VEGF-D correlates with lymphatic tumor burden and extrapulmonary manifestations including chylous complications and renal angiomyolipomas. The mTOR inhibitor sirolimus reduces serum VEGF-D levels while simultaneously stabilizing or improving pulmonary function, exercise capacity, and quality of life over extended treatment periods. These findings establish VEGF-D as both a reliable diagnostic tool and a treatment-responsive biomarker for monitoring LAM progression and therapeutic response.

title: When Estrogen Signaling Refuses to Die: Receptor Rewiring, Compartmentalization, and Endocrine Plasticity in Gynecological Cancers
summary:
# Summary

Estrogen signaling in gynecological cancers involves complex compartmentalization and receptor rewiring that paradoxically shifts from tumor-promoting to tumor-suppressive roles depending on context and receptor subtype.
