Estradiol activates three distinct receptor types—ERα, ERβ, and GPER1—which exert opposing functions: ERα generally drives proliferation and carcinogenesis, while ERβ exhibits antiproliferative and anti-inflammatory effects essential for normal ovarian function and female fertility. In cervical cancer, ERα expression paradoxically decreases with disease progression despite initiating carcinogenesis, whereas the ERα variant ERα-36, localized to plasma membranes and cytoplasm, becomes upregulated and activates oncogenic MAPK/ERK signaling. Estrogen receptor plasticity—involving splice variants, subcellular relocalization, epigenetic silencing of ERβ, and integration of non-genomic pathways with receptor crosstalk—enables gynecological cancers to maintain estrogen dependence while progressively achieving autonomy from hormone control. Selective modulators like raloxifene and fulvestrant can therapeutically target established cervical lesions, highlighting the potential of exploiting estrogen signaling complexity for cancer treatment.

title: Modulation of Nociceptive Ion Channels by Protease-Activated Receptor-2 in Inflammatory Pain: Molecular Mechanisms and Therapeutic Potential
summary:
# Summary

Protease-activated receptor 2 (PAR2) functions as a critical molecular hub in inflammatory pain by directly activating multiple nociceptive ion channels expressed in dorsal root ganglion neurons. Upon proteolytic cleavage by trypsin or mast cell tryptase, PAR2 couples to Gαq/11 and downstream effectors including phospholipase C, protein kinase C, and ERK1/2 to potentiate calcium influx through TRPV1, TRPV4, and TRPA1 channels, as well as acid-sensing ASIC3, thereby sensitizing sensory neurons to multiple pain stimuli. PAR2-mediated signaling also activates nuclear factor-κB, triggering proinflammatory cytokine release that creates a positive feedback loop sustaining nociceptor sensitization and contributing to thermal hyperalgesia, mechanical allodynia, and acidosis-evoked pain. Genetic deletion or pharmacological inhibition of TRPV1, TRPA1, and MAPK pathways significantly attenuates PAR2-induced pain behaviors in experimental models, establishing these ion channels and signaling cascades as promising therapeutic targets for protease-driven inflammatory pain conditions. This multimodal mechanism positions PAR2-ion channel interactions as central to the transition from acute to chronic pain states.

title: Design, Synthesis, Biological Evaluation and Molecular Docking Studies of New N-Heterocyclic Compounds as Aromatase Inhibitors
summary:
# Summary

This study describes the design and synthesis of novel N-heterocyclic compounds, particularly 1,2,4-triazole and 1,3,4-thiadiazole derivatives, as aromatase inhibitors for breast cancer treatment. Aromatase catalyzes androgen aromatization to estrogen in tissues including the ovary, placenta, and brain, and approximately 80% of breast cancers are estrogen-dependent, making aromatase a critical therapeutic target. The 1,2,4-triazole derivatives 5b, 5c, 5e, 5f, and 5g demonstrated potent aromatase inhibition comparable to the clinical standard letrozole (IC₅₀ = 0.031 μM) and exhibited strong antiproliferative activity against MCF-7 breast cancer cells, with compounds 5c, 5e, and 5f identified as the most potent inhibitors. Molecular docking studies revealed that compound 5c achieves superior binding through multiple critical interactions within the aromatase active site, including nitrogen atoms in the triazole ring coordinating with heme iron and specific interactions with key residues such as Arg115, Phe134, and Trp224. All synthesized compounds demonstrated favorable drug-like properties, including compliance with Lipinski's rules, moderate to good water solubility, and high gastrointestinal permeability, supporting their potential as lead candidates for further development as aromatase inhibitors in breast cancer therapy.

title: Sleep deprivation impairs follicular development attributable to granulosa cell pyroptosis mediated by S100A8/A9-driven macrophage M1 polarization
summary:
# Sleep Deprivation Impairs Follicular Development Through NLRP3-Mediated Granulosa Cell Pyroptosis

Sleep deprivation causes systemic immune dysregulation that compromises ovarian function and female fertility by triggering a cascade of inflammatory signaling centered on S100A8/A9 proteins secreted by activated neutrophils. These damage-associated molecules bind to TLR4 on macrophages, activating the TLR4/Myd88/NF-κB pathway, which promotes M1 macrophage polarization and subsequent granulosa cell pyroptosis through NLRP3 inflammasome activation. In sleep-deprived mice, this inflammatory response manifests as elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and IL-17A), extensive DNA damage, impaired mitochondrial function, and reduced numbers of developing follicles alongside increased atretic follicles, ultimately decreasing oocyte retrieval, maturation, and embryonic developmental potential. The sleep deprivation-induced oxidative stress involves increased reactive oxygen species accumulation, depleted antioxidant defenses (SOD and GSH), reduced ATP production, and enhanced lipid peroxidation that exacerbate granulosa cell damage. Notably, treatment with the anti-inflammatory drug acetaminophen effectively mitigates these pathological effects by reducing pro-inflammatory cytokines, oxidative stress markers, and DNA damage while partially restoring mitochondrial function and energy metabolism. These findings establish a mechanistic link between sleep disorders and impaired fertility, highlighting S100A8/A9-driven M1 polarization and pyroptosis as critical therapeutic targets for preserving ovarian reserve and reproductive function in sleep-deprived individuals.

title: Cell‐Free DNA‐Based Theranostics for Inflammatory Disorders
summary: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) acts as both a biomarker and pathogenic driver in inflammatory diseases, orchestrating innate and adaptive immune responses through multiple signaling pathways. Upon release from damaged or dead cells, cfDNA activates DNA sensors including TLR9 and the cGAS-STING pathway, triggering downstream activation of NF-κB, Type I interferon responses, and inflammasome assembly that collectively drive production of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-1β, IL-18, TNF-α, and IL-6. cfDNA also forms a positive feedback loop with reactive oxygen species (ROS), amplifying inflammatory responses while simultaneously modulating coagulation through interactions with coagulation factors and fibrin, thereby inhibiting fibrinolysis. In inflammatory bowel disease and rheumatoid arthritis, elevated serum or synovial fluid cfDNA levels directly correlate with disease severity and expression of key inflammatory markers, establishing cfDNA as both a disease indicator and active contributor to pathogenesis. While current treatments using NSAIDs, glucocorticoids, immunosuppressive agents, and biologics effectively reduce inflammation, their long-term use causes significant adverse effects including drug resistance, gastrointestinal discomfort, liver and kidney injury, and osteoporosis. These findings position cfDNA-targeted therapeutics as a promising alternative approach for managing inflammatory disorders by directly addressing a central upstream driver of disease pathogenesis.

title: TRPM3 activation causes CGRP release in trigeminal neurons: Implications for migraine mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that TRPM3 channel activation in trigeminal neurons triggers calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) release, a key mechanism implicated in migraine pathophysiology. TRPM3 is colocalized with CGRP in trigeminal ganglia and vascular tissues including the dura mater and middle cerebral artery, where activation by the agonist CIM0216 induces both CGRP release and vasodilation through elevated intracellular calcium. The endogenous TRPM3 ligand pregnenolone sulfate, combined with hormonal modulation by estrogen and progesterone, may link TRPM3 activation to menstrual migraine patterns, with evidence suggesting elevated TRPM3 expression in endometriosis patients. These findings position TRPM3 as a novel therapeutic target for migraine, supported by the development of the selective TRPM3 antagonist BHV-2100, which completed Phase I clinical trials without significant adverse events, offering an alternative approach to existing CGRP receptor antagonist therapies.

title: Neuroendocrine regulation of the reproductive system: integration of research of neuropeptide and experimental pathophysiology of amenorrhea of different genesis (literature review)
summary:
# Summary

This literature review examines neuroendocrine regulation of reproductive function through neuropeptide signaling pathways, particularly focusing on kisspeptin and brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF). Kisspeptin, synthesized by KNDy neurons in the hypothalamus, acts as a central regulator of reproductive function by activating GnRH neurons, with its activity modulated by neurokinin B (activating) and dynorphin (inhibiting). Kisspeptin is significantly reduced in functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, a condition characterized by disrupted GnRH secretion, stress-induced suppression of Kiss1 gene expression via corticotropin-releasing hormone and glucocorticoids, and impaired fertility. Conversely, kisspeptin is elevated in polycystic ovary syndrome, where it may contribute to hormonal imbalance and anovulation. Beyond central regulation, kisspeptin and its receptor KISS1R are expressed locally in ovarian follicles, where they support oocyte maturation through intraovarian signaling. BDNF, another critical neuropeptide produced by granulosa cells, enhances oocyte quality, increases first polar body extrusion, and reduces aneuploidy through ERK1/2 pathway activation; however, BDNF levels are reduced in functional hypothalamic amenorrhea, primary ovarian insufficiency, and aging, with maternal placental BDNF depletion during pregnancy compromising offspring ovarian reserve through germ cell apoptosis.

title: Integrative network toxicology and experimental evidence reveal mechanisms underlying diethyl phthalate-induced initiation and progression of endometrial cancer
summary: This study reveals that diethyl phthalate (DEP), an endocrine-disrupting chemical, promotes endometrial cancer initiation and progression through oxidative stress-mediated activation of pro-proliferative signaling pathways. DEP exposure induces oxidative stress and enhances phosphorylation of ERK1/2 and AKT kinases, which subsequently upregulate Cyclin D1 and CDK4 to promote G1/S phase cell cycle transition and endometrial cancer cell proliferation. Integrative network toxicology identified five core DEP-responsive genes—FOS, NR4A1, ADRA2C, JUN, and SLC6A2—that molecular simulations confirmed directly bind DEP and are associated with endometrial cancer risk. The study identified nineteen overlapping DEP-endometrial cancer genes predominantly enriched in MAPK, cAMP, and cGMP-PKG signaling pathways, implicating multiple cell proliferation and metabolic signaling networks in DEP-induced carcinogenesis. These findings mechanistically connect phthalate exposure to endometrial cancer through oxidative stress-mediated signaling crosstalk and accelerated cell cycle progression, complementing epidemiological evidence linking endometrial cancer to prolonged estrogen exposure, obesity, and metabolic syndrome. The results establish phthalates as environmental risk factors for endometrial cancer via conserved MAPK and PI3K/AKT proliferation signaling.

title: Development of a novel NanoBRET high-throughput drug screening assay for human GnRH receptor using sulfo-cyanine 5 fluorophore
summary:
# Summary

This study develops a novel NanoBRET high-throughput screening assay for the human gonadotropin-releasing hormone receptor (GnRHR) using sulfo-cyanine 5 (sCy5) as a fluorophore. The researchers engineered a GnRH ligand by substituting glycine 6 with sCy5-conjugated D-lysine (sCy5-D-Lys6-GnRH), which stabilizes the critical βII' turn configuration of the peptide while maintaining high receptor binding affinity and demonstrating low non-specific binding. The assay construct combines NanoLuciferase fused to the GnRH receptor's N-terminus with an IL6 secretory signal peptide and K191 deletion, producing optimal NanoBRET signals for receptor ligand binding. The NanoBRET-based assay offers significant advantages over traditional radioligand binding methods, including improved safety, enhanced sensitivity, convenience, and cost-effectiveness while eliminating radioactive disposal concerns. GnRH receptor signaling is physiologically critical, regulating reproduction through G protein coupling while also modulating endocrine, immunological, and aging processes, making this screening platform valuable for developing novel therapeutics targeting hormone-dependent diseases and reproductive disorders.

title: Reviewing the Peripheral and Central Mechanisms of Visceral Hypersensitivity in Intestinal Disorders
summary:
# Summary

Visceral hypersensitivity is a major pathophysiological mechanism underlying abdominal pain in irritable bowel syndrome and inflammatory bowel disease, characterized by altered gastrointestinal environments and mucosal inflammation respectively. Peripheral sensory neurons detect multiple stimuli through diverse receptor families: transient receptor potential channels and mechanoreceptors (Piezo1/2, voltage-gated sodium channels) sense thermal, chemical, and mechanical stimuli, while purinergic receptors, toll-like receptor 4, protease-activated receptor 2, and serotonin receptors detect chemical signals including ATP, glutamate, and trypsin-3 released from enterocytes and enteric endocrine cells. These stimuli activate dorsal root ganglia neurons and trigger pain sensitization through converging pathways: ATP-P2X3 signaling upregulates TRPV1, glutamate promotes release of substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide, and trypsin-3 activates PAR2 on sensory neurons. Additionally, enterocyte-derived IL-33 activates mast cells through ST2 receptors, and activated mast cells disrupt the intestinal barrier while secreting pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-4, IL-5, IL-6, TNF-α), amplifying visceral hypersensitivity. Elevated serotonin and mast cell numbers in IBS and IBD patients support this multi-mechanism pathophysiology underlying intestinal pain disorders.

title: Stromal cell-derived itaconate promotes endometriosis via macrophage NRF2 and lysosomal pH modulation
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by immune dysregulation driven by a pathogenic metabolic-immune axis wherein ectopic endometrial stromal cells upregulate ACOD1 to produce excessive itaconate, which is subsequently taken up by peritoneal macrophages. Once internalized, itaconate activates NRF2 signaling and enhances lysosomal acidification through NOX2 inhibition, both of which suppress pro-inflammatory macrophage functions including phagocytosis and cytotoxic activity. Enhanced lysosomal acidification reduces calcium release, thereby inhibiting p38-MAPK signaling and further attenuating pro-inflammatory gene expression. Blocking ACOD1 or preventing itaconate accumulation restores macrophage immunosurveillance capacity and reduces endometrial lesion burden, while exogenous itaconate administration aggravates disease progression. These findings define the "ESC-ACOD1-itaconate-macrophage" axis as a critical mechanism of immunosuppression in endometriosis and suggest that targeting this metabolic pathway may offer therapeutic benefits for affected patients.

title: Proinflammatory GSDMD activation in live macrophages and DLBCL cells marks cognate interactions and better prognosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that cleaved gasdermin D (GSDMD), activated through inflammasome signaling, plays a pivotal role in immune cell function and lymphoma prognosis. Caspase-mediated cleavage of GSDMD releases its N-terminal fragment, which forms membrane pores leading to release of proinflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, driving pyroptosis and immune activation. Notably, cytoplasmic GSDMD-N expression in diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) correlates with significantly better overall and progression-free survival, and associates with increased CD38+ M1 macrophages, lower PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint expression, and active cognate interactions between macrophages and both lymphoma and T cells. In contrast, nuclear GSDMD localization predicts poor prognosis and correlates with reduced CD8+ T-cell infiltration and lymphoma cell resistance to death. Additionally, GSDME, expressed in vascular endothelium, associates with adverse outcomes, while GSDMC promotes chemoresistance through AKT/mTOR pathway activation, highlighting context-dependent roles for different gasdermin family members in cancer immunity and progression.

title: FSH, bone, belly and brain
summary:
# Summary: FSH Beyond Reproduction—Systemic Effects on Bone, Metabolism, and Inflammation

Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) exerts far-reaching physiological effects beyond its classical reproductive role through FSH receptor (FSHR) expression in diverse extragonadal tissues including bone, adipose tissue, immune cells, and the brain. In adipose tissue, FSH binding to FSHR triggers distinct signaling that suppresses energy expenditure by coupling to Gαi proteins, decreasing cAMP, and inhibiting thermogenic pathways mediated by UCP1, while simultaneously promoting lipid synthesis through upregulation of PPARγ, fatty acid synthase, and lipoprotein lipase. Conversely, FSH blockade enhances mitochondrial biogenesis, UCP1 expression, and energy expenditure while reducing fat mass and waist circumference, as supported by human cohort studies showing positive correlations between circulating FSH levels and adiposity measures, BMI, and bone marrow adiposity. FSH also activates pro-inflammatory signaling in immune cells by promoting TNFα and IL-1β expression in monocytes and macrophages, contributing to atherogenesis and cardiovascular disease risk markers including increased carotid intima-media thickness and aortic plaque formation in perimenopausal women. These findings establish FSH as a systemic metabolic and inflammatory regulator with therapeutic implications for managing postmenopausal metabolic dysfunction and related cardiovascular complications through FSH pathway antagonism.

title: TRPV1 and TRPA1 channels exhibit bifurcated sensing of singlet oxygen and hydrogen peroxide
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals bifurcated sensing mechanisms whereby singlet oxygen and hydrogen peroxide differentially regulate TRPV1 and TRPA1 ion channels. Singlet oxygen, generated by photosensitizers like rose Bengal or the engineered protein SOPP3 under light exposure, potently activates TRPV1 through oxidation of histidine residue H167 in its N-terminal ankyrin repeat domain, accelerating channel opening kinetics and left-shifting voltage-dependent activation. In stark contrast, singlet oxygen causes transient activation followed by permanent inhibition of TRPA1. Hydrogen peroxide shows the opposite pattern: it robustly activates TRPA1 primarily through cysteine oxidation in the channel's N-terminus, while only moderately enhancing TRPV1 function as a potentiator of other stimuli like heat and capsaicin. These findings demonstrate that reactive oxygen species achieve stimulus-specific regulation of TRP channels through distinct molecular targets and mechanisms, with implications for understanding redox signaling in cellular processes ranging from pain sensation to immune responses.

title: Is it time to think about chronotherapy in migraine
summary:
# Summary

Circadian rhythm dysregulation plays a critical role in migraine pathophysiology, with the suprachiasmatic nucleus and core clock genes (CLOCK, BMAL1, PER, CRY) forming the molecular foundation of this relationship. Over 50% of migraine patients demonstrate circadian patterns of attack onset that correlate with individual chronotypes, where early chronotypes experience early morning attacks while late chronotypes experience late-day attacks, and disruptions to sleep-wake cycles significantly increase attack risk. Genetic variations in clock-regulating enzymes, particularly loss-of-function mutations in Casein Kinase 1 Delta (CK1δ), are associated with advanced sleep phases and migraine with aura, indicating direct mechanistic links between circadian dysregulation and migraine susceptibility. The trigeminal ganglion contains an intrinsic peripheral clock whose circadian gene expression is disrupted by chronic nitroglycerin exposure, and existing migraine therapeutics (ergotamine, triptans, verapamil, lithium, propranolol) exert effects on clock gene expression and amplitude. These findings suggest that chronotherapy—timing medication administration and light exposure according to individual circadian phenotypes—represents a promising therapeutic approach to optimize migraine prevention and acute treatment efficacy.

title: Human IGF1 pro-forms induce breast cancer cell proliferation via the IGF1 receptor
summary:
# Summary

IGF1 pro-forms, generated through alternative splicing of the IGF1 gene (producing IGF1Ea, IGF1Eb, and IGF1Ec variants), potently induce breast cancer cell proliferation through activation of the IGF1 receptor independently of mature IGF1. Although IGF1 receptor is overexpressed in approximately 90% of breast cancers and elevated circulating IGF1 levels correlate with increased cancer risk, this study demonstrates that IGF1 pro-forms activate IGF1R phosphorylation and promote MCF7 and ZR751 breast cancer cell proliferation despite being only partially recognized by anti-IGF1 antibodies that completely neutralize mature IGF1. The E-peptide attached to pro-forms masks epitopes recognized by standard anti-IGF1 antibodies, explaining why these pro-forms evade antibody inhibition; however, direct IGF1R neutralization completely blocks pro-form-induced proliferation, confirming IGF1R-dependent signaling. Post-translational modifications, particularly glycosylation of IGF1Ea, and furin-mediated cleavage of E-peptides regulate the balance between pro-form and mature IGF1 production. These findings reveal that IGF1 splice variants represent functionally distinct signaling entities in breast cancer, suggesting that current therapeutic strategies targeting mature IGF1 may fail to suppress the oncogenic activity of IGF1 pro-forms present in the tumor microenvironment.

title: TRPM3 mediates spontaneous pain and mechanical allodynia in a mouse model of chronic orofacial neuropathy
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that TRPM3, a heat-activated cation channel expressed in somatosensory and nociceptive neurons, is essential for both spontaneous pain and mechanical allodynia in a mouse model of chronic orofacial neuropathy induced by trigeminal nerve injury. TRPM3 knockout mice and wild-type mice treated with TRPM3 antagonists (primidone and isosakuranetin) showed marked suppression of pain behaviors, whereas TRPV1 antagonists had minimal effects, highlighting TRPM3's distinct role in neuropathic pain mechanisms. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that while trigeminal nerve injury induces robust changes in gene expression related to axonogenesis and ion channel regulation, these transcriptional responses occur independently of TRPM3, suggesting that TRPM3 mediates pain through post-transcriptional mechanisms. These findings identify TRPM3 as a promising therapeutic target for treating trigeminal neuralgia and other neuropathic pain conditions.

title: Nano-Enabled Fluorescence Switching: A Novel Strategy for PDGFRβ Detection and TKI Therapy Monitoring
summary:
# Summary

PDGFRβ functions as a transmembrane tyrosine kinase receptor that becomes abnormally upregulated following tyrosine kinase inhibitor (TKI) treatment, contributing to acquired TKI resistance in cancers including glioblastoma and non-small cell lung cancer. Upon activation by growth factors, PDGFRβ initiates intracellular signaling cascades through the PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK pathways, promoting tumor proliferation, migration, angiogenesis, metastasis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. The authors developed a novel fluorescence-switching nanoprobe (Cy3-Gint4.T@BPNSs) composed of a PDGFRβ-specific aptamer conjugated to black phosphorus nanosheets that dynamically detects PDGFRβ expression levels with high specificity and sensitivity, enabling real-time monitoring of TKI resistance mechanisms. In glioblastoma cells, both erlotinib and gefitinib substantially upregulated PDGFRβ expression (65-69%), while PDGFRβ knockdown reversed TKI resistance and significantly enhanced drug efficacy. These findings establish PDGFRβ as a critical mediator of TKI resistance and demonstrate that the nanoprobe platform can serve as a diagnostic and prognostic tool for tracking resistance-related alterations during cancer therapy.

title: Ezrin, radixin, and moesin are novel citrullinated proteins in the decidua during pregnancy
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies sphingolipid metabolism as a critical regulator of decidualization and pregnancy success through control of neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation. Sphingosine kinases (Sphk1 and Sphk2) synthesize sphingosine-1-phosphate, which activates S1P receptors and regulates immunity and inflammation; mice deficient in these kinases exhibit impaired decidualization, elevated neutrophil chemoattractants (CXCL1/CXCL2), and excessive NET formation at the fetomaternal interface leading to early pregnancy loss. The study reveals that peptidylarginine deiminase 4 (PADI4) mediates histone citrullination required for NET formation, and PADI4 inhibition partially restores normal pregnancy outcomes in deficient mice. Notably, ezrin, radixin, and moesin (ERM) proteins—which regulate cell polarity, adhesion, and migration—undergo citrullination exclusively in the decidua during pregnancy and are regulated by both sphingolipid signaling and PAD-mediated citrullination, representing novel citrullinated proteins in pregnancy. These findings establish a mechanistic link between sphingolipid metabolism, PAD-mediated protein modification, NET regulation, and successful decidualization, with implications for understanding pregnancy complications and potentially endometriosis-related pathology.

title: ChemR23 prevents phenotypic switching of vascular smooth muscle cells into macrophage-like foam cells in atherosclerosis
summary:
# Summary

ChemR23, a chemokine-like receptor expressed predominantly in contractile vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs), prevents atherosclerotic progression by maintaining the contractile VSMC phenotype and restraining dedifferentiation into synthetic, macrophage-like foam cells. ChemR23 deficiency in apolipoprotein E-deficient mice leads to increased atherosclerotic lesion size, enhanced VSMC proliferation, and elevated foam cell formation through downregulation of contractile genes and upregulation of macrophage markers (CD68, LGALS3). Chemerin-9 (C9), the endogenous ligand of ChemR23, exerts protective effects by activating AKT1/2 signaling and reducing inflammatory burden, while the small molecule ChemR23 antagonist α-NETA promotes pathogenic phenotypic switching by activating ERK1/2 signaling, increasing KLF4 expression, and disrupting cholesterol homeostasis through suppression of efflux transporters (ABCA1, ABCG1, SR-BI) and upregulation of the scavenger receptor CD36. Co-treatment with C9 mitigates α-NETA effects by restoring expression of cholesterol efflux machinery and blocking VSMC proliferation, suggesting ChemR23 represents a dual-targeting therapeutic option for atherosclerosis through either receptor activation or macrophage polarization modulation.

title: CGRP expression and signaling sensitization in a mouse model of chronic oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that oxaliplatin, a platinum-based chemotherapy agent, induces chronic peripheral neuropathy through oxidative modification and sensitization of TRPA1 ion channels in dorsal root ganglion neurons, rather than through increased CGRP expression. Oxaliplatin generates reactive oxygen species that oxidize TRPA1 channels, lowering their temperature threshold for activation and promoting chronic calcium influx and CGRP release from sensory neurons without elevating CGRP mRNA or protein levels. The resulting TRPA1-mediated sensitization pathway—characterized by oxidation-induced channel opening, calcium-dependent neuropeptide exocytosis, and sustained CGRP signaling—mechanistically underlies the prominent cold allodynia and reduced nerve conduction velocity observed in oxaliplatin-treated mice. Notably, Ramp1, a key component of the functional CGRP receptor complex, was specifically upregulated in dorsal root ganglia, suggesting that enhanced receptor sensitivity rather than ligand abundance drives pain sensitization in this chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy model. These findings reveal a signaling sensitization mechanism distinct from traditional upregulation models and identify TRPA1 channel oxidation as a potential therapeutic target for chemotherapy-induced neuropathic pain.

title: Anabolic–Androgenic Steroids Revisited: Structural Biology, Receptor Signaling, and Mechanisms of Anabolic–Androgenic Dissociation
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the structural biology and signaling mechanisms of steroid hormones, which orchestrate diverse physiological processes including reproductive function, metabolic homeostasis, immune regulation, and neural development through interactions with specific receptor proteins. Steroid hormone signaling operates through both classical genomic pathways, where ligand-bound receptors like the estrogen receptor and glucocorticoid receptor translocate to the nucleus to modulate gene expression, and rapid non-genomic signaling cascades involving G-protein-coupled receptors, phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase, and mitogen-activated protein kinases. The article details the structural features and functional specificity of five major steroid hormone classes: estrogens (regulating reproduction, bone metabolism, and cardiovascular health), androgens (controlling male sexual development and muscle/bone mass), progestins (maintaining pregnancy), glucocorticoids (regulating stress responses and inflammation), and mineralocorticoids (managing electrolyte homeostasis and blood pressure). Structural modifications at the C17 position critically modulate androgen receptor affinity, metabolic stability, and pharmacokinetics, while endogenous androgens like dihydrotestosterone exhibit higher receptor binding affinity than testosterone. These mechanistic insights into steroid hormone signaling provide a foundation for understanding both normal physiology and potential therapeutic applications in hormone-dependent conditions.

title: Complement, Inflammasome, and Microglial Crosstalk in Glaucoma: From Neurodegeneration to Immune-Based Precision Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Glaucoma is a neurodegenerative disease driven by a convergent inflammatory cascade involving complement activation, NLRP3 inflammasome signaling, and microglial reactivity that collectively promote retinal ganglion cell dysfunction and death. Local complement proteins (C1q, C3, C5) are upregulated in the retina and optic nerve head by resident glial cells (microglia, astrocytes, Müller glia) and drive maladaptive synaptic pruning, phagoptosis, and membrane attack complex-mediated cellular stress. Multiple danger signals—including biomechanical strain, ischemia, mitochondrial damage, and oxidative stress—activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, which triggers caspase-1-dependent maturation of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, further disrupting synapses and axonal transport. Microglia integrate these inflammatory signals through bidirectional crosstalk with complement and NLRP3, converting acute episodic stress into chronic maladaptive states characterized by excessive pruning, persistent cytokine release, and amplified inflammatory output. This interconnected immune axis represents a rational target for precision therapeutics aimed at selective pathway inhibition to halt the progression of glaucomatous neurodegeneration.

title: Obesity and Cancer: From Systemic Metabolic Reprogramming to Immunotherapy Paradox
summary:
# Summary

Obesity promotes tumorigenesis through coordinated metabolic, hormonal, and inflammatory reprogramming. Hyperinsulinemia and elevated free insulin-like growth factor (IGF) activate the PI3K–AKT–mTOR and RAS/MAPK pathways, licensing cell cycle progression and transformation, while adipokine imbalance (elevated leptin and reduced adiponectin) perpetuates these pro-proliferative signals through JAK2-STAT3 and continuous mTOR activation. Obesity-associated inflammation upregulates aromatase expression in adipose stromal cells, increasing local estrogen exposure and further promoting hormone-responsive tumorigenesis. Circadian desynchrony compounds these effects by elevating reactive oxygen species and disrupting clock gene interactions with cell cycle checkpoints, thereby promoting tumor development and increasing genomic instability. Additionally, altered leptin signaling impairs anti-tumor T cell function through PD-1 upregulation, creating an immunotherapy paradox. These interconnected metabolic, endocrine, and immune dysfunctions establish obesity as a systemic risk factor across multiple cancer types.

title: Danggui Buxue Decoction and Its Active Constituents Inhibit Drug-Induced Uterine Contractions via L-Type Calcium Channels and the IP3/Ca2+ Pathway
summary: Danggui Buxue Decoction (DBD), a traditional Chinese herbal formula composed of Astragalus membranaceus and Angelica sinensis, effectively treats primary dysmenorrhea by inhibiting uterine contractions through dual suppression of calcium signaling pathways. The decoction and its eight active constituents—particularly quercetin, calycosin, and ligustilide—block both L-type calcium channels (reducing extracellular calcium influx) and IP3 receptors (blocking intracellular calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum). These inhibitions disrupt the oxytocin/PGF2α-GPCR-PLC-IP3 cascade that normally triggers the calcium-calmodulin-MLCK pathway essential for myosin light chain phosphorylation and smooth muscle contraction. In vivo studies demonstrated that DBD and quercetin alleviated pain, reduced inflammation by downregulating TNFα and IL-6 expression, and decreased uterine calcium and IP3 levels in dysmenorrheic mice. The mechanism directly addresses the pathophysiology of dysmenorrhea, wherein excessive uterine contractions cause ischemia and hypoxia-induced pain, making DBD a potential complementary alternative to NSAIDs for menstrual pain management.

title: Mechanistic insights and therapeutic potential of sphingosine-1-phosphate in the development of pulmonary fibrosis (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Sphingosine-1-phosphate (S1P), a bioactive sphingolipid produced primarily by erythrocytes and platelets, acts as a critical intercellular signaling molecule that binds to five S1P receptors (S1PR1-5) to regulate cell proliferation, survival, migration, angiogenesis, and inflammation. S1P synthesis occurs through a stepwise enzymatic pathway involving sphingomyelin hydrolysis to ceramide, conversion to sphingosine, and phosphorylation by the rate-limiting enzymes SphK1 and SphK2, with SphK1 promoting cell survival while SphK2 induces apoptosis. In pulmonary fibrosis, excessive collagen and extracellular matrix deposition occur through pathological mechanisms involving elevated S1P levels in lung tissue and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, where S1P promotes synthesis of pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic mediators including interleukin-1β and transforming growth factor-β (TGF-β). S1PR1 activation can suppress TGF-β-mediated Smad 2/3 and RhoA/ROCK1 signaling while enhancing vascular barrier integrity, whereas S1PR2 and S1PR3 activation of PI3K/Akt and ERK1/2 pathways drives fibroblast proliferation, with S1PR3 also promoting M2 macrophage polarization that secretes TGF-β to further stimulate fibroblast activation. Selective SphK1 silencing effectively reduces endogenous S1P levels and attenuates bleomycin-induced pulmonary fibrosis severity, highlighting the therapeutic potential of targeting

title: Pathogenic homozygous PLCZ1 mutation reduces sperm protein levels with a simulated reduction of PIP2 binding: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a homozygous PLCZ1 R385Q mutation in an infertile male patient that impairs the critical sperm-specific phospholipase C zeta (PLCζ) protein required for oocyte activation. PLCζ normally triggers calcium oscillations in the oocyte by hydrolyzing phosphatidylinositol 4,5-bisphosphate (PIP2) into inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3), which releases calcium from endoplasmic reticulum stores—a process essential for fertilization. The identified R385Q mutation, located in the Y domain of PLCζ's active site at a highly conserved amino acid position, disrupts the local protein fold and critically reduces PIP2 binding interactions, as demonstrated through structural modeling and docking analyses. Consequently, the patient's sperm exhibited severely reduced PLCζ protein levels compared to controls, resulting in fertilization failure despite intracytoplasmic sperm injection during IVF. This case emphasizes that pathogenic PLCZ1 variants represent an important molecular cause of male factor infertility and oocyte activation deficiency, with potential diagnostic and therapeutic implications for affected couples, including rescue possibilities through assisted oocyte activation or PLCζ RNA injection.

title: Neuroinflammation and Disease: Pathways and Opportunities
journal: Annual review of pharmacology and toxicology
summary:
# Neuroinflammation Pathways and Therapeutic Targets in Disease

Neuroinflammation, orchestrated primarily by astrocytes and microglia with contributions from infiltrating monocytes, precedes and likely causes the onset of major neurodegenerative and neurological diseases including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and epilepsy. The EP2 prostanoid receptor emerges as a central driver of this pathology: EP2 activation elevates during neuroinflammation and exacerbates proinflammatory cytokine production in activated microglia through adenylyl cyclase-cAMP-PKA signaling and β-arrestin pathways, while simultaneously creating a positive feedback loop by upregulating COX-2 and amplifying PGE2 production. The CCR2 chemokine receptor complements EP2 signaling by regulating peripheral immune cell recruitment to inflamed brain tissue, with CCL2-stimulated monocytes activating the JAK2/STAT3 pathway to propagate neuroinflammatory cascades. Notably, both EP2 deletion and antagonism reduce oxidative damage, amyloid-beta burden, and neuroinflammation in disease models, while CCR2 antagonism blocks inflammatory monocyte infiltration into the brain. These findings position EP2 and CCR2 as promising pharmaceutical targets, with clinical evidence suggesting that blocking these pathways could attenuate neurodegeneration and delay disease progression across multiple neurological conditions including endometriosis-associated neuroinflammation.

title: Expression Analysis of the orphan receptors GPR161, GPR132, GPR20, and GPR139 in patients with cervicitis and low-grade, and high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions
summary:
# Summary

This study examined expression patterns of four orphan G protein-coupled receptors (GPR161, GPR132, GPR20, and GPR139) in cervical tissue samples from patients with cervicitis, low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (LSIL), and high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL). Cancer-associated chronic inflammation activates key transcription factors (NF-κB, STAT, and FOXO) that regulate expression of these orphan receptors, which have been implicated in various malignancies including breast cancer metastasis and gastrointestinal stromal tumors. The study found that GPR132 and GPR20 expression significantly increased in LSIL samples, while GPR139 expression was reduced in LSIL, with GPR161 showing no significant variation across groups. These differential expression patterns suggest that upregulated GPR132 and GPR20 may promote cervical lesion progression through the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway, whereas diminished GPR139 expression correlates with increased disease severity. The findings indicate that orphan GPCRs, regulated by inflammatory signaling pathways, may serve as biomarkers or therapeutic targets in cervical intraepithelial neoplasia development.

title: β-casomorphin-7: a review of occurrence, identification, techno-functionality, and effects on human health
summary: β-casomorphin-7 (βCM-7) is a bioactive peptide released during digestion of the A1 variant of β-casein, which is predominantly found in milk from Northern European cattle breeds such as Holsteins and Friesians. The peptide is generated through proteolytic digestion by pepsin, pancreatic proteases, and pancreatic elastase, with a critical tyrosine residue at its N-terminus enabling its opioid-like activity through binding to μ-opioid receptors involved in pain and reward regulation. The A1/A2 polymorphism in the β-casein gene, caused by histidine substituting proline, confers greater proteolytic susceptibility to the A1 variant, resulting in significantly higher βCM-7 release compared to the A2 variant found in milk from Jersey cattle, goats, and camel. βCM-7 has been identified in various dairy products including fermented milk drinks, cheeses, and infant formulas, with levels modulated by processing conditions such as heat treatment, fermentation time, and cheese ripening. While the peptide demonstrates potential benefits for immune and gastrointestinal health, concerns persist regarding its capacity to cross the blood-brain barrier and its association with gastrointestinal discomfort, particularly in sensitive individuals. Selective breeding toward A2 β-casein variants represents a viable strategy for reducing βCM-7 content in commercial milk supplies.

title: Attack of the kinases: JNK signaling in metabolism
journal: American journal of physiology. Regulatory, integrative and comparative physiology
summary:
# Summary

This review examines c-Jun N-terminal kinase (JNK) signaling as a central mechanism linking inflammatory stress to metabolic dysfunction in obesity and related disorders. High-fat diet feeding drives hypothalamic neuroinflammation through multiple pathways: saturated fatty acids like palmitic acid promote ceramide synthesis, which triggers endoplasmic reticulum stress and disrupts leptin and insulin signaling in the hypothalamus. Proinflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-α cross the blood-brain barrier and activate JNK through transforming growth factor-β activated kinase 1 (TAK1), leading to phosphorylation of c-Jun and activation of the AP-1 transcription factor complex that amplifies proinflammatory gene expression. JNK1 and JNK2 are ubiquitously expressed and play critical roles in systemic energy homeostasis, regulating hypothalamic control of glucose metabolism, adipogenesis, thermogenesis, and immune function through coordinated action of the hypothalamus-pituitary-thyroid and hypothalamus-pituitary-adrenal axes. Additionally, JNK signaling controls reproductive processes including oocyte meiotic resumption, granulosa cell proliferation, and germline development, establishing JNK as a key integrator linking metabolic and reproductive dysfunction in obesity.

title: Molecular mechanisms of NLRP3 inflammasome activation
summary:
# Summary

The NLRP3 inflammasome is a multiprotein signaling complex that orchestrates innate immune responses through a well-characterized two-signal activation mechanism. Signal 1, initiated by toll-like receptors or cytokine receptors, induces expression of NLRP3 and pro-inflammatory cytokines, while signal 2 promotes inflammasome assembly through diverse mechanisms including potassium efflux via the TWIK2 channel, ATP-mediated P2X7 receptor activation, mitochondrial dysfunction-derived reactive oxygen species and mtDNA release, and calcium influx. Upon activation, the NLRP3 inflammasome recruits and activates caspase-1, which cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature forms and triggers gasdermin D cleavage, leading to pyroptotic cell death and robust inflammatory responses. Metabolic rewiring, particularly enhanced glycolysis and succinate accumulation, fuels inflammasome activation by stabilizing HIF-1α and promoting IL-1β production, while metabolic inhibitors like itaconate and fumarate suppress activation through distinct mechanisms. Dysregulated inflammasome activation is implicated in numerous inflammatory, autoimmune, and degenerative diseases, making the NLRP3 complex a critical therapeutic target for conditions including endometriosis where uncontrolled inflammatory signaling drives pathophysiology.

title: Mechanistic insights into cartilage-sensory nerve crosstalk in osteoarthritis progression
summary: This study elucidates the bidirectional crosstalk between cartilage and sensory nerves in osteoarthritis pathogenesis, revealing how mechanical damage and inflammatory signals initiate a self-perpetuating cycle of pain and degradation. Degraded cartilage and synovial tissues release cytokines, neurotrophic factors, and lipid mediators that activate sensory nerves and promote nerve sprouting through nerve growth factor and pro-inflammatory cytokines. Activated sensory afferents subsequently release neuropeptides—particularly substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP)—and inflammatory mediators that amplify joint inflammation and further enhance cartilage degradation. At the molecular level, substance P binds neurokinin-1 receptors to activate mitogen-activated protein kinases and nuclear factor-κB signaling, upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines and reactive oxygen species while suppressing chondrocyte survival and function. CGRP similarly suppresses anabolic cartilage markers while promoting inflammatory and catabolic gene expression, with preclinical evidence demonstrating that CGRP knockout or inhibition via receptor antagonists like Rimegepant can attenuate OA progression. This integrated network of inflammatory mediators, neuropeptides, and mechanosensitive ion channels drives persistent OA pain while mechanically linking biomechanical stress to nociceptive signaling, establishing nerve-cartilage interactions as a critical therapeutic target.

title: Versatile SMAD2 and SMAD3 epitope–tagged mouse models for genomic profiling of TGFβ signaling: Uncovering GDF9–SMAD2/3 targets
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
summary: This study characterizes GDF9-SMAD2/3 signaling in ovarian granulosa cells using epitope-tagged mouse models for TGFβ pathway profiling. GDF9, secreted by oocytes, activates SMAD2 and SMAD3 through a receptor complex containing ALK4/ALK5 and BMPR2/ACVR2A/B, with phosphorylated SMAD2/3 forming complexes with SMAD4 that translocate to the nucleus to regulate gene expression. The study identified direct GDF9-SMAD2/3 target genes including Ptgs2, Ptx3, Has2, and Tnfaip6, all of which promote cumulus expansion and are essential for folliculogenesis, while GDF9 signaling also upregulates let-7 microRNAs involved in oocyte maturation and granulosa cell expansion. Beyond cumulus expansion, GDF9-SMAD2/3 signaling coordinates broader cellular processes including extracellular matrix remodeling, cell-fate transitions, and suppression of competing pathways like activin signaling, while recruiting transcriptional regulators that control cellular growth, differentiation, and steroidogenesis. The genomic toolkit established here provides in vivo genetic models for studying TGFβ signaling and reveals how GDF9-SMAD2/3 signaling integrates multiple biological programs critical for female fertility and ovarian function, with mutations in GDF9 implicated in infertility, polycystic ovary syndrome, and primary ovarian insufficiency in humans.

title: The Inflammasome–miR Axis in Alzheimer’s Disease and Chronic Pain: Molecular Mechanisms and Therapeutic Opportunities
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the inflammasome-microRNA axis as a critical regulatory pathway linking neuroinflammation in Alzheimer's disease and chronic pain. The NLRP3 inflammasome, activated by amyloid-β accumulation, assembles via ASC-mediated recruitment of caspase-1, which then cleaves pro-IL-1β and pro-IL-18 into their mature inflammatory forms that drive both neuroinflammation and pain sensitization. Multiple microRNAs (miR-22, miR-34a, miR-146a, miR-155, and miR-223) modulate this cascade and influence nociceptive sensitivity, while specific miRs such as miR-9a-5p and miR-672-5p can suppress individual inflammasome components like NLRP1 and AIM2. The article highlights dysfunction of the locus coeruleus-noradrenergic system as a convergent mechanism in both conditions, whereby reduced norepinephrine impairs blood-brain barrier integrity, potentiates microglial activation, and aberrant miR expression further compromises LC-NE signaling. Therapeutic opportunities include NLRP1 and caspase-1 inhibition to improve autophagy, amyloid-β clearance, and cognitive function, while microRNA-targeted interventions may provide an additional strategy to modulate inflammasome-driven pathology in both neurodegenerative and pain-related disorders.

title: Tetrabromobisphenol A: MAK Value Documentation – Translation of the German version from 2023
journal: The MAK Collection for Occupational Health and Safety
summary: Tetrabromobisphenol A (TBBPA) exhibits multifaceted endocrine and immune toxicological effects relevant to reproductive and systemic health. The compound acts as a potent agonist of PPARγ and promotes GPER expression in multiple tissues, including the endometrium and uterus, where it stimulates cell proliferation through GPER-mediated mechanisms, particularly in ovarian carcinoma cells. TBBPA disrupts estrogen metabolism by inhibiting sulfotransferases (SULT1E1 and SULT1A1) and binding to transthyretin, leading to elevated estradiol levels and reduced thyroid hormone availability; it also suppresses thyroid function by decreasing total thyroxine concentrations. The chemical induces robust oxidative stress through multiple pathways, including increased reactive oxygen species production, mitochondrial dysfunction, and alterations in antioxidant markers such as reduced glutathione and elevated malondialdehyde levels. Long-term exposure causes organ-specific tumorigenic and toxic effects, including uterine adenocarcinoma in female rats and hepatoblastomas and hemangiosarcomas in mice, alongside significant immunosuppression characterized by impaired antiviral immunity and altered inflammatory signaling through downregulation of immune response genes including Tlr4 and Ccl6.

title: Specific inhibition of NLRP3 inflammasome by a Smurf1 inhibitor in vitro and in vivo
summary:
# Summary

A01, a Smurf1 inhibitor, selectively suppresses NLRP3 inflammasome activation by disrupting the NLRP3-ASC interaction and preventing ASC oligomerization, thereby blocking downstream signaling events including caspase-1 cleavage and IL-1β maturation and secretion. In vitro studies demonstrate that A01 dose-dependently inhibits inflammasome activation triggered by ATP and nigericin while remaining selective for NLRP3, having no effect on AIM2 or NLRC4 inflammasomes and showing comparable efficacy to the known NLRP3 inhibitor MCC950. In vivo, A01 treatment significantly reduces peritoneal inflammation in an alum-induced peritonitis model by decreasing IL-1β secretion and suppressing neutrophil and monocyte recruitment. Additionally, A01 improves metabolic outcomes in high-fat diet-fed mice by enhancing glucose tolerance and insulin sensitivity, consistent with the NLRP3 inflammasome's established role in chronic inflammation and metabolic disorders. These findings establish A01 as a potent and selective NLRP3 inhibitor with therapeutic potential for treating NLRP3 inflammasome-related inflammatory and metabolic diseases.

title: Complement and inflammasome crosstalk in chronic inflammation
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the critical mechanistic crosstalk between the complement system and NLRP3 inflammasome signaling in driving chronic inflammation. The complement cascade directly activates the NLRP3 inflammasome through multiple components including C3a, C5a, and the membrane attack complex (MAC), which serve as both priming and activation signals. NLRP3 inflammasome activation triggers caspase-1-dependent processing of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, which subsequently enhance complement synthesis and activation, establishing a potent feed-forward inflammatory loop. C1q plays a context-dependent dual role in modulating inflammasome activation, while complement components simultaneously promote immune cell recruitment, phagocytosis, and tissue repair through classical, alternative, and lectin pathway mechanisms. Therapeutic strategies targeting this pathway crosstalk, including complement inhibitors (eculizumab, avacopan), inflammasome inhibitors (MCC950), and IL-1β blockers (anakinra), demonstrate clinical promise for managing chronic inflammatory conditions driven by complement-inflammasome amplification.

title: Pathogenesis of Adenomyosis: An Integrated Review of Cellular Origins, Molecular Mechanisms, and Intersecting Diseases
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis is driven by a complex interplay of estrogen-dependent mechanisms, epigenetic dysregulation, and inflammatory pathways that collectively cause its hallmark clinical symptoms of dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and infertility. Elevated local estrogen production—resulting from hypomethylation-induced upregulation of steroidogenic enzymes and impaired estrogen inactivation—hyperactivates estrogen receptor-beta, which directly activates the NLRP3 inflammasome and triggers interleukin-1β release, perpetuating a COX-2/prostaglandin E2/aromatase positive feedback loop. This hyperestrogenism drives oxytocin receptor overexpression in the myometrium, causing pathological uterine hyperperistalsis that disrupts embryo implantation and normal contractile physiology. Mechanically, progesterone resistance—stemming from KRAS-driven MAPK signaling and loss of KLF11 transcriptional repression—allows unopposed fibrotic signaling (TGF-β and COL1A1), leading to irreversible myometrial fibrosis and hypertrophy. The adenomyotic tissue is further sustained by elevated prolactin secretion from ectopic glands, which exerts antiapoptotic effects and drives myofibroblast transdifferentiation through paracrine signaling. These integrated pathways converge on epithelial-mesenchymal transition, immune polarization toward fibrosis-promoting macrophages, and angiogenesis, establishing adenomyosis as a multifactorial disorder amenable to targeting estrogen metabolism, inflammasome signaling, and the prolactin-PRLR axis.

title: The Metabolic Calibration of Female Immune Plasticity: From X-Linked Vulnerability to Precision Metabotyping
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates how female immune cells are rendered simultaneously hyperresponsive to pathogens yet susceptible to autoimmunity through X-linked gene dosage effects and metabolic regulation. The X chromosome harbors high densities of immune genes including TLR7 and Foxp3; TLR7 frequently escapes X-inactivation in females, leading to elevated expression that primes antiviral responses but also heightens autoimmune vulnerability when activated by Xist RNA autoantigens through TLR7/TLR9 signaling and Type I interferon production. Regulatory T cells (Tregs), critical for immune homeostasis, depend on stable Foxp3 expression, which is protected through microbial metabolites—particularly butyrate from commensals like Faecalibacterium and Eubacterium rectale—that inhibit histone deacetylases and promote H3K27ac-mediated epigenetic "locking" of the Foxp3 enhancer. Dysbiosis and reduced butyrate production, as seen in systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis patients, destabilizes female Tregs and promotes pathogenic ex-Treg conversion. The authors propose that the "Western diet" and microbiota depletion represent critical environmental modifiers of female immune plasticity, and that precision metabotyping of butyrate-producing capacity could enable personalized therapeutic interventions to reinforce Treg stability in autoimmune-prone females.

title: Steroidogenic Acute Regulatory Protein in Breast Cancer: Mechanistic Insights into Pathogenesis and Therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

This article elucidates how steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) drives estrogen biosynthesis in hormone-sensitive breast cancer through a novel post-translational modification mechanism. StAR facilitates the critical first step of steroid hormone synthesis by transporting cholesterol across the mitochondrial membrane, and StAR acetylation—particularly at lysine residues K111 and K253—enhances its activity and promotes pathological 17β-estradiol (E2) overproduction. Elevated E2 subsequently activates estrogen receptors to drive breast tumorigenesis, and StAR gene amplification correlates with poor survival outcomes in hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. Aromatase inhibitors, the conventional endocrine therapy, suffer from long-term efficacy loss due to endocrine resistance, but histone deacetylase inhibitors offer a promising therapeutic alternative by reducing StAR acetylation and suppressing pathological E2 synthesis. This mechanistic insight into StAR acetylation as a critical driver of estrogen dysregulation provides a rational basis for targeting protein acetylation as a therapeutic strategy in hormone-dependent breast cancer.

title: Advances in antimicrobial peptides: promising cancer treatments and vaccines
summary:
# Summary

Antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) represent a promising therapeutic class with dual anticancer and immunomodulatory properties. These cationic, amphipathic molecules selectively target cancer cells by exploiting their elevated expression of negatively charged phosphatidylserine and O-glycosylated mucins on the cell membrane, enabling electrostatic interactions that create transmembrane channels and disrupt membrane integrity. Once internalized, anticancer peptides employ multiple cytotoxic mechanisms including inhibition of DNA replication and protein synthesis, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cytoskeleton damage, ultimately triggering cancer cell apoptosis and necrosis while simultaneously suppressing tumor angiogenesis. Beyond direct cytotoxicity, AMPs function as potent immunomodulators that induce immunogenic cell death, serving as in situ vaccines that enhance antigen presentation and prime systemic antitumor immunity. The article reviews additional investigational cancer therapeutics employing distinct mechanisms, such as integrin-targeting peptides that block tumor angiogenesis and immunomodulatory compounds that restore immune function. These findings position AMPs and related peptide-based therapies as innovative treatment approaches with selective efficacy toward malignant cells and capacity to simultaneously engage adaptive immune responses.

title: Sensing succinate: SUCNR1 as a context-dependent metabolic and cellular signal integrator
summary:
# Succinate Receptor 1 as a Metabolic and Inflammatory Signal Integrator

Succinate, a central tricarboxylic acid cycle intermediate that accumulates under hypoxia and inflammatory conditions, acts as an endogenous ligand for succinate receptor 1 (SUCNR1), a G-protein-coupled receptor expressed across immune cells, hepatocytes, kidney, and adipose tissue. SUCNR1 activation engages multiple signaling cascades including ERK1/2, protein kinase B, and c-Jun N-terminal kinase, enabling context-dependent regulation of immune polarization and metabolic homeostasis. In pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages, elevated succinate stabilizes HIF-1α and promotes IL-1β secretion, while SUCNR1 enrichment on anti-inflammatory M2 macrophages drives IL-10 and prostaglandin E2 release, with metabolic balance—particularly the α-ketoglutarate-to-succinate ratio—determining inflammatory versus resolution outcomes. Beyond immune regulation, SUCNR1 signaling controls glucose and lipid metabolism in hepatocytes, inhibits lipolysis in white adipose tissue, and regulates blood pressure through renin release in the kidney. Elevated systemic succinate is consistently associated with inflammatory diseases including endometriosis, Crohn's disease, diabetes, and obesity, positioning succinate sensing as a critical integrator linking cellular metabolism to pathological inflammation that may be therapeutically targeted through SUCNR1 modulation.

title: Advancements in nanomedicine for the therapeutic regulation of efferocytosis: opportunities and challenges
summary:
# Summary

Efferocytosis, the process by which phagocytes engulf apoptotic cells, is fundamental to maintaining tissue homeostasis and immune balance by promoting inflammation resolution through release of anti-inflammatory mediators including IL-10, TGF-β, and lipid mediators. Dysregulation or deficiency of efferocytosis leads to accumulation of apoptotic cell debris and drives chronic inflammation, autoimmunity, atherosclerosis, and cancer, implicating this process as a central mechanism in multiple disease pathologies. The efferocytic process is orchestrated through orchestrated recognition signals: apoptotic cells release "find me" signals (ATP, UTP, lysophosphatidylcholine, sphingosine-1-phosphate, and chemokines) that recruit phagocytes via receptors like P2Y2, CX3CR1, and G2A, followed by "eat me" signal recognition (phosphatidylserine) through bridging molecules MFG-E8 and Gas6 that engage integrins and TAM family receptors (Tyro3, Axl, MerTK), while "don't eat me" signals (CD47, CD24) via SIRPα and Siglec-10 engagement provide inhibitory safeguards. Mechanistically, MerTK activation recruits the ELMO1-Dock180 complex to activate Rac1 and Cdc42, driving actin polymerization and phagocytic cup formation, while downstream efferosome maturation through Rab5-to-Rab7 transition and lipophagy-associated phagocytosis (LAP) facilitates cargo digestion and suppresses proinflammatory signaling. Nan

title: Heparanase-1 facilitates bone remodeling on bone defect animal model with optimal dosage and treatment duration
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary: Heparanase-1 facilitates bone defect repair through its ability to cleave heparan sulfate proteoglycans in the extracellular matrix, thereby enhancing angiogenesis and blood flow to promote osteogenesis and bone mass accumulation. However, this study demonstrates a critical dose-dependent relationship in bone regeneration, where low-dose heparanase-1 treatment significantly enhances bone defect remodeling while high-dose administration paradoxically suppresses osteogenic activity. The findings reconcile conflicting reports in the literature by showing that heparanase-1's effects on bone healing are biphasic, requiring optimization of dosage and treatment duration for therapeutic benefit. While heparanase-1 is associated with tumor angiogenesis and metastasis in cancer contexts, its controlled application in bone regeneration highlights the importance of context-specific and dose-dependent mechanisms. These results suggest that low-dose heparanase-1 recombinant protein represents a promising therapeutic strategy for bone defect repair when administered under carefully controlled parameters.
